The closing distinction: Paxton's weakness is character-based, not competence-based; he climbed the ladder doing crimes the way Trump did.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias deploying his standard popularist toolkit for a specific high-leverage race, and it's worth taking seriously even where his diagnosis is debatable. The core argument — that Texas Democrats have to peel off voters who've voted Republican their whole lives, and that doing so requires conspicuous heterodoxy on cultural and energy issues — is correct as electoral math, given Trump's 52% Texas performance in 2020 against his 47% national share. The Beto/Allred WAR-overperformance-but-still-losing examples are well-chosen and underline a real pathology in Democratic post-mortems: candidates who "did everything right" by activist standards still lose by fundamentals, and the political class congratulates them for it.

The "obligation to win" framing is the analytically novel piece. Yglesias is essentially saying that when your opponent is uniquely bad, the failure mode of running a clean-but-losing race is worse than the failure mode of saying unpopular-with-the-base things to win. That's a real ethical claim about what political competition is for, and it cuts against the prevalent Democratic culture where small-donor donors and online progressives reward purity over pragmatism. The Talarico-Pulido-podcast-on-oil-and-gas detail is the most reportable evidence that the campaign understands this, and the Sirota/abundance-bros antagonism in the Platner race (referenced in Yglesias's Saturday piece) is the implicit anti-model.

His treatment of Paxton's corruption is unusually thorough — the Nate Paul affair-job-corruption-scheme details, the multiple-primary-residences mortgage fraud, the impeachment-and-acquittal arc — and it sets up his closing point: Paxton is "weak" only in the sense that his moral character is uniquely terrible, not in the sense that his political instincts or operational competence are weak. The Trump comparison is apt: both are genuinely good at the climbing-the-ladder part of politics even while doing things that would have ended a normal politician's career. That's a more sophisticated read than the Democratic-operative class's tendency to confuse "we should be able to beat this guy" with "we will beat this guy."

The piece's weakness is its implicit assumption that the Joe Rogan and Bobby Pulido moves scale. Talarico's Joe Rogan appearance was load-bearing for his primary win, but Rogan's audience is enormous and unwieldy — Yglesias acknowledges this when he says "the issue genuinely is Rogan" rather than podcasts generically. But there's no obvious second move that replicates the Rogan effect; "do another Rogan-esque taboo-breaking" is a strategy that depends on the existence of comparable validators on the right who aren't simply MAGA. The piece would be stronger if it named specific taboos Talarico should break (immigration enforcement? trans-in-women's-sports?) rather than gesturing at the category. The grassroots-fundraising-trap warning is also a standard Yglesias move that elides a real tension: small donors are how Talarico became viable in the first place, and the Texas race requires money on a scale that out-of-state Democratic megadonors haven't historically committed to. Telling small donors to back off would make the financial math much harder.

The closing line — "important to actually defeat him and not just beat the spread" — is the popularism argument in its most distilled form. It's also a useful frame for thinking about how Democrats should evaluate their candidates more generally: WAR overperformance is meaningful for cumulative party-building, but in any individual race the binary outcome matters more than the margin. Whether Yglesias is right that Texas is winnable in a "good national political environment" against Paxton is a different question — the Silver Bulletin generic ballot for Texas as of mid-2026 is one number worth checking against this claim.

↩ Back to TOC

The structural answer to corporate power: anti-monopoly, tariffs, state AGs, capital's triumph

I'm kind of over the whole "Anti-monopoly" movement

Noah Smith · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Jonathan Chait's May 2026 Atlantic profile of Barry C. Lynn and the neo-Brandeisian anti-monopoly movement ("antitrust theory Barry Lynn"); Matt Stoller's "Abundance of Sleaze" attack on Klein-Thompson; Lina Khan's denial that "market forces" exist; and ongoing factional warfare inside the Democratic Party over the Abundance agenda.

Smith reconstructs his own decade-long arc from 2017-era market-power believer (citing Autor, De Loecker-Eeckhout, Barkai, Azar et al. as a "circumstantial detective case") to 2026 apostate. He keeps the corporate-power concern — heightened, he says, by AI concentration and Trump-era corruption — but argues the actual anti-monopoly movement has gone bad along four axes. First, monomania: Lynn explicitly attributes racism, the radical right, attacks on abortion, and "every ill" to monopoly; Zephyr Teachout in conversation with Ezra Klein refused to name any social problem antitrust couldn't solve. Second, wrong-target selection: Biden's FTC/DOJ went after grocers, airlines, and insurers — all low-margin industries where the causal chain from concentration to harm requires high profits the targets don't have. The Spirit/JetBlue blockage produced 17,000 unemployed workers when Spirit went under entirely. The corporate-landlord crusade ignores that institutional owners are a tiny share of rentals and may actually charge lower rents, distracting from supply-side housing reform. Third, anti-empiricism: Lynn calls economics "Lysenkoism" and Khan denies market forces exist; meanwhile evidence that local labor-market concentration has decreased (Rossi-Hansberg, Rinz, Autor 2023) and that greedflation didn't show up in markups (Alvarez, Leduc, Bouras) gets ignored, and the De Loecker-Eeckhout markup story is itself heavily contested. Fourth, factionalism: the Stoller-Lynn-Prospect axis savaged Klein-Thompson's Abundance as "selling oligarchy" rather than coalescing with allies, and Lynn told Chait dissenting liberals are "those who are paid to do so." Smith frames the movement as an intellectual pseudo-cult that captured the Warren-era Democratic personnel pipeline — comparable in pathology to 1980s supply-siders and MMT — and writes that even strong shared priors aren't enough to hold his nose for it.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Smith doing what he is genuinely good at — taking a position he himself held in print for a decade, and apostatizing publicly with citations. The piece's value is mostly in the apostasy structure: he can't be dismissed as a long-time hater because the receipts of his 2017-2021 enthusiasm are right there in the post. The four-pronged critique (monomania / wrong targets / anti-empiricism / factionalism) is also analytically clean, and the receipts for each prong are real — Lynn's "every ill" quote is in his own book, Khan's "no such things as market forces" is in print, the Spirit collapse happened, and the Stoller-on-Abundance attack reads exactly the way Smith characterizes it.

But the piece's central move depends on Chait's Atlantic profile of Lynn doing a lot of load-bearing work, and Smith largely outsources his critique of the movement's intellectual leadership to that profile. The Klein-Teachout exchange Smith quotes (Teachout retracting her own concession that antitrust can't solve racism) appears in Chait, and Smith concedes Chait "may have cherry-picked" it — but then proceeds on the basis that the quote is representative anyway. A stronger version of the piece would have done its own audit of the movement's recent intellectual output rather than treating Chait's selection as definitive. There's also a category-confusion: Lynn (a journalist who runs the Open Markets Institute), Khan (a lawyer trained at Yale doing FTC enforcement), Stoller (a polemicist), and Teachout (a Fordham law professor) are doing very different intellectual work, and the Warren/Klobuchar/AOC senators who actually shape policy outcomes are different again. Smith folds them all into one "movement" and indicts each member with the others' worst statements. That's the kind of collective-indictment move he correctly criticizes the anti-monopoly people for using against the Abundance crowd.

The strongest substantive argument is the low-margin-targets one. The point that the causal chain from concentration to consumer harm runs through profit, and that grocers and insurers operate at near-zero margins, is a real test the neo-Brandeisians have not satisfactorily answered. The Kroger-Albertsons blockage and the Spirit-JetBlue blockage are both cases where the movement's framework predicts harms its critics didn't see, and the outcomes (Spirit going under entirely) genuinely embarrass the framework. The corporate-landlord case is similar: institutional ownership is a small share of single-family rentals, and Coven's paper finds corporate landlords charge lower rents, which the movement has never engaged. Smith's strongest contribution here is forcing the question: if your theory's targets keep being low-margin or marginal-share, your theory has a target-selection problem.

Where Smith is weakest is on what to do about corporate power instead. He concedes corporate power is real and getting worse (AI concentration, Trump-era corruption), notes monopsony probably matters in some local labor markets, and grants there are industries where stronger antitrust is needed — but doesn't propose a sorting mechanism for which ones, beyond gesturing at "more reasonable programs." The position is closer to "the Khan-Lynn-Stoller faction is wrong about most cases" than "here's the framework that would correctly identify the high-value targets." A reader sympathetic to the basic concern is left with a critique of one approach and no replacement. Smith might respond that the Abundance agenda is the replacement — but Abundance's supply-side housing/permitting agenda doesn't actually address corporate concentration in pharma, healthcare, defense procurement, or AI compute. The unspoken alternative may be "trust expert antitrust enforcement under sober Obama-era leadership," which has its own failure record (consider the FAANG-era under-enforcement Smith himself was complaining about a decade ago).

One genuinely useful contribution: the Robert Solow quip ("everything reminds Milton of the money supply; everything reminds me of sex, but I keep it out of the paper") as a frame for movement monomania is sharp and portable. So is the closing "intellectual pseudo-cults" frame — the parallel between supply-siders, MMT, and the anti-monopoly movement as charismatic gangs that exploit policymaker hunger for decisiveness is a useful piece of political-economy taxonomy, even if you don't accept Smith's specific verdict on the third member.

↩ Back to TOC

A Billionaire Explains Why American Business Now Feels like the Mafia

Matt Stoller · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: John Malone's autobiography "Born to Be Wired" (Simon & Schuster, late 2024/2025), released to coincide with his CNBC press tour as the Paramount-Warner merger heated up — Malone sits on Warner's board.

Stoller frames the book review as a generational excavation: the cable-and-media barons of the 1970s-90s (Malone, Rupert Murdoch, Barry Diller, Craig McCaw, Sumner Redstone, Ted Turner) are the missing link between New Deal-regulated capitalism and the contemporary tech oligarchy of Bezos, Zuckerberg, and Musk. He opens with a 1981 documented threat by Malone's TCI lieutenant Paul Alden against a Jefferson City, Missouri cable consultant ("We know where you live… we are going to destroy you") — a threat Malone airbrushed from the autobiography while preserving the moments that flatter him. Stoller traces Malone's career: Bell Labs to McKinsey to Jerrold Electronics (where he pioneered offshoring), then TCI (where he pioneered the roll-up — 482 acquisitions 1973-89 — and popularized EBITDA as a tax-avoidance metric), then Liberty Media. The Reagan-era Cable Communications Policy Act of 1984 deregulated pricing; the Cable Act of 1992 partially re-regulated after Senator Al Gore called Malone "King of the Cable Cosa Nostra" at a 1989 hearing (Stoller watched the original tape; Malone's book misrepresents the exchange). Stoller's central interpretive claim is that Malone is not just greedy but a religious believer in efficiency as moral achievement — "Money is simply the scorecard for moral achievement" — and that his self-diagnosed Asperger's serves in his telling as a libertarian superpower rather than an interpersonal limit. The piece ends by tying Malone to the current Paramount-Warner merger fight (against Larry Ellison) and noting his belated, internally-incoherent embrace of antitrust against Big Tech, who "never were subject to the kind of regulation he had to face."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the strongest piece in Stoller's recent run because the book-review form lets him do something he rarely does — sustained primary-source close reading of an oligarch in his own words, cross-referenced against the documentary record. The 1989 Gore hearing video is the load-bearing move: Malone's autobiography describes himself as a painfully shy man humiliated by grandstanding senators, and Stoller's reply is to go watch the original hearing tape and report that what humiliated Malone was being caught lying about whether his stock had appreciated (Senator Fritz Hollings's question about TCI's share price). That is the kind of analytical move the project most prizes — a check on a source that survives independent verification — and it elevates the piece from generic anti-monopoly framing to genuine primary-document journalism.

The most analytically generative move is the framing that Malone & co. are the "bridge" generation between New Deal regulated capitalism and the contemporary tech-oligarch era. This is a real structural claim about how the deregulatory shift was produced — not by economists or judges (Bork) alone, but by the businessmen who used Bork's framework to construct contracts, roll-ups, and tax-avoidance vehicles that made the new regime operational. The provocation: "Malone walked, so Zuckerberg could run." That's a sharper claim than the standard Stoller line ("monopolies bad") because it identifies a specific institutional vector by which the cultural and economic norms of mob-style coercion were transmitted to the next generation of American capitalists. Worth carrying into any synthesis section that touches the oligarchy/billionaire pattern.

The piece is not without weaknesses. Stoller's "religious faith in efficiency" framing leans on a single autobiography quote ("$1 investment in 1973 became $3950 by 2024 — Not bad, eh Bob?") and a few cliched lines from Malone's own prose. It's evocative — Malone's apparent inability to understand his children alongside his love for financial metrics is a real observation — but it's also a one-document inference, and Malone's actual psychology may be more banal (number-go-up as basic capitalist motivation, dressed in autobiographical self-importance) than Stoller's "zealot" framing requires. The piece's strongest claim is structural-historical (the bridge generation, the roll-up playbook, the EBITDA-as-tax-shield innovation); the psychological-interpretive layer ("he is a brilliant and sparkling authoritarian") is more decorative and would not survive much pushback from a Malone defender.

There's also a moment of Stoller doing the thing he criticizes — when he describes Malone's "Asperger's" as enabling "financial authoritarianism," he's diagnosing across distance from a self-described autistic person's own autobiography. The autobiographical claim ("My autism has gifted me with the ability to hyper-focus… I could recall verbatim entire sections of books") is Malone's, but Stoller's mobilization of it ("the man shaping the American media experience is a finance-obsessed guy with Asperger's") flirts with the genre move of treating a neuropsychiatric self-diagnosis as causal evidence for moral character. A Stoller defender would say the link is Malone's own, not Stoller's, and the piece is reporting Malone's self-presentation. That's correct but partial; the texture of the passage reads as character-assessment-by-diagnosis, and the piece would be tighter if the autism passage were attributed and left to stand on Malone's own framing rather than rhetorically annexed.

What the piece adds to this week's roster: a genuinely-reported, primary-sourced excavation of a single oligarch as a case study for the broader pattern the project tracks. The Paramount-Warner merger angle is current-news; the Malone-as-bridge-generation framing is the load-bearing analytical contribution. Pairs cleanly with Klippenstein's executive-security piece (the same culture of insulating elites from normal life that produced Brian Thompson's assassin) and with Stoller's own monopoly-roundup AI-repricing argument (the same below-cost-to-acquire-market-power playbook Malone pioneered at TCI is now running at Anthropic-OpenAI scale). The Iron Law of Institutions-style framing — that an entrenched class of business leaders has internalized a particular morality of capital that downstream finance and policy now reproduce automatically — is the most analytically usable claim.

↩ Back to TOC

Monopoly Round-Up: After SpaceX Goes Public, Does the Stock Market Finally Fall?

Matt Stoller · 2026-06-01 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: SpaceX's announced public-listing plans (Q2 2026); the Financial Times' "Enshittification of the Stock Market" framing; Anthropic's announced $45B annualized revenue rate (Series H announcement, May 2026); Joe Weisenthal's Odd Lots theory that Big Tech's 30% economy-wide profit share is driving the AI buildout; Michael Burry's recent thread on Grok/Nvidia round-trip financing.

Stoller's monopoly round-up frames two coupled phenomena. First, the SpaceX IPO: Musk is bringing SpaceX public at a $1.8 trillion valuation with $18.7B revenue, a $4.7B loss, and 15% growth. The structure is suspicious — only 5% of shares will float, 30% of the float will go to retail investors (vs. 10% normal), Musk has supermajority governance control, and NASDAQ has changed its rules so SpaceX can enter the NASDAQ-100 index after just 15 days of trading rather than three months. The insider class (Google, Andreessen, Thiel, Arab sovereign wealth funds) will be able to dump shares onto retirement accounts via index funds. Stoller notes Michael Burry's thread tracing a complicated financial structure where SpaceX's biggest revenue driver is Nvidia chips rented to Anthropic — round-trip financing he likens to Enron's special-purpose vehicles. Second, the AI-repricing story: Anthropic and OpenAI scaled subsidized below-cost pricing to lock in corporate customers; token-based billing started Q1 2026; Microsoft and Uber are now capping employee token use; one Axios-cited firm spent half a billion in a single month before instituting limits. Stoller's macro frame is that AI was a "weird pro-monopoly industrial policy" — cross-subsidized by search/advertising/cloud monopolies plus Wall Street lending — and that as pricing right-sizes, the trillions in data-center capex will need real end-user value to justify, which Chinese open-source models suggest may not exist at the scale assumed. The closing analogy: AI is selling picks and shovels into a gold rush where it's unclear whether there's gold. Note: piece is partially paywalled at the round-up's main news roundup section ("after the paywall"); the substantive AI/SpaceX analysis above the cut is intact.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Stoller in his strongest analytical mode — the macro-financial mode that pairs concrete reporting (NASDAQ rule changes, Anthropic revenue numbers, Burry's round-trip-financing thread) with structural argument about how regulatory choices produce specific market outcomes. The piece's most analytically generative move is the "AI as pro-monopoly industrial policy" framing, which reframes the entire 2022-2026 AI boom not as natural market evolution but as a cross-subsidized investment program in which a specific compute-heavy, closed-source, vertically-integrated stack was selected by policy (Trump's $500B Stargate, federal procurement, regulatory laxity) over alternatives (Chinese open-source, cheaper inference, distributed compute). This is the rare framing that names why the specific industrial structure exists rather than treating it as exogenous, and the synthesis composer should treat it as the load-bearing argument.

The SpaceX-as-market-rigging analysis is tighter than usual for Stoller because the specific mechanisms are documented and falsifiable: the NASDAQ rule change (15-day-trading and 10%-public-float requirements both relaxed) is a real, citable institutional fact, and the 30%-retail-allocation vs. 10%-normal-IPO comparison is specific. The piece could be stronger if it engaged Wall Street's actual counter — that SpaceX's Starlink + launch monopoly are genuinely valuable assets and that the IPO is exotic only because the company is exotic — but Stoller anticipates this with the company/stock distinction ("SpaceX the company represents Musk the engineer; SpaceX the stock represents Musk the financial huckster"), which is a defensible analytical split even if some readers will think it too neat.

The weakest seam is the rhetorical move from "AI is commodified picks-and-shovels" to "the collapse will be stunning and quite scary." Stoller has reasoning for the first half (Chinese open-source nearly matches frontier; token pricing reveals limited end-user willingness-to-pay; Anthropic's $45B doesn't justify trillions in data-center capex) but the second half (catastrophic collapse) is asserted rather than mechanistically argued. The strongest version of the opposing view: token-pricing right-sizing is exactly what a healthy market should do, the buyer-side caps Stoller cites are evidence that price discovery is working as intended, and the productive AI applications (the WSJ-cited "AI Math Solves Erdős Problem" piece Stoller links approvingly) are the actual value, not the speculative excess. A Goldman/Morgan Stanley-style read would say there's a 15-20% correction coming on individual stocks but not a systemic collapse; Stoller cites those exact analysts but treats them as Pollyannas without engaging the substance. He may be right; the piece does not show its work on the catastrophic-tail framing.

The Burry-on-Grok-Nvidia-round-tripping thread is gestured at ("a complicated financial structure involving Grok taking part in a scheme to keep Nvidia's AI chip sales numbers off-the-books, similar to Enron") but not detailed in the piece itself; readers have to go to the linked X thread. Given that this is the single most analytically explosive claim — that the AI revenue boom contains Enron-style accounting concealment — Stoller leaves it too thinly sourced. For a synthesis composer downstream, the appropriate treatment is "Stoller cites Burry as flagging round-trip financing between SpaceX/Grok/Nvidia, which if accurate would represent an Enron-comparable structural fraud; Stoller does not himself audit the claim."

What the analysis adds to the roster: a primary-financial-mechanism argument about how the AI boom's economics actually work, which pairs cleanly with deBoer's micro-finding (LLM social deference, modest utility for real workflow) and his this-week's-novel-composition piece on "LLMs were mostly useless." Where deBoer's argument is "the technology is overhyped at the user level," Stoller's argument is "the technology may or may not be overhyped, but the financial structure built around it is over-leveraged regardless." That's the version of the AI skepticism that a synthesis composer should foreground — not as a competing claim with deBoer's but as a complementary one operating at a different scale.

↩ Back to TOC

The Feds Don't Have an Antitrust Monopoly

Gail Slater · 2026-05-31 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The state attorneys general' win over LiveNation (settlement following bipartisan-coalition lawsuit, April 2026) and the multi-state suit against the NexStar-Tegna broadcast merger (filed March 2026, pending in the Northern District of California).

Slater — Assistant Attorney General for Antitrust in the Trump-2 DOJ — argues that state-level antitrust enforcement is "a feature and not a bug" of American antitrust law, and that "federalism in antitrust is having a moment" as state AGs step in where the federal government withdraws. She traces the historical case: the Sherman and Clayton Acts do not preempt state enforcement; many state antitrust statutes predated the federal laws (the Grangers' 1873 "Farmer's Declaration of Independence" against railroad and grain-elevator monopolies anticipated Sherman's 1890 act, which Senator John Sherman framed as an extension of the Founders' rejection of tyranny). Texas, Kansas, Missouri, and Michigan all passed antitrust laws in the 1880s; the Texas constitution declared monopolies "contrary to the genius of a free government." Slater highlights two recent state-driven cases: LiveNation/Ticketmaster (settled after a bipartisan multistate coalition pressed forward) and NexStar/Tegna (filed by multiple state AGs after federal withdrawal). She also notes that in the Google search-monopolization case, state AGs pushed more aggressive theories of harm (platform dominance, vertical integration) than the DOJ did. Slater's closing claim: competitive markets enable Americans to flourish "not just as consumers and producers, but as citizens" — placing the antitrust argument inside the Compass/Bessent "citizenship as the goal of policy" frame.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is unusual placement: the DOJ Antitrust chief writing in Commonplace (American Compass's magazine) on why state AGs should be aggressive antitrust enforcers — including, by clear implication, on cases the DOJ has declined to bring or has dropped (LiveNation and NexStar are both cases where Slater says "the United States decided to withdraw from the enforcement playing field"). Read at surface level, this looks like an institutional-decorum piece celebrating federalism. Read for what it does, it's an explicit invitation to state AGs to do enforcement work the Trump-2 DOJ has decided not to do. That's a politically interesting move: rather than describe the federal withdrawal as a strategic choice, Slater frames it as creating space for states to act, with the implicit endorsement that they should. The Texas-AG-as-antitrust-hawk parenthetical naming Ken Paxton, the Bessent quote on the American Dream, and the closing citizenship framing all signal which state AGs Slater wants to see most active.

The historical case is mostly accurate but selective. The Grangers and the railroad-trust origins of antitrust are real; the state-precedence of antitrust law is real. What Slater elides is that the post-1890 trajectory of antitrust was centralizing — the Sherman Act was passed in part because state-by-state enforcement against interstate trusts was failing, and the FTC was created in 1914 precisely because state-level enforcement could not keep up with industrial concentration crossing jurisdictions. Returning to state-led enforcement after federal retreat reproduces the structural problem 19th-century reformers were trying to solve: multistate-coalitional enforcement is heroic and rare, but routine concentrated power escaping state-by-state enforcement is what made federal antitrust necessary in the first place. The LiveNation example illustrates this: it took a bipartisan multistate coalition to bring the case, which Slater frames as state-system strength but which is also a demonstration of how high the bar is when the federal government is absent.

The strongest argument-by-omission here is what Slater doesn't address: the Trump-2 DOJ's strategic-leniency moves on tech and media mergers (Paramount-Skydance, the FTC's softened tech posture). If state federalism is the answer, the question of what the federal government's enforcement priorities should be is being substituted away. The piece reads cleanest as a public-record statement that the Trump-2 DOJ is comfortable having state AGs (predominantly Republican-state AGs, in practice) carry the enforcement load on the cases the administration is choosing not to fight — which is a substantive position about what federal antitrust is for, dressed in the language of historical continuity. The Bruenig-style critique that state-level enforcement is structurally weaker against interstate concentrated power applies here whether or not Slater concedes it.

↩ Back to TOC

Tariffs on Forced Labor Are Free-Market

Oren Cass · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: USTR's June 2, 2026 Section 301 determination imposing 10–12.5% tariffs on 60 countries (including China, Japan, India) for failing to bar forced-labor goods; press treatment of it as a pretext for the administration's post-IEEPA tariff search.

Cass (writing as the Commonplace newsletter editor, with Daniel-bylined lead essay) argues the forced-labor tariff action is a genuine policy continuity, not a workaround. He grounds it in a chain from the 1890 Tariff Act (McKinley's ban on convict-made imports), through the 1930 Tariff Act's Section 307, to the 1974 Trade Act's Section 301 — the same statute Trump-1 used against China and that survived years of litigation precisely because it carries built-in procedural requirements (investigation, hearings, on-the-record determination). He argues this matters legally because Section 301's procedural skeleton is what gives the new tariffs durability after the Supreme Court struck down the IEEPA-based tariffs in February 2026. The economic argument is that forced labor is the most extreme form of capital seeking workers it can pay poorly and productively, and that excluding it sets a floor below which competition shouldn't occur — a standard the AFL-CIO, USW, and UAW endorsed when the investigation opened. Cass extends the frame: the nine Agreements on Reciprocal Trade USTR has finalized embed the same labor article (forced-labor bans, ILO rights, export-processing-zone coverage); USMCA's Rapid Response Mechanism has nearly doubled Mexican manufacturing wages since 2020 from $2.30 to $4.20/hr. Surrounding sections then aggregate: Tabarrok-style markets-adapt evidence from the Strait of Hormuz, the Fifth Circuit's reversal on Texas app-store age verification, the manufacturing-jobs uptick masked by FT framing, and the EU's slow learning curve on Chinese trans-shipment through Morocco.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The strongest part of Cass's case is the legal-genealogy move. The IEEPA tariffs got struck down because the statute authorizing presidential "regulation" of imports doesn't authorize tariffs; Section 301 works because it specifies a process (investigation, consultation, on-the-record determination) that courts can check. That's not pretext — the administration is using Section 301 precisely because Section 301 has been pre-cleared by years of litigation in Trump-1, and pretextual use of it would invite the same scrutiny. The 1890/1930/1974 chain genuinely is in the statute books; the AFL-CIO/USW/UAW endorsement when the investigation opened is real; the nine ART agreements with embedded labor articles are public. Where the case gets thinner is on the empirical claim that the forced-labor concern is doing the work. A 12.5% tariff that covers China, Japan, and India is a near-universal tariff; the granularity isn't between "forced labor exporters" and "non-forced-labor exporters" but between "made a commitment" and "didn't" — which is closer to a binary signaling rule than to a calibrated response to documented forced-labor practices. The USMCA Rapid Response Mechanism data Cass leans on (Mexican manufacturing wages doubling) is the strongest empirical anchor, but it's also the case farthest from the new Section 301 action and closest to an institutional mechanism with multiple administrations (Biden + Trump 2) operationalizing it — which makes the Mexico-wages number an argument for bilateral enforcement mechanisms, not for unilateral broad tariffs.

The Cass-coalition's framing of the May jobs report is worth flagging. The piece concedes labor-market arithmetic doesn't add up — 500,000 jobs in three months without immigration, native-born prime-age growth, or LFPR increase — and suggests "a dropoff in immigration enforcement is a driver here." This is an unusually direct admission against interest from an outlet aligned with the administration's restrictionist program: either the enforcement is slipping or the jobs numbers are noisier than the surrounding sections claim. The piece doesn't quite resolve the tension; the rest of the newsletter takes the manufacturing-jobs reversal as evidence the industrial-base story is real. Both can be true, but they pull on the same labor-supply question, and Cass doesn't carry the argument all the way through.

The newsletter's surrounding-aggregation function — Strait of Hormuz adapting, EU Confederation analogy, Stephen Curry on Li-Ning — does what American Compass content typically does: it builds a "post-liberal economic nationalism" frame by stitching together apparently disparate stories under one diagnostic. That's legitimate columnist work, but it's also where the project's blind spots show. The Curry/Kerr passage, framing celebrity China-licensing as a "cultural problem with a policy solution" (cultural export controls), reads as the kind of policy proposal that emerges from a coalition that is structurally less constrained by counter-veto. It would target individual athletes' commercial speech in ways the First Amendment would in fact contest — but the analysis-side absence of that constraint is what gives the Compass-aligned program its appearance of coherence in newsletter format.

↩ Back to TOC

The triumph of capital

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-02 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: recurring left-economist arguments (he cites no specific names but the target is the ProPublica/Saez-Zucman school) that try to redefine unrealized capital-gains appreciation as income in order to claim billionaires pay low effective tax rates. Yglesias rejects the redefinition — asset appreciation isn't income — but argues conservatives are equally wrong to dismiss the underlying frustration. The real difference between Nordic and U.S. tax systems isn't top statutory rates (Nordic top rates are similar) but where they kick in: Norway's at 180% of average income, Sweden's at 110%, vs. America's at 880%. Asking middle-class Americans to pay Nordic levels is politically impossible, so progressives keep trying to reframe billionaire taxation as a substitute. The deeper structural issue is that from 1996-2012, stock prices, house prices, median household income, and average hourly wages all ended up in similar places (despite stock-market volatility); but since 2012, stocks have soared, housing has risen faster than wages, and wages have lagged badly. A hypothetical heir who inherited $1M in S&P 500 index in early 2012 would now have ~$6M plus ~$40K/yr in dividends; that's the divergent fate of a non-heir with a similar white-collar job. The Walton family's net worth has compounded from $21B (Jim Walton, 2011) to $144B today, with newer-generation heirs like Lukas Walton gaining $2.6B this year doing essentially nothing. Yglesias concludes: Piketty's aristocracy-of-inherited-wealth concern partly happened (the Waltons are now enormous) but was outstripped by an unanticipated explosion of new-founder fortunes (Musk, Bezos, Zuckerberg, Page, Brin, Ellison, Dell, Huang, Ballmer). The U.S. economy remains open to talented founders, but the best route to affluence for a normie non-founder is to start with money and let compounding work.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias doing the rare thing of taking a left-economist diagnostic claim seriously even while rejecting the proposed remedy. The chart of stock prices vs. wages vs. house prices vs. median household income post-2012 is the load-bearing visual, and his honesty about its implications is what makes the piece work. He's not denying the divergence — he's pointing out that it's real, structurally consequential for political vibes, and not easily fixed by any of the policy levers currently on the table. The Norway/Sweden tax-threshold comparison (top rates kick in at ~1.1-1.8x average income vs. ~8.8x in the U.S.) is the technically correct way to explain why Nordic tax revenues are higher despite similar top rates — and it implicitly admits that progressive U.S. politicians who reject middle-class tax increases have boxed themselves into trying to extract Nordic-level revenue from a tax base that's a small fraction of the Nordic one.

The Piketty critique is the piece's most interesting analytic contribution. Yglesias is granting that the inherited-wealth dynamic is real and intensifying (the Waltons are a genuine multi-generational dynasty now valued at hundreds of billions) but observing that Piketty failed to anticipate the rise of even-bigger new-founder fortunes that distort the headline rankings. This is fair to Piketty in a way that most American Piketty critics aren't — he's saying the substance of the worry was correct, just that the foreground got obscured by a parallel phenomenon (tech-platform monopolies generating individual fortunes of unprecedented scale). The "awfully French in its way to just kind of assume there wouldn't be huge new fortunes" line is a cheap shot but accurate as a historical-sociological observation about what European intellectuals overlook in American capitalism.

What's notable about the piece is what Yglesias doesn't propose. He's identified the divergence, named the political consequences ("look at that chart, because it's kind of crazy" — i.e., this is why people are mad), and acknowledged that the standard liberal-tax-policy fix is mostly unavailable for political reasons. But he doesn't pivot to "and therefore we need a wealth tax" or "and therefore we need a major CTC expansion" or any of the usual progressive moves. The piece ends with a flat statement that all this is "going great" in some macroeconomic sense (rising capital stock is good) while creating a widening wedge that the political system has no tools to address. This is unusually structural-pessimistic for Yglesias, who normally has a popularist policy fix ready. It pairs neatly with his Friday mailbag answer endorsing the Gallego "buy-borrow-die" loan-as-income bill while noting it raises only ~$100B over 10 years — modest relative to need.

The piece is a useful counter-data-point for the "Yglesias is just a defender of the status quo" caricature. He's saying: the people who are mad about wealth concentration are not crazy, the divergence is real and intensifying, and the right's "the rich are paying their share" defense is willful blindness about how vibes work. He's just also unwilling to pretend that any of the available tax-policy levers solve the problem. That's an honest middle ground — even if it leaves the reader with no clear policy ask.

↩ Back to TOC

Why Europe should put up trade barriers against Chinese goods

Noah Smith · 2026-06-06 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Tej Parikh's Financial Times column arguing China "has a comparative advantage in industrial policy" and Europe should cede manufacturing and specialize in services; the OECD's new finding (via Paul Hannon in the WSJ) that subsidies explain ~60% of Chinese market-share gains; and the Rhodium Group's "industrial policy of everything" report.

Smith, normally ambivalent about trade barriers, argues Europe is a special case: China's subsidy-driven "industrial policy of everything" is generating unbalanced trade (goods for IOUs) rather than the goods-for-goods exchange that comparative-advantage theory assumes, and the strategic stakes are unusually high. He attacks Parikh's "comparative advantage in industrial policy" framing as a category error — industrial policy is a production input, not a traded good, and even an absolutely-better producer cannot have comparative advantage in everything. The operative argument is military: if Russia invades Europe, Europe will need mass drone production, and the drone supply chain (radio modules, lithium-ion batteries, electric motors, navigation cameras, carbon frames, rare earths) currently runs through China, which is already arming and funding Russia's war on Ukraine. A Chinese embargo at the moment of invasion would be game-over. Secondarily, Smith argues European trade barriers might help ordinary Chinese people by forcing Xi to redirect industrial policy from export-led national-greatness mercantilism back toward domestic consumption — Chinese auto consumption is below a decade ago, EV makers like BYD are in a ruinous price war, and the solar industry is bankrupting itself. The piece reads Germany's "let China make everything, we'll do services" reflex as 2002 thinking that ignores both supply-chain vulnerability and China's own innovation industrial policy.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Smith is doing his characteristic move: deploying mainstream economics (here, the actual textbook theory of comparative advantage) to discipline a fellow center-left commentator (Parikh at the FT) who is wielding the term loosely as a free-trade trump card. The pedantic detour on Parikh's category error matters because the looseness is doing real work in European policy discourse — "China has comparative advantage in X, therefore Europe shouldn't try to compete" recurs across the FT and Economist as a way to launder political-economy capitulation as economic literacy. Smith is right that comparative advantage is about relative costs of traded goods, not absolute production efficiency, and right that the existence of huge persistent Chinese surpluses (goods for IOUs) is incompatible with the equilibrium the theory describes. That's a clean correction.

The military argument is the load-bearing one, and it is unusually concrete for a Smith piece. The drone-supply-chain claim has been documented (CSIS, MWI) and the China-Russia coordination evidence has accumulated over 2024-2026 — battlefield intelligence sharing, Russian-soldier training, defense-component supply. The strongest pushback would not deny these facts but ask whether the implied counterfactual works: can Europe actually stand up upstream drone-component industries (battery cells, rare-earth processing, RF modules) on a timeline compatible with the threat? Smith doesn't engage this. Industrial-policy-from-scratch in Europe has a poor track record (compare the gap between the European Chips Act's ambitions and its 2026 reality), and the same political dysfunctions that have left Europe dependent on Chinese components in the first place don't disappear because tariffs go up. Tariffs are necessary but not sufficient; without paired subsidy + procurement + permitting reform, they just raise prices on inputs Europe still has to import.

The "trade barriers will nudge China toward domestic-consumption-led growth" argument is weaker. The mechanism Smith proposes — Xi recalculates and rebalances because exports get harder — assumes Xi treats Chinese household welfare as a substitutable goal with national-greatness mercantilism. The Chinese-people-on-a-treadmill data Smith cites is genuine, but the political-economy reading that this represents a suboptimal equilibrium Xi would happily exit if the export channel closes is contestable. An alternative reading: the suppression of household consumption (financial repression of savers, hukou system, weak social safety net, real-estate-as-savings-vehicle) is not an unfortunate side effect of the export strategy but a structural feature of a regime that prefers a controlled, savings-rich, dependent population to a consumer-empowered one. On that reading, European trade barriers might just produce more capacity dumping into the Global South, or accelerate state-led financialization, rather than triggering the Deng-Jiang-Hu-era consumption pivot Smith hopes for.

The piece also leans hard on the assumption that a Russian invasion of the EU is sufficiently probable to anchor industrial policy around. That's a defensible premise given the Baltics rhetoric, but it does ~all the moral work in the argument — strip it out and the trade-barrier case becomes much weaker. Smith is essentially conceding that the policy is bad on conventional welfare grounds and justified only by tail-risk military scenarios. That's an honest framing, but it's also the framing that makes the policy harder to sustain politically once the immediate threat recedes, which is a known historical pattern with security-justified trade barriers (cf. semiconductor controls drifting into general industrial policy).

↩ Back to TOC

AI policy at the Pope Leo XIV inflection

There's Something Else We Should Be Worrying About

Ezra Klein · 2026-05-31 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Pope Leo XIV's first encyclical (Crosscurrents 5/30) and the broader anti-A.I. mood on the left, including data-center bans and the discourse treating any A.I. use as borderline immoral.

Klein concedes the standard A.I. fears (job loss, surveillance, bioweapons, concentration of power, runaway systems) are real and worth policy attention, but argues the discourse has crowded out a second, "strangely absent" question: what public goods could A.I. deliver, and what would it take to make that happen? His core analytic move is that A.I.'s benefits are not automatic — like electricity or I.T. before it, A.I. requires deliberate work to be useful, and the AlphaFold breakthrough only happened because the National Science Foundation had funded the Protein Data Bank since the 1970s. From there he sketches a public agenda in three planes: (1) access — a public-option frontier model under direct public control, plus subsidized compute for universities and public agencies, to head off a private-public divide where Goldman Sachs can run massive jobs and public universities cannot; (2) directing private capacity at public problems via Operation Warp Speed-style outcome guarantees aimed at rare diseases, drug repurposing, and battery materials; (3) citizen-government interfaces, including an A.I. tax assistant grounded in I.R.S. data (he cites Trump's killing of Direct File as the contrast case) and an L.L.M. "digital concierge" to government services. Underneath all three, he argues, lies a data infrastructure problem: cleaning up government data sets (he flags Alberta's experiment as proof of concept) and funding new public data banks on the Protein Data Bank model. He closes with a rhetorical inversion of the discourse: "We know what we fear A.I. will do to us. But what do we hope it will do for us?"

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Klein in abundance-liberal mode rather than Madoff-frame mode — he is laying out a positive program rather than naming an institutional pathology. The piece sits squarely in his "concrete-state-capacity-for-public-purpose" beat and is essentially a friendly amendment to the Pope Leo encyclical (Crosscurrents 5/30), accepting the moral diagnosis but refusing the implication that the right response is retreat. The argument's most useful contribution is the AlphaFold/Protein Data Bank example: it does real explanatory work, showing that the most celebrated A.I. science wins of the past five years ride on a fifty-year-old NSF data infrastructure investment, which means the U.S. has already done this kind of thing successfully and could do it again deliberately. The Warp Speed analogy is also doing more than rhetorical work — it names an actually-implemented mechanism (advance market commitments + outcome guarantees) and asks why we wouldn't use it for, say, rare-disease drug discovery where private incentives are weakest.

That said, the piece's structural weakness is that it is "only the barest sketch toward an agenda" (Klein's own admission) and the sketch papers over the real political-economic obstacles. The public-option frontier model proposal in particular ducks the question of who. NSF was a Cold War-funded basic-research agency in a moment when both parties supported large research budgets; the current Trump administration has just killed Direct File (which Klein himself cites as the contrast case), is cutting NSF funding, and has shown no interest in standing up a public-purpose A.I. lab. The piece does not name the political coalition that would build such a model, nor the institutional home (DOE national labs? DARPA? a new agency?), nor the tens of billions of dollars in capex required. Without that scaffolding, the "public option for A.I." reads more as conceptual provocation than implementable proposal.

A second analytical gap: Klein's framing treats "the public" as a unitary actor whose interests can be defined and pursued, but his own examples (rare diseases, battery materials, tax assistance) suggest the real political work is aggregating across narrow constituencies — rare-disease patients, lithium-supply-chain firms, tax filers — none of whom have the lobbying weight of corporate A.I. customers. The Operation Warp Speed comparison is illuminating here too, but inversely: Warp Speed worked because COVID was a single legible emergency with overwhelming public salience. There is no equivalent forcing function for, say, cleaning up government data sets.

Finally, the piece's most interesting move is the implicit one. Klein is positioning himself against both the left A.I.-skeptic consensus (the Pope, the data-center bans, the "borderline immoral" framing) and against the Silicon Valley triumphalist position. The argument that A.I. requires deliberate public effort to produce public benefits — rather than emerging automatically from market deployment — is a quiet rebuke to the e/acc framing as much as to the degrowth framing. That's a coherent and worth-defending position; it would be a stronger piece if he stated it explicitly rather than letting readers infer it.

↩ Back to TOC

When Is It Wrong to Use A.I.?

Ross Douthat · 2026-06-06 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Pope Leo XIV's manifesto-style encyclical on artificial intelligence (Crosscurrents 5/30) and the more radical responses to it — Princeton's Greg Conti in Compact ("Must we have such an age declared already?"), Anton Barba-Kay in The Hedgehog Review (the "cocaine with a pinch of salt" line), and Tyler Austin Harper in The Atlantic on why secular AI skeptics are reaching for religious language.

Douthat shares the radical AI skeptics' frustration that the pope didn't go far enough — he thought Leo could have explored "the sheer strangeness" of AI, its challenge to human exceptionalism, and its tendency to breed messianic and apocalyptic responses — but argues a papal call for massive resistance would have been miscalibrated to the actual conditions of 2026. The "too late" half: the technology has already produced too much wealth, infrastructure, and institutional commitment for an extirpation strategy. The "too early" half: humans regulate technology in response to manifest harms (industrialization, Hiroshima/Nagasaki, the post-2020 smartphone backlash), not hypothetical ones, so the discourse moralizing AI before its central catastrophe is visible cannot mobilize a political coalition. He layers a brief Christian theological move on top — that God will allow the time needed for regulation unless this is the eschatological "last temptation," in which case nothing matters — and then turns the column to the operative recommendation: critics should moralize specifically. Don't lament higher education; say that students who use AI to cheat are doing something gravely wrong. Don't bemoan Claude-inflected prose; say that a novelist or essayist outsourcing a chapter to AI has committed what should be a career-ending literary crime. Don't fret about "misleading" chatbot intimacy; tell Christians that treating an AI as girlfriend or boyfriend is a sin. The structural argument: the goal isn't to prevent the disruption now but to "lay the foundation for whatever structures we build on the other side." Naming specific sins persuades some people first, then more, then eventually almost everyone, that "it might be wrong to use AI that way." The piece functions as both a friendly amendment to Leo's encyclical and a methodological prescription for AI critics, secular and religious alike.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Douthat in his "useful Catholic operator on a fast-moving culture-war issue" mode — and it is the column's strongest mode, because his theological frame here does explanatory rather than merely decorative work. The two-part diagnosis ("too late and too early") cleanly explains why the radical-skeptic position (Conti, Barba-Kay) is correct in its analytical premise but wrong in its political prescription. The "we regulate manifest harms, not hypothetical ones" claim is a true historical generalization (it covers industrial-revolution regulation, nuclear non-proliferation, the smartphone backlash) and it is doing real work — it tells the AI critic where to put their effort if they accept the diagnosis but want to be politically effective. The operative move is the moralize-specifically prescription, and it is genuinely original within the AI discourse: most AI critique is either at the level of system (Pope Leo's encyclical, much of the Compact-style critique) or at the level of policy (Klein-style public-option proposals, Crosscurrents 6/6). Douthat is staking out a third lane — the level of individual moral condemnation of specific uses — and arguing it is the level where critics have the most leverage and where the institutional foundation gets laid for later regulation. That is a useful contribution.

The piece's structural weakness is that the moralize-specifically prescription is mostly directed at critics whose existing rhetorical posture is already generalized lament, and Douthat doesn't engage the obvious counter: that generalized lament is the form their position takes because the specific-case condemnation requires giving up on the systemic critique. The novelist who outsources a chapter to Claude is not just doing one bad thing; in the radical-skeptic frame, that act is constituted as a sin by the broader claim that the AI-mediated authorial relation is corrupt regardless of which specific chapter it touches. To call only that act sinful is to concede that AI-assisted prose can be unproblematic in other contexts, which is exactly what the radical skeptics will not concede. Douthat's prescription works for the practical critic but undercuts the radical critic's position; he treats this as a feature (it's how political coalitions get built) but it is also a category that the column underargues.

A second analytical move worth flagging: the "students cheating with AI is gravely wrong" sentence is the easiest of his three examples and the one where universities, employers, and parents already agree. The harder cases are where his rhetorical method runs into real difficulty: is the academic researcher who uses AI to draft a literature review sinning? The doctor who uses AI to draft a discharge summary? The journalist who uses AI to clean up a transcript? Douthat's framework is built around obvious cases (cheating, ghostwriting, parasocial relationships) but the actual cultural battle over AI is being fought at the margins — where existing professional norms permit substitutability with traditional tools (research assistants, junior associates, transcription services) and where the AI substitute may be qualitatively continuous with what was already allowed. The column doesn't engage these middle cases; the strongest version of the secular response would be "your sin-language works for the obvious cases and dissolves at the margin, which is where actual regulation has to operate." Douthat would probably grant this and say the obvious-cases-first strategy is the correct one for institution-building; that move is implicit but not stated.

Finally, the theological turn ("God will allow whatever time is necessary unless this is the last technological temptation") is doing more work than it appears. It functions as a hedge — Douthat is not committing to either the AI-is-just-another-technology view or the AI-is-eschatologically-distinct view, which lets him recommend incrementalist tactics without losing the radical-skeptics' framing. That is rhetorically efficient but worth noticing: it lets him write a centrist-pragmatist column with maximalist theological subtitles, which is a useful position for him to occupy but should not be confused for actually settling the question. The implicit Christian reader is being told "do the incrementalist work; God's got the rest covered," which is a coherent move only if the eschatological hedge bears real weight, and the column doesn't actually make that argument.

↩ Back to TOC

What Pope Leo Should Have Said About AI

Gregory Conti · 2026-06-03 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Pope Leo XIV's encyclical Magnifica Humanitas on AI, plus Andrew Sullivan's reading of the encyclical as "calm and moderate" and William Jones's prior Compact piece on the same document.

Conti contends that the encyclical, while widely received as tech-critical, in fact concedes the most important argumentative ground at the outset by accepting AI as a fait accompli and framing the Church's task as helping humanity "live with" the technology — the inevitabilist premise of American liberal regulation-discourse. He notes that the Church has historically helped suppress technologies (human cloning) and that the framing of Magnifica Humanitas — built around Leo XIV's positioning as heir to Leo XIII's Rerum Novarum on industrialization — forecloses prohibition by treating AI as just-the-latest-disruption. Conti argues two features make generative AI categorically different from prior technologies (telephone, social media, nuclear weapons): (a) it strips humans of our monopoly on fluent language production — citing Yuval Levin's framing of AI as "a linguistic breakthrough" — and theological implications follow if "the word" no longer belongs uniquely to God and his image-bearers; (b) the AI builders themselves express terror about losing control, with "p(doom)" estimates and "broken containment" incidents already on record. Because the encyclical doesn't grapple with these features, Conti says, it fails to ask the only questions that matter: should we continue this experiment at all, and should we resist rather than regulate? Its policy proposals (worker retraining, child-protection from sexual content) Conti calls "woefully underpowered" against the prospect of a superhuman multimodal intelligence, and he closes by saying the only fit response is to "yell 'stop!'" — prohibition, not "regulatory fiddling" — but that this ethos is foreign to American liberal regulators, including, unfortunately, Leo XIV.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece's central move is to identify Magnifica Humanitas as participating in the very technocratic paradigm it nominally critiques — a sharp diagnostic observation that the post-liberal Catholic critique of regulation-as-acquiescence is uniquely positioned to make. Conti is correct that "AI is a transformative technology that requires adaptation" and "AI is a categorical threat that requires extirpation" are different propositions, and that the encyclical's Rerum Novarum analogy commits it to the first while pretending to register the concerns of the second. The Yuval Levin "linguistic breakthrough" citation is doing real work here: it's a non-Catholic, non-alarmist conservative source who is willing to name the categorical difference (humans lose monopoly on language production), and Conti uses it without flagging it as load-bearing, which lets the reader assess the claim on its merits rather than on the citation's politics.

The strongest argument from inside the piece's own logic is the prior-prohibition example: human cloning. Conti is right that "we could but we don't" is a coherent moral category even in late-modern liberal societies, and that the encyclical's failure to even consider this option for AI is a tell about its underlying inevitabilism. The Andrew Sullivan reference doubles as evidence — even a sympathetic American liberal reader registered the document as "calm and moderate" rather than apocalyptic.

The weakest part is the prohibition prescription. Conti says the only "conceivably fit response" is "rejection and prohibition, not regulatory fiddling." But he never specifies who could prohibit, with what enforcement mechanism, against what jurisdictional substitutes (China, Russia, an independent open-source community). Human cloning is genuinely a useful analogy because the experiments are slow, expensive, easily detectable, and require specialized labs — making moral consensus enforceable. Generative AI, by contrast, is being trained on commodity hardware by hundreds of organizations across multiple sovereignties, and unilateral US prohibition would not stop a Chinese, Russian, or open-source replica. Conti's piece could survive this objection if it explicitly named the multilateral coordination problem and asked whether the Church has anything to say about it; instead he treats the call for prohibition as if its political-economy were not the actual hard part. The encyclical Conti wants would be more honest than the one Leo wrote, but it wouldn't necessarily be more effective.

The other tension is between Conti's stated agnosticism on consciousness ("AI will never actually be conscious — as I also believe") and his claim that AI poses extinction-level risk. The two are not contradictory (a sufficiently capable optimizer doesn't need to be conscious to be dangerous, and Conti gestures at this) but the piece doesn't do the work to integrate them, and the reader is left wondering whether Conti's case is fundamentally a humanist-theological case (we lose linguistic distinctiveness) or a Bostrom-style instrumental-convergence case (the system gets out of our control). These are different arguments, and bundling them weakens both. The strongest version of the piece would either pick the theological frame and develop the "what is a Christian conception of personhood without the linguistic monopoly" line, or pick the safety frame and develop the "p(doom)" line. As written, the piece does both and lets each carry the other.

For the roster: Conti is a Princeton politics professor and Compact's editor-at-large, so this is essentially in-house Compact editorial. His piece is doing the kind of structural-frame critique that the project values, but the piece's prescription (prohibit AI) is sufficiently unmoored from any feasible mechanism that the diagnostic part has to do nearly all the work. The diagnostic part is good enough that the piece earns its space, but Conti's policy register is weaker than his analytical register and should be read accordingly.

↩ Back to TOC

How much more software do we really need?

Noahpinion · 2026-06-02 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Anthropic's pending ~$1T IPO at ~20x annualized revenue; a series of practitioner reports (John Loeber, Uber COO Andrew Macdonald, EntelligenceAI, Jellyfish, Microsoft canceling Claude Code licenses, Salesforce overhauling employee targets) finding tokens-in is decoupled from products-shipped; Chad Jones's "AI and our Economic Future" weak-links paper; Demirer et al. (2026) on AI-coding production hierarchies; David Oks on ATMs vs. iPhone banking; Azhar and Warren on AI-first organizational redesign.

Smith argues Anthropic is probably underpriced at 20x revenue given 130% quarterly growth, but uses the IPO as the entry point to a deeper question — companies spending hundreds of thousands per month on Claude Code can't articulate what's actually being shipped. He offers three explanations. First, weak-links economics: Chad Jones shows that automating one input only raises output by 1/(1-s) where s is the input's GDP share; software is ~2% of GDP, so even infinite automation yields ~2% gains. Demirer et al. quantify this empirically: AI coding agents produce 741% more lines of code and 65% more pull requests but only 20% more releases, because the binding constraint shifts to reviewing, integrating, and distributing. Second, competition: AI compresses the skill distribution, so B-tier engineers with AI can compete with FAANG, commoditizing software and putting downward pressure on margins — which explains why software stocks have lagged in the AI era. Third, and most important, the demand-side ceiling: consumer software may be a mature industry. The internet's physical buildout is mostly done, time-spent-online peaked during COVID and may be reversing, and post-Facebook entrants (Uber, Airbnb, Figma, Notion, crypto) are all marginal compared to spreadsheets or search. Smith — calling himself "softwared out" — argues AI's real economic gains will come not from better Facebook/Amazon but from new categories: robotics (if dexterity is solved) and AI-first business models that rebuild organizations around AI the way 1920s factories were rebuilt around electricity (the Paul David/Tim Harford story) and banking was rebuilt around the iPhone rather than the ATM.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Smith at his most genuinely useful — synthesizing the practitioner literature (Loeber, Macdonald, Azhar-Warren) and the academic literature (Jones, Demirer et al., Paul David's electrification papers) into a single causal story. The Jones weak-links framing is doing a lot of work: it explains why the macro impact of AI on GDP may be smaller than the breathless valuations imply, while leaving room for the technology to be genuinely transformative within specific bottlenecked sectors. The Demirer numbers (741% more lines of code → 65% more PRs → 20% more releases) are the kind of falsifying-evidence-against-the-hype-cycle citation the AI discourse needs more of, and Smith deserves credit for foregrounding them. So is the David Oks line about ATMs not killing tellers but the iPhone did — a useful counter to the standard automation-narrative.

The strongest substantive contribution is the demand-ceiling argument. The claim that post-Facebook consumer software has been marginal (Uber/Airbnb as service apps, Figma/Notion as productivity tools, none game-changing the way spreadsheets were) is harder to dismiss than it first sounds. The implication — that Claude Code's customers can ship 17x more code but can't find ~17x more user demand because consumers' time and attention are saturated — is a serious challenge to the assumption that productivity improvements in software production automatically translate into revenue. This recasts the AI-revenue gap not as a temporary diffusion lag but as a structural demand-side problem.

Where the piece is weaker: Smith concedes Anthropic is probably underpriced and argues consumer software is mature and that AI value will come from new categories not yet invented. These are in tension. If the demand ceiling is real and new business models are a "hard, ill-defined, may-or-may-not-happen" frontier, the case for paying 20x revenue for the coding-agent leader gets weaker, not stronger — most of Anthropic's current revenue is coming from the very tokenmaxxing he is arguing produces little economic value. The honest version of Smith's argument would concede that Anthropic's current revenue may be exposed to a future markdown when buyer-side budgets recalibrate around shipped-output metrics (as Salesforce and Microsoft are already starting to do). He gestures at this risk ("downward pressure on software margins") but doesn't connect it to the IPO valuation he just defended.

There's also a missing alternative explanation that Smith doesn't engage: that tokenmaxxing isn't a productivity push at all but a defensive labor-discipline mechanism — using AI-tool-use targets as a way to shift the wage/effort bargain in management's favor, similar to how Six Sigma and lean were partly real productivity tools and partly cultural disciplining technologies. The Meta-leaderboard and "spend your salary in tokens" anecdotes are easier to read in that frame than as confused productivity hopes. Smith is well-positioned to engage this (he has written critically about labor-market dynamics before) but doesn't.

The piece is at its weakest in the closing robotics/AI-first-business-models prescription, which is essentially "the answer is whatever someone hasn't invented yet." That's an honest concession to uncertainty rather than a real answer; the operative claim is just "the IPO is fine because the buildout of the new business models is coming." Compared to the rigor of the diagnostic section (weak links, Demirer attenuation, demand ceiling), the prescriptive section is faith-based. That's not necessarily wrong — Smith is right that general-purpose technologies historically require organizational reinvention to deliver — but it's worth flagging that the same argument has been made about every wave of corporate IT for thirty years.

↩ Back to TOC

Law professors prefer AI over peer answers

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A new paper by Alejandro Salinas et al. in which sixteen US law professors created 40 contracts-course questions, wrote their own short-answer responses, and then judged 2,918 anonymized comparisons between human and LLM answers — finding LLMs won 75.33% of comparisons and were rarely flagged as harmful (3.53% vs. 12.06% for the human professors). Also responding to a separate study (via John Chamberlain) finding AI tools can mass-produce academic finance papers nearly indistinguishable from human-authored research.

Cowen presents two empirical results — law professors preferring LLM responses to peer responses 75-to-25, and AI-produced academic finance papers being indistinguishable from human-authored research — and uses both to argue that academia's human capital has been devalued. The previously load-bearing comparative advantage of being prolific in a judgment-rich field (law, finance) is no longer valuable, because the marginal cost of producing prolifically has fallen to near-zero. Cowen anticipates the standard academic defense ("but I know what questions to ask") and explicitly tells the reader to "solve for the equilibrium": the wage paid for merely asking questions, in a world where LLMs answer them better than the questioner's peers, is low. His concluding move: "the real rewards will go to people doing new, innovative, and hitherto impossible projects with AI" — the academic value chain is being restructured around AI-augmented frontier projects, not around credentialed expertise in established methods.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is direct Cowen, not Straussian Cowen — the argumentative move is stated frontally. The salience of the study is real: the Salinas et al. design is unusually clean for an LLM-evaluation paper. The participating professors created the questions themselves (so the LLM wasn't optimizing for known prompts), wrote their own answers (so the LLM wasn't being compared to weak humans), and judged blinded comparisons (so the rating wasn't biased by knowing which was which). 75% win rate against your professional peers is a striking effect size, and the harmful-flag asymmetry (3.5% LLM vs. 12% human) is doing additional work the headline rate doesn't capture: LLMs are not just preferred, they're also safer in the sense of avoiding the kinds of responses raters flag as problematic. If this generalizes — and the contracts-tutoring domain is a defensible test bed because legal reasoning is "judgment-rich" rather than ground-truth-anchored — it's a stronger result against the "AI is fine on facts but breaks on judgment" defense than most existing studies.

Where Cowen's framing overreaches is the equilibrium-wage move. The argument that the value of "asking the right questions" falls to near-zero in an LLM-saturated world is wrong on its face: the equilibrium is being set by the distribution of question-askers, not by the marginal cost of answers. If LLMs make answers cheap, then the marginal-value scaling shifts: someone who asks a question whose answer was previously impossible to access now creates more value than someone who asks routine questions, because the gating constraint has moved upstream. Cowen knows this — he says it in the closing line ("the real rewards will go to people doing new, innovative, and hitherto impossible projects with AI") — but the body of the post argues the opposite, that question-asking value collapses to zero. The piece is internally inconsistent on this; the closing line is the right one, but the body is the dramatic-but-wrong one that will get more retweets.

The deeper analytical question the piece doesn't engage is what happens to the institutions that selected for the prolific-academic-paper-producer comparative advantage when that advantage disappears. Tenure, peer review, journal publication, and citation-based prestige are all institutional structures built around a regime where producing high-quality publications was costly. If LLMs collapse the cost of production but not the cost of verification (which they may also collapse, per the finance-paper indistinguishability finding), the institutional architecture of academic credibility breaks down. Cowen has written on this before — the "talent is the binding constraint" theme — but the post here just tells academics they're cooked without specifying what the institutional successor looks like. The structural critique is implicit; making it explicit would be the more useful version of the post.

↩ Back to TOC

Can AI Cure Cancer?

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: AI lab leaders (Hassabis, Brockman, Amodei) routinely citing "curing cancer" as the payoff that justifies AI's broader societal disruption; recurring D.C. talking points from administration officials defending data-center build-out on cancer-cure grounds. This is a transcript of an Argument live debate held in San Francisco between Jerusalem Demsas and Kelsey Piper, posted on Slow Boring as a podcast cross-post. Piper argues that "curing cancer" means three different things depending on which lab CEO you ask — Hassabis's AlphaFold-driven incremental therapeutic development (defensible), Brockman's empowered amateurs writing personalized mRNA vaccines for their dogs (cool but a narrow mechanism), and Amodei's recursive-self-improvement "build God" plan (which she finds both terrifying and orthogonal to incremental cancer research). Her core claim: LLMs are insanely good at verifiable tasks (math, coding, text where the answer is in the text) but bad at unverifiable ones like cancer biology, so we should expect the next decade to look like the last 40 years of cancer progress — steady incremental gains driven by specialized RL systems like AlphaFold, not a step-change. Demsas pushes back, arguing she's seen scientific possibilities expand faster than expected during her chemistry-major years and expects a significant step-change; she also emphasizes underrated indirect benefits like AI-as-centralizing-information-source ("Grok, is this true?") and AI lowering the entrepreneurship barrier (she credits Claude for letting her launch The Argument without scaling lawyers). They mostly agree on the diagnostic side (mammography/colonoscopy improvements) and the value of AI-as-literature-review for cancer patients, but disagree on whether AlphaFold-class breakthroughs will compound into something more than gradual progress. The framing essay (likely written by the Slow Boring team, not Yglesias personally) notes both started skeptical and remained so after research, with Piper conceding she'd lose the debate if all cancers were curable in 10 years.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is essentially a podcast cross-post, not a Yglesias essay — the substantive content is a Demsas-Piper debate transcript with a short framing intro and a brief paywall-bait paragraph. The intro is likely written by the Slow Boring editorial team rather than Yglesias personally (it's signed off with "Jerusalem dug into" as if from the show's PR voice, not Yglesias's first-person), so attribution requires care. The transcript itself is the substance, and what's most interesting about it is the implicit alignment with Yglesias's broader worldview even though he didn't write it.

Piper's central analytic move — distinguishing AlphaFold-style specialized RL (real, useful, incremental) from the LLM-driven "build God" plan (terrifying and bad bet) — maps cleanly onto Yglesias's general anti-hype stance toward Silicon Valley AI maximalism. The implication, which Piper makes explicit: if AlphaFold is where the cancer-curing action actually is, that's an argument against the "country of geniuses in a data center" plan that Amodei and others use to justify the broader AI build-out. This is a meaningful intra-rationalist disagreement: Piper is essentially saying that the AI-safety community's worry about runaway recursive self-improvement is justified, and that this worry should make us less receptive to the cancer-cure pitch when it's used as PR cover for compute scaling. It's the rare case of a Slow Boring guest argument that pushes the AI-safety pessimist side without immediately conceding to abundance/build-it logic.

Demsas's optimism is more diffuse and harder to evaluate. She makes two genuinely interesting non-cancer moves: AI as a centralizing information force (countering social-media fragmentation, with "Grok, is this true?" as an emergent fact-checking mechanism), and AI as an entrepreneurship multiplier (specifically in lower-resource economies — her Eritrea/Ethiopia gesture). Both are speculative but underrated relative to the more familiar AI-doom and AI-utopia poles. The "AI is libbed-out across languages" finding from Piper's multi-language experiment (frontier models reason in English regardless of prompt language, and broadly defend individual rights in any language) is the most reportable empirical claim in the transcript — it has implications for AI's effects on cultural-political environments globally that neither speaker fully develops.

The transcript form is a problem for synthesis purposes: there's no thesis, no resolution, and significant filler (banter about the clock, paywall promotion, mic logistics). For roundup purposes, the analytically load-bearing portions are Piper's verifiable-vs.-unverifiable distinction, the three definitions of "cure cancer" (Hassabis/Brockman/Amodei), and Demsas's centralization claim. The Amodei "build God to cure cancer" critique is the highest-stakes content — it's a sharp version of the argument that AI labs are using cancer cures as moral cover for a project they can't actually justify on its own terms.
