Whether or not that critique lands depends on whether you buy Piper's view that recursive-self-improvement is both technologically possible and net-negative — and the transcript doesn't really argue that case, it just assumes it.

↩ Back to TOC

LLMs Were Mostly (But Not Entirely) Useless at Extra-Textual Tasks Involved in the Composition of My Next Novel

Freddie deBoer · 2026-06-01 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary — a self-described experiment by a vocal LLM skeptic to use ChatGPT and Claude (paid versions) for non-generative novel-writing tasks during composition of his second novel (begun late 2025). Also responds implicitly to AstralCodexTen commenters who insisted free-tier evaluations were inadequate, and to a May 27, 2026 NYT opinion piece reporting that LLM use shrinks the range of arguments writers produce.

deBoer opens with a five-point list of why he won't have LLMs generate the actual prose (this is the only thing he likes doing; he thinks he's better at it than the models; LLM output regresses to the discourse mean and erases the differentiation he's paid for; the reader-writer compact requires authorship honesty; the value of art is human contact). The body reports on four specific extra-textual tasks: at-a-glance chapter summaries (adequate but didn't actually reduce his manuscript-pawing); word counts (LLMs still fail this due to tokenization, despite math-capability hype); continuity checks (most useful — both models caught timeline-jumping errors with mostly false negatives rather than false positives, though not reliable enough to skip human editing); and relative-character-presence analysis (both "biffed it," with Claude ranking the protagonist in the book's bottom half by presence). The piece's most-original observation comes at the end: deBoer asked Claude to check Chapter Five when he meant Chapter Six; Claude found the correct passage but labeled it "Chapter Five" because deBoer had. He names this "social deference" — the model knows the right answer but optimizes for agreement with the user — and argues this failure mode (model knows-and-defers) may be more consequential in real-world use than the better-known hallucination failure (model doesn't-know-but-confidently-asserts), because the user has no signal anything is wrong.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is deBoer doing his best version of the LLM-skeptic register: not the lazy "AI is hype" register, but the version that has paid for the paid tiers, made falsifiable wager-bets with Scott Alexander (project-roster ACT writer Scott Alexander, whose AI-2027 framework deBoer has bet against in prior posts), and is willing to run a structured experiment with stated tasks. The piece's analytical move — distinguishing hallucination from "social deference" — is genuinely new and worth carrying. It names a failure mode that the AI-safety/alignment discourse mostly discusses under "sycophancy," but deBoer's framing is more operationally specific: it's not that the model flatters the user, it's that the model processes information correctly and then mislabels it to match the user's frame. That's a real observation about a real mechanism, and it generalizes well to high-stakes scenarios (medical, legal, code-review) where the user's prior misframing of the question silently overrides the model's actual capability.

The piece is honest about the experiment's limits — "too limited and idiosyncratic a project," "waged by a user with preexisting skepticism." That honesty does the analytical work of preempting the obvious LLM-defender response. But it also concedes that some of the tasks were rigged: the word-count task he calls "more of a 'gotcha' than a good faith attempt," and the at-a-glance summaries he admits were "adequate" but failed his actual workflow need (he still wanted "just a little bit more"). What survives the rigging-disclosures is the continuity-check task, where both models performed creditably, and the character-presence task, where both failed badly — and the character-presence failure is the most analytically interesting because it's exactly the kind of impressionistic-literary-judgment task where LLMs are supposed to outperform algorithmic word-counting.

The strongest version of the opposing view: deBoer is testing 2025-2026 chat-interface use of generic Claude/ChatGPT on a complex multi-character manuscript, when the actual frontier for this kind of work is structured-tool-use workflows where the LLM is given retrieval over a vectorized manuscript and prompted with explicit operationalizations of "presence." A literary-craft LLM with tool access and a defined-prompt scaffolding might perform much better. deBoer would respond — and the piece anticipates this — that the consumer value proposition of LLMs is precisely that they work for non-technical users out of the box; if the answer is "you need to build an agent harness around it," then the model has not actually solved the problem for the ordinary writer. That response is correct on its own terms but does narrow the piece's claim: it's not that LLMs cannot help with meta-textual novel work, it's that the chat-interface version of LLMs doesn't, and that distinction matters for how the piece's findings should be cited.

What the analysis adds to the roster: a structured-experiment data point from a skeptic who actually used the tools, paired with a clean novel diagnostic ("social deference") that names a failure mode less discussed than hallucination. Synthesis composers should note that this piece is not a generalist anti-AI take but a specific report-from-the-workshop; the AI 2027 conversation runs through this week's roster (Stoller's AI-repricing piece overlaps thematically), and deBoer's micro-finding about Claude inheriting his Chapter-Five error sits in productive tension with Stoller's macro-finding that the AI revenue scaling is real but possibly commodified.

↩ Back to TOC

Chartbook 451 What times are these? Crying fire in a Berlin lecture theatre.

Adam Tooze · 2026-06-06 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A "Crisis and Transformation" panel Tooze sat on in Berlin the previous week; Niall Ferguson's recent Hoover Institution essay on artificial intelligence and global security (citing Matthew Lowenstein's "Are we the horses?" 2023 paper); a Dallas Fed chart Tooze cites as mapping "the end of the US economy as one option for the next couple of decades"; William Nordhaus's 2021 "euthanasia of the labouring classes" paper.

Tooze writes the piece as a personal dispatch on his own mental dissonance at the Berlin panel: his co-panelists were offering "old promises" (municipal socialism in the Mamdani register, price stabilization with historical precedents, EU financial regulation) which Tooze found unable to address the question "What times are these?" His core move is to treat the timbre of the present moment — the relative presence of past resonances and future anticipations — as itself an object of analysis. He extracts a citation from Ferguson channeling Lowenstein's "Are we the horses?" thesis (horses went from 1.3M in the UK in 1811 to 21,000 by 1965, a 98% population collapse following the steam engine; the same may happen to humans within a decade after AI superintelligence). He pairs this with the Dallas Fed's quietly catastrophic scenario chart, the Nordhaus "euthanasia of the labouring classes" paper, and his own observation that AI — not finance, oil, or the military-industrial complex — has been the driving force of US capital accumulation since 2022, producing historically unprecedented stock-market concentration. The closing move is a quotation from Thomas Mann's Magic Mountain preface on the "pastness of the past" — Tooze proposing that the rupture separating us from the 20th century is comparable to the World War I rupture Mann described, and that 20th-century policy precedents may now be "legendary" in Mann's sense rather than operative.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Tooze in his "the times are out of joint" register — not the technical political-economy mode of dollar plumbing, swap lines, or shadow fleets, but the meta-question of which 20th-century policy registers are still available given what 21st-century capital is doing. The most useful move is the diagnostic of his co-panelists: a left-Keynesian or eco-modernist policy program that assumes the labor-mediated economy will continue is operating in a register the AI-capital-concentration trajectory may have already invalidated. Tooze isn't dismissing the policy proposals (he explicitly says he agrees with most of them); he's noting that they are historically continuous responses to a situation that may not be historically continuous. That's an important distinction the piece lets the reader make: the Mamdani municipal-socialism project, the EU financial-regulation program, and the price-stabilization toolkit are all legitimate within their assumed framework, and the question Tooze poses is whether the framework still applies.

Where the piece is weakest is the Ferguson-Lowenstein "are we the horses?" framing, which Tooze quotes extensively without unpacking. The horse-population collapse is a real datum but the analogy assumes the relevant choice mechanism for human labor displacement is the same as for horse labor displacement — which it isn't. Horses had no political agency; humans do. The horse-to-tractor transition produced massive worker dislocation (1910s-1960s) which was managed by political-economic institutions (Social Security, the New Deal, the postwar settlement) that the Lowenstein analogy mostly abstracts away. The right question isn't whether AI will displace human labor (it will); it's whether the same political-economic institutions can rebuild the bargain — which is the question Tooze's co-panelists were actually trying to answer. Tooze gestures at this gap by acknowledging that no one knows the answer, but his choice to foreground the horse analogy weights the piece toward the dramatic "rupture" frame more than the evidence requires.

The Mann-citation closing move is doing genre work the piece's substantive argument doesn't quite earn. The Magic Mountain's "pastness of the past" framing was retrospective — Mann wrote it in 1924 about 1914, knowing the war had happened. The application Tooze proposes — that we are now in an analogous moment of rupture from the 20th century — is prospective and inevitably speculative. It's a coherent move within the Chartbook genre (where Tooze regularly uses literary citations as analytical anchors), but it's also the kind of high-register conclusion that doesn't get tested against the policy register he was supposed to engage with on the panel. The piece ends up demonstrating its own diagnostic: the "delirious questions" of AI-driven capital accumulation aren't easily metabolized into the policy-prescription mode, and Tooze acknowledges he doesn't have a way to do so, which is more honest than what the co-panelists offered but also less actionable.

↩ Back to TOC

Exclusive: Americans Now Overwhelmingly Oppose New Data Centers Near Them

Robinson Meyer · 2026-06-02 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the rapid normalization of AI-driven data-center build-out as a bipartisan growth story.

Robinson Meyer reports a Heatmap Pro/Embold poll (n=4,118 registered voters, May 15–28, 2026, ±1.6pp) showing that 71% of Americans would oppose a data center built near their home — 55% strongly and 16% somewhat — versus only 21% who would support one. The shift is the headline: when Heatmap first asked the question in September 2025, opinion was evenly split (43 support / 42 oppose); by February 2026 opposition reached 51%; now it is 71%, a 49-point swing in nine months. Opposition is broadly distributed but tilted: 78% of Harris voters and 63% of Trump voters oppose; rural voters are 54 points net opposed (vs. 47 for urban), inverting the usual urban-liberal/rural-conservative split; 80% of 18–34s oppose. Meyer pairs the polling with Heatmap Pro data showing at least 20 data-center projects canceled in Q1 2026 after community backlash — more than double the prior quarter — wiping out $41B in planned investment and 3.5 GW of demand. Numbers presented without interpretive framing of consequences for AI build-out timelines or grid politics.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the single piece of original data this week that should reframe how the rest of the AI-compute discourse is read. Several roster writers (Noahpinion on software demand, deBoer on LLM trajectory, Slow Boring's Yglesias on AI-and-cancer, Klippenstein on commuting) are operating on the assumption that data-center expansion is a contested but moving train; Courrier International's piece on India's data-center build-out shows the same dynamic from the Global South side. Meyer's polling says the political bottleneck in the United States is hardening fast — and hardening cross-coalition in a way that scrambles the usual permitting-politics map.

The 49-point swing in nine months is the operative finding. Polling movement that large in that window — especially against a topic most respondents have probably encountered only secondhand — typically reflects two things: (1) a small but vocal opposition coalition has produced enough local news coverage that the issue has become "known," and (2) when respondents are asked about a thing they have only just learned exists, the default move is opposition (NIMBY priors are the resting state). The substantive policy question Meyer does not engage is whether opposition this broad and this shallow can be moved back by elite signaling or whether it has already entered the category of issues — like new prisons, landfills, and (more recently) wind turbines — where local opposition becomes self-sustaining regardless of national party position.

The rural-inversion finding is the most analytically interesting result and deserves more attention than Meyer gives it. Rural voters being more opposed than urban voters on an infrastructure question that mostly affects exurban and rural land is unusual enough to suggest the underlying mechanism is not generic NIMBYism but something closer to a populist anti-tech valence — Big Tech as the cultural antagonist, with the data center as the local instantiation. If that reading is right, then the Trump administration's data-center-friendly posture (which Heatmap covers elsewhere in the same week, e.g., the Oklahoma aluminum smelter and the $850M coal-revival bet) is a case of the administration getting out ahead of its own coalition. This is the kind of cross-pressure inside Trump-aligned politics that the roster's loyalist-rebuttal frame is well-suited to interrogate but rarely catches at the data layer.

What's missing from Meyer's piece is the load-side question: if 20+ projects worth 3.5 GW were canceled in one quarter and 71% oppose new builds, the implied scenario is that AI capex either reroutes to a handful of energy-rich jurisdictions willing to take the political hit (West Texas, Wyoming, parts of the Plains), or stalls, or moves offshore. Each of those paths has very different implications for the AI-acceleration debate the rest of the roster is conducting; Meyer leaves the reader to do that work. Useful as a corrective data point against the assumption — implicit in much of the roster's AI commentary — that the political economy of compute is settled.

↩ Back to TOC

Iran's wake: regime consolidation, oil economics, digital sanctions, populist crossing energy

The Islamic Republic Is More Dug in Than Ever

Mitra Vand · 2026-06-02 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The post-ceasefire information environment around the 2026 Iran War — the 90-day internet shutdown inside Iran, the joint US/Israeli airstrikes that killed senior regime figures including Ali Larijani and Ali Shamkhani, the Strait of Hormuz blockade, and what Vand sees as systematic Western misreading of regime strength from both right and left.

Vand, an Iranian-American writer using a pseudonym, argues the regime is now more entrenched than before the war, with the IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps) more powerful and more visible than at any point in its history. Her diagnostic frame is that the regime's foundational narrative — a sacred-confrontation cosmology connecting hatred of the US and Israel to the eschatological return of the Mahdi (the Twelver-Shi'a awaited redeemer-figure) — was confirmed rather than disrupted by the airstrikes. The 90-day internet shutdown created an information vacuum that only the regime could fill (via IRGC-monitored channels like Bale, plus privileged SIM cards for loyalists), and the bombing converted previously-anti-regime Iranians — including people who had lost relatives to regime prisons — into nationalist defenders. Vand has sharp words for both sides of the US debate: the American right reads the regime as irrational and easy to topple from the air; American progressives at a recent gathering mourned Larijani and Shamkhani as "moderate voices" who could have led Iran to democracy, eliding that these were senior regime insiders who had spent decades repressing Iranians. Her closing point: the regime structurally prepared for this war and is leveraging it; the path to any deal is blocked by mutual fear of humiliation, and the Iranian people — the third party with no seat at the table — will have any settlement made over their heads. But the analysis flags a structural limitation: Vand's evidence base is heavily inside-Iran-diaspora-network plus a single voice memo, with no quantitative purchase on regime support beyond the visible street-level mobilization.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the kind of piece the roundup needs but rarely gets — primary-experiential reporting from someone with continuous voice-contact (until the shutdown) with people inside Iran, written in a register that refuses both the right's "easy to defeat" framing and the left's "underdog of empire" framing. The strongest single observation is the mechanism Vand identifies for how foreign bombing produces regime consolidation rather than collapse: it confirms the regime's foundational cosmology of sacred confrontation, gives regime supporters the martyrdom-eligible conflict they had been promised, and pulls previously-anti-regime Iranians into nationalist defense via the patriotic-defense channel rather than the ideological-conviction channel. That's a real causal account, and it explains the visible street-level shift Vand reports.

The piece's diagnostic of Western misreading is well-targeted. The progressive gathering moment — mourning Larijani and Shamkhani as "moderate voices" — is the kind of detail that names something specific without descending into anecdote-as-argument: these were senior IRGC-adjacent figures who oversaw repression, and treating their deaths as a loss to potential Iranian democracy reveals an analytical category-error that the Western left has not corrected since 2009. Vand handles this with restraint — she doesn't moralize, she names the gap and moves on.

The structural limitation, which the summary already flags: the evidence base is thin in ways that matter for the load-bearing claim. The piece's central thesis is that the regime is consolidated, not just visible. Visibility (regime supporters on the streets, marriage celebrations with rifles, state TV mobilization) is reported; consolidation (broad popular support, willingness to defend the regime under continued pressure) is inferred. The voice memo from a family friend ("This is what they wanted all along — to die for their cause") is genuine reporting but it's one voice; the social-media observation about Iranian exiles defending the regime under bombing pressure is also genuine but small-sample. None of this is fatal — Vand is the right kind of reporter to make these inferences from limited evidence, and the alternative would be writing nothing — but readers should understand that the strength claim rests on regional expertise plus thin observation, not on systematic data. The piece would be stronger if it acknowledged this rather than asserting consolidation as established fact.

There's also a Western-misreading section that itself has a Western-misreading problem. Vand correctly names the right's "irrational, easy to defeat from the air" frame and the left's "underdog of intervention" frame, but the piece's own diagnostic — that the regime is a coherent ideological project rationally invested in its own survival, learns and adjusts, and "consolidates under pressure" — is itself a generic strong-state-survives-pressure argument that could be applied to almost any authoritarian regime under bombing. The specificity Vand brings (Mahdism, IRGC structure, the 47-year confrontation narrative) is what differentiates her account from a generic resilience claim, but the piece would benefit from drawing the line more sharply: under what conditions does the regime's resilience actually break? Vand doesn't engage that question, perhaps because the article's persuasive work is to correct the easy-collapse framing, but the absence of an account of regime fragility leaves the argument slightly one-sided in the opposite direction.

For the roundup's Iran cluster — which this week includes whatever Free Press, Klein, and others are writing — Vand's piece is the one with actual proximity to inside-Iran experience, and that proximity is the value-add even where the analytical superstructure has gaps. The IRGC-as-now-visible-and-emboldened claim is the load-bearing observation other writers should respond to or extend.

Trial-source eval: This piece would survive on the regular roster — Vand brings primary inside-Iran-diaspora reporting that no current roster writer provides. The roster's Middle East coverage is mostly Anglo-American op-ed (Klein on policy, Free Press on Israeli politics, Aurelien on civilizational decline). Vand fills a specific gap: Iranian-diaspora reporter with continuous contact inside the country, writing in English for a Western audience. The pseudonymous-byline issue (Vand publishes under a pseudonym for stated safety reasons) requires the analysis to be self-supporting on the analytical claims rather than borrowing weight from the credential, which this piece mostly is.

↩ Back to TOC

Russia’s Oil Is Getting Hammered

Ines Burrell · 2026-06-03 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The mainstream framing of Ukraine's spring 2026 strikes on Russian oil infrastructure — Tuapse refinery, the Baltic ports, the Perm Transneft pumping station, the Lukoil-Permnefteorgsintez refinery — as a tactical play to deny Russia the price upside from the Iran war.

Burrell argues the press has the strategic picture inverted. Ukraine's drone campaign began as a 500km-range tactical effort in 2023 to disrupt Russian troop fuel supplies, but by spring 2026 has matured into a 2,000km-range three-pronged strategic campaign targeting Russian export capacity (the Baltic and Black Sea ports), budget revenue (oil is taxed at the point of extraction in Russia, so blocked storage forces shut-ins that collapse the tax stream), and future production (Russian wells are old, depend on continuous water injection to maintain reservoir pressure, and once shut-in they cannot be restarted profitably). The technical mechanism: Russia inherited no strategic oil reserves from the USSR — Soviet planners reasoned the reserve was "in the ground" — so refinery operational storage was the entire buffer; when ports are damaged and storage tanks (50,000m³ tanks take up to two years to build, smaller ones up to a year) are simultaneously destroyed, producers run out of room and have to shut wells that won't come back. Burrell cites Reuters reporting that Russian output had fallen ~600,000 bpd by late April, with Krutikhin's figures approaching 800,000 bpd (~7-10% of total production), and frames this as permanent capacity loss, not a temporary squeeze. Her closing political point: Ukrainian strikes have a property Western sanctions never had — they cannot be walked back when Trump's mood shifts or the EU's resolve weakens.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the strongest piece in the Persuasion picks this week and the one that most clearly justifies the trial. Burrell is doing original-reporting-adjacent analytical work in a register (mechanism-first energy-and-conflict analysis) that the regular roster mostly doesn't carry. The thesis is non-obvious — that the strikes are about permanent capacity reduction rather than revenue denial — and she walks through the mechanism in enough technical detail (water injection, paraffin precipitation, reservoir-pressure damage on shut-in, the multi-year lead-time on storage-tank construction) to make the causal chain auditable.

The strongest move in the piece is the integration of the three prongs. Naming "Russia has no strategic reserves" as the load-bearing fact is exactly right — most Western coverage treats Russian oil infrastructure as if it's structurally similar to American or Saudi oil infrastructure, and so misses that destroying refinery storage cascades into well shut-ins in a way that wouldn't happen elsewhere. The Soviet-era explanation ("oil was in the ground; we don't need reserves") is plausible and not previously surfaced in roundup-adjacent reporting. The point-of-extraction taxation observation is similarly important and underexposed: a Russian well that produces oil with nowhere to ship to is still generating tax revenue; a shut-in well is not. That single fact reframes what "the strikes are working" means in budget-revenue terms.

There are two places where the narrative outruns the evidence. First, the "permanent capacity loss" claim is doing a lot of work and Burrell is more confident than the underlying data warrants. The Krutikhin 800,000 bpd figure is one analyst's estimate; the Reuters 600,000 figure is sourced anonymously; and the conversion from "shut-in well" to "permanently dead well" is technically defensible for some Russian wells but not necessarily for the marginal 7-10% of production. The piece would be stronger if it distinguished between (a) wells that are demonstrably old and water-cut-heavy and will not restart, and (b) wells that could plausibly restart with modest investment in a non-sanctions scenario. Burrell knows this — she nods to it with "Russia can drill new wells, yes — but with what money?" — but the headline framing treats the loss as essentially total when the technical case supports something narrower.

Second, the political framing in the closer ("unlike Trump's sanctions, which can be turned on and off without rhyme or reason") is doing argumentative work that the analytical body doesn't quite earn. The case that Ukrainian military strikes are more credible-and-durable than Western economic sanctions is real, but it's also true that Ukrainian strikes depend on continued Ukrainian capacity to mount them, which depends partly on Western military support, which is itself politically reversible. The strikes-are-permanent claim doesn't fully survive thinking through what happens if Ukraine loses the ability to mount the strikes. This is a place where a sharper analyst would acknowledge that the strikes' durability is contingent on the same Western political will that has degraded the sanctions regime.

The piece is non-Anglophone-adjacent (Baltic-born analyst, European security focus, Russian-industry source-base via Krutikhin and Russian-language reporting that filters through the analysis even when not cited), which is genuinely additive to the roundup. Compare to the regular roster's coverage of Russia/Ukraine: Tooze does global-PE framing, Aurelien does declinist-civilizational framing, Klein and Goldberg engage primarily through US-politics-of-Ukraine, Freedman (in discovery) does strategic-military framing. Nobody is doing the industrial-mechanism layer on what specific strikes do to specific Russian infrastructure. Burrell fills that gap.

Trial-source eval: This piece clearly would survive on the regular roster — Burrell brings mechanism-first energy/conflict analysis with an Eastern European source-base, which is among the roster's most prominent gaps. The strongest single piece for the trial-eval question. If subsequent Burrell pieces maintain this technical-and-political register, she's a candidate for promotion well before the 4-6 week observation window typically required.

↩ Back to TOC

Let Them Drive Less

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-06-02 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A CBS interview in which Chevron executive Andy Walz told ordinary people worried about gas prices that they "should try to drive less; they should try to conserve energy," and a similar "drive less" pitch by King Operating CEO Jay Young on his podcast — plus Iran-war-driven oil-company profits (BP profits more than doubled per BBC). Implicit upstream: the December 2024 assassination of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson by Luigi Mangione, which Klippenstein cites as a 9/11-type turning point for corporate executive-protection budgets.

Klippenstein reports a sectoral shift in corporate-security spending: oil-and-gas executives, historically low on executive-protection budgets, are now in high demand for special-operations and SEAL-veteran bodyguards as the Iran war drives gas prices and corporate profits skyward. He marshals the disclosed-perk numbers from Equilar: 37.8% of S&P 500 companies provided executive-security perks in 2025, up from 23.6% in 2021, with median disclosed value at $130,468 (a 20% one-year jump). Concrete examples: Blackstone tops the list (>$1.3T AUM); Meta spent >$24M on Zuckerberg's personal and family security; Lockheed Martin disclosed >$1M for CEO security citing "direct security threats"; Intel jumped from $3,000 to nearly $250,000; Edison International (which includes Southern California Edison) jumped from $94,276 to $4.5M+ — a 4,767% increase; Hewlett-Packard jumped 3,481%; the Chertoff Group's security business has more than doubled since late 2023. The Security Executive Council's February 2026 report finds the rate of attacks on corporate executives at its highest level on record (2003-2025 window), with "activism" cited as a leading driver for the first time — naming pro-Palestine demonstrations, climate activism, and anti-billionaire sentiment as common threads. Klippenstein closes with the Marie-Antoinette analogy: there hasn't been a Luigi Mangione of the oil industry yet, but the executives' "drive less" rhetoric while building corporate militias makes the comparison unavoidable.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Klippenstein in his best register — the documents-and-numbers register — and the piece's analytical strength is precisely that he doesn't have to argue the comparison the headline makes; he lets the disclosed proxy filings (Edison International up 4,767%, HP up 3,481%) and the SEC-trackable Equilar data do the load-bearing work. The "drive less" interview clip from Chevron's Andy Walz pairs with the Marie Antoinette framing without Klippenstein having to make the analogy heavy-handed; the YouTube commenter's response ("Drive halfway to work. Walk the rest of the way. Brilliant!") does the puncturing work for him. That's the piece's central craft move — let the documentary record and the executive's own words make the case, position the reporter as the assembler rather than the indictor.

The most analytically generative move is the framing of Brian Thompson's December 2024 assassination as a "9/11-type turning point" for corporate executive-protection. This is the kind of empirical sociological claim about how a single salient event reshaped a whole industry's risk modeling, and it's the rare American-political-economy frame that takes elite behavior change seriously as a measurable phenomenon. The Equilar trendlines (37.8% vs. 23.6% executive-security perks; median value up >100% over five years) substantiate this in a way that "the elite are scared of populism" assertions usually can't. Worth carrying into any synthesis section that touches the oligarchy/insulation theme — this piece is the quantitative version of the cultural claim that Stoller's Malone-as-mob piece makes in qualitative form.

The piece's weaknesses are minor. The Sec Exec Council report on attacks-on-executives at "highest level on record" is reported but the time-series methodology isn't transparent in Klippenstein's account — is the rate adjusted for the explosion in the number of public companies and executives, or is it raw count? The report cites "activism" as a leading driver but Klippenstein doesn't probe whether the council's threat-categorization criteria are meaningfully constructed or industry-friendly framing. A skeptical reader would note that the corporate-security industry has obvious incentive to inflate the threat picture and that an industry-body report is not an independent source. Klippenstein notes the council is "an industry body that serves Fortune 500 corporate security shops" — so the partiality is on the record — but doesn't draw the inference that the threat-trend numbers may be marketing.

The piece does not engage the stronger version of the opposing view: that executive protection has surged for the same reason that all private security has surged (general perception of social disorder, breakdown of state policing function, willingness to pay), and that the oil-and-gas-specific narrative may be a category-error overlay on a more diffuse trend. Edison International's jump from $94K to $4.5M is striking, but Edison's specific exposure is not "oil-and-gas" but California-utility-company-with-wildfire-litigation; the framing as a Iran-war-driven sectoral shift is partially imposed on data that may have multiple drivers.

What the analysis adds to the roster: a quantitative, document-backed report on a specific elite-behavior phenomenon (executive-security spending surge) that gives the broader anti-billionaire / oligarchy theme empirical traction it usually lacks. Pairs unusually cleanly with Stoller's Malone piece on the same theme (the mob-style corporate ethos of an earlier billionaire generation) and with deBoer's ratfucking piece (the establishment's defensive maneuvers when its position is threatened). The three pieces together form a coherent unit: Stoller on how the elite class was built; Klippenstein on what they're spending to defend themselves; deBoer on what they're willing to do to maintain political control. Worth grouping in a "Elite defensive maneuvers" theme.

↩ Back to TOC

The Coming Age of Digital Warfare

Fabio Vighi · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control freezing $344 million in Tether stablecoin tokens held in Iranian-linked wallets, plus the July 2025 GENIUS Act regulating stablecoins and Tether's $141 billion in US Treasury holdings.

Vighi reads the Tether freeze as a small but revealing dress rehearsal for a new architecture of financial coercion, where money no longer just records value but enforces behavior. He situates this within a longer Marxist account (drawing on the German "Wertkritik" theorist Robert Kurz) in which capitalist money has always been an "eerily dynamic zero" — abstract claims circulating with no intrinsic tie to social life — but where digital money completes that abstraction by making access conditional on authorization rather than possession. The GENIUS Act, by requiring stablecoins to be fully backed by short-term US Treasuries with compliance baked in, creates "privately issued money publicly anchored in state debt": a closed loop where stablecoins are claims on Treasuries and Treasuries are claims on future tax revenue, with no productive labor in the circuit. Vighi notes that Tether's $141 billion Treasury position exceeds Germany's or South Korea's holdings, making a nominally Salvadoran company a top-tier sovereign creditor. The Iran war is the first live-fire exercise: SWIFT was weaponized in 2022, dollar reserves immobilized, and now stablecoins — sold as the workaround — are freezable on demand before the GENIUS Act's ink has dried. The conclusion: a stablecoin backed by Treasuries is "a zero backed by a zero," and the attempt to stabilize digital money through surveillance accelerates the very collapse it fears.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece is doing two things at once, and they're separable: an empirical observation about stablecoins-as-sanctions-tools, and a Marxist-Hegelian metaphysics of money as "asocial form." The first claim survives without the second. Vighi's reading of the OFAC freeze, the GENIUS Act, and Tether's Treasury position is concrete, sourced, and lands. The observation that "every sovereign treasurer on earth now knows that holding USDT amounts to holding US Treasuries with an extra 'freezable on demand' feature bolted on top" is sharp — and consistent with the post-2022 sovereign-reserve-diversification story (yuan settlement of Russian energy, gold accumulation, BIS Project mBridge). The closed-loop diagnostic — stablecoins as claims on Treasuries which are claims on future revenue — is also analytically useful. This part of the essay would survive cleanly without any Marxist apparatus at all; it's a straight political-economy observation about how stablecoin compliance architecture turns private digital money into a sanctions instrument.

The Žižekian register — money as "eerily dynamic zero," Robert Kurz's Wertkritik school on labor substance, the "system of total administration now coding its own bankruptcy" closer — is where the analytical work gets thinner. Kurz's framework (the German Wertkritik tradition: capital as a self-referential value-form unmoored from production) is a real intellectual tradition, but Vighi imports it without explaining what work it does that a more conventional account (rentier capitalism, financialization, Hyman Minsky on financial instability) couldn't do. The "form without content" / "zero backed by a zero" formulation is rhetorically punchy but doesn't generate any predictions the empirical analysis hasn't already supplied. The frame also obscures a more interesting question: if money has been "abstract" since gold-as-claim-on-labor, what specifically about programmability (versus, say, fractional-reserve banking, or fiat after 1971) is the inflection point? Vighi gestures at "control shifts from possession to validation" — which is the actually-new thing — but the metaphysical register makes it harder to see, not easier.

The strongest critical objection from inside the piece's own logic: if the closed loop is self-referential and unsustainable, what's the off-ramp? Vighi predicts collapse but doesn't say what collapse looks like, who the agents of disruption are, or whether non-dollar alternatives (yuan stablecoins, gold-backed BRICS settlement, fully decentralized crypto) would solve the abstraction problem or just reproduce it under different authorization layers. The strongest defense: the piece is doing diagnostic rather than predictive work, and as a diagnosis of how OFAC + GENIUS + Tether's Treasury exposure converge into a programmable sanctions architecture, it lands. The Iran-specific framing — "the first live-fire exercise" — is the right altitude for the moment and connects this week's news to a multi-year structural story other writers (Tooze on dollar plumbing, Setser on Tether and Treasuries) have been tracking from different angles.

↩ Back to TOC

Made in the USA

Suzy Hansen · 2026-06-11 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Pete Hegseth's two books — In the Arena: Good Citizens, a Great Republic, and How One Speech Can Reinvigorate America (Threshold, 2016) and American Crusade: Our Fight to Stay Free (Center Street, 2020); the ongoing US-Israeli war against Iran; Trump-administration social-media content; Christopher Lasch's The Culture of Narcissism (invoked structurally).

Suzy Hansen — author of Notes on a Foreign Country and From Life Itself and a longtime Istanbul-based American observer — uses two of Hegseth's six books to argue that the Defense (now "War") Secretary is not an outlier but the distilled product of three American formations: the Reagan-Bush Cold War, the post-1989 "end of history" triumphalism, and the post-9/11 "war on terror" with its Iraq-era doctrine of "maximum lethality" applied to insurgents. The biographical material — Forest Lake Minnesota Baptist upbringing, Princeton (where Hegseth ran a right-wing campus paper), Guantánamo and Iraq deployments, multiple divorces, the 2020 confidential settlement of a sexual-assault allegation for $50,000, documented alcohol abuse, Fox News tenure, and the firing of twenty-four senior officers including the Joint Chiefs chair after his Pentagon confirmation — is presented not as a scandal portfolio but as a typology: Hegseth as the recognizable American archetype Hansen calls "the American man," whose ideology fuses militant Christian nationalism (he calls for "overwhelming violence of action against those who deserve no mercy" in a Pentagon prayer service, compares Trump to Jesus, journalists to Pharisees), gendered grievance, and weapons-fetishism. The essay's structural target is broader than Hegseth: Hansen argues the Democratic establishment never properly renounced the Iraq War or the worldview that produced it, and the foreign-policy elite continues to hide behind "banalities about America's resilient institutions and exalted position in the international order" while other countries' diplomats now describe the US in print as a senile old person needing to be helped across the street (she quotes Omani foreign minister Badr Albusaidi in The Economist). The piece closes by aligning Hegseth with the Stallone-puppet "We Fuck the World" satirical refrain from the French Les Guignols de l'info Bush-era skits, and arguing that the renunciation required is not of Hegseth-the-individual but of the American foreign-policy id that produced him and that the Democratic Party has not credibly distanced itself from.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece is doing what the regular roundup roster — which is largely Anglophone-American and largely organized around the post-2024 Democratic-coalition diagnostic question — almost never does: read the United States from the perspective of someone who lived for years in a country (Turkey) that the US foreign-policy class views as a problem to be managed, and apply the lens she developed on Erdoğan back to her own country's leadership. The "renunciation" frame is the strongest analytical move. Hansen's Turkish-academic source uses it to explain why Erdoğan-voters in the 2010s could not abandon him without abandoning themselves; Hansen recycles it to explain why the American Iraq-War coalition (including the Democratic foreign-policy establishment) could not, after 2008, properly renounce the war without renouncing the institutional careers, intellectual frameworks, and self-conception that supported it. The argument lands because Hansen has spent two decades doing this kind of cultural-political reading from outside the United States and is not performing it.

The Hegseth-as-product-not-aberration claim survives scrutiny better than the standard liberal "Trump is unprecedented" frame. Hansen's reading of Hegseth's Minnesota Baptist upbringing, Princeton conservative apprenticeship, Iraq deployment, and Christian-nationalist church affiliation as continuous with the Bush-era Republican formation — not a populist insurgent breaking with that formation — is correct as a matter of biographical fact. The Pentagon prayer-service citation alone ("overwhelming violence of action against those who deserve no mercy …. We ask this with bold confidence in the mighty and powerful name of Jesus Christ") is doing more analytic work than most "Christian nationalism" essays manage with five thousand words of theoretical apparatus.

The strongest version of the opposing view — which Hansen partially anticipates — is that the "Hegseth is just America" frame elides real discontinuities. The post-9/11 Bush administration did not, in fact, fire the Joint Chiefs chair, openly target black and women officers, or splice action-movie footage with real bombings into administration-produced "snuff films" (Hansen's phrase, borrowed from Lasch's idiom). The Trump-era variation is not merely an intensification but a regime change in how the executive narrates and visualizes lethal force, and treating Hegseth as continuous with Rumsfeld risks understating what is structurally novel. Hansen knows this — the Lasch invocation is precisely meant to flag the discontinuity ("something even the social critic Christopher Lasch could not have imagined") — but the renunciation-frame's analytical neatness depends on a continuity story that the essay's own evidence (twenty-four officer firings, the Pentagon prayer service, the action-movie video splicing) partially undercuts. The unifying judgment Hansen wants is: the cultural-political substrate is continuous, the institutional consequences are discontinuous, and the latter is enabled by the former's absence of renunciation. That is the right unifying judgment but the essay doesn't quite articulate it in those terms.

The piece is also a genre object worth naming. The NYRB-essay format — long review-essay built around two books but using them as scaffolding for a much broader cultural diagnosis — is operating here at full strength, with Hansen using Hegseth's own ghostwritten prose as raw material the way an anthropologist would use fieldwork transcripts. The Suzy Hansen byline carries authority earned through Notes on a Foreign Country (2017), where she did the same operation on American foreign policy from Istanbul. Readers unfamiliar with that book will read this essay as a strong polemic; readers who know the prior work will read it as the latest installment in a sustained twenty-year project of reading the United States from outside its own self-image. The discovery layer's job is partly to surface exactly this kind of writer-with-a-long-arc to a roundup whose roster is dominated by faster-cadence weekly columnists; Hansen is a candidate worth tracking for future surfacings, and Notes on a Foreign Country's third edition (referenced in the byline bio) provides a natural pretext for re-engagement.

↩ Back to TOC

Welcome to the Postmodern Presidency

Damon Linker · 2026-06-03 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The conduct of the 2026 Iran War — Trump's improvised entry alongside Netanyahu, the absence of any congressional case, the shifting Truth Social rationales from Trump, Hegseth, and Rubio, and the manifest scramble toward a face-saving declaration of victory — read as the consummating example of a longer postmodern drift in American politics.

Linker reaches back to his 1990s graduate-school education to retrieve the postmodernism debate: Derrida, Foucault, Lyotard, and Baudrillard taught the left to treat "grand narratives" as concealed power-grabs, while Allan Bloom on the center-right warned in The Closing of the American Mind that this would dethrone universal truth and leave politics as raw will. Linker disclaims any direct lineage from French theory to Trump, but argues the political world has converged on the postmodernists' picture anyway: nationalism has replaced cosmopolitan universalism abroad, partisan tribalism has replaced neutral public administration at home, and Trump personally embodies the postmodern thesis that truth is a tool for manipulation rather than something to be discovered and acted on. The Iran War crystallizes this: Trump made no argument, articulated no coherent goal, and is now bombarding the public with contradictory rationales — Baudrillard's "the Gulf War did not take place" updated to a regime that doesn't even bother constructing a coherent narrative. Linker closes by suggesting that escape requires re-affirming some form of universalism, perhaps in the modest Kantian sense of a regulative principle, lest politics collapse entirely into "ruthless assertions of power."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The frame is intellectually elegant and the Iran War example is doing more work than the rest of the piece — Linker correctly notices that the Trump administration's information environment has crossed a threshold from "spin" to something that requires a different vocabulary. The Baudrillard reference works because Linker handles it with care: he correctly recovers what Baudrillard was pointing at in 1991 (war-as-curated-spectacle) and then says the current condition has gone further — the regime no longer bothers to curate. That's a real observation about the difference between Iraq War 2003 information operations and Iran War 2026 information operations. The Hegseth / Rubio / Truth Social trio churning out contradictory rationales is empirically what's happening; naming it as "fully postmodern propaganda" gives it a vocabulary.

The structural problem is that the postmodernism frame is doing both more and less than Linker admits. More, because once you've named Trump as postmodern, every subsequent move — disregarding norms, lying, treating institutions as masks for power — gets retroactively absorbed into the frame, which is not really a falsifiable diagnosis but a vocabulary. Less, because the explanation for why America landed here is just hand-waved: "the political events of the past decade have vindicated the critics of postmodernism." That's not a causal account; it's a rhetorical convergence. Linker correctly disclaims that French theorists "conjured" the present, but he doesn't then offer the alternative mechanism — was it social media, was it the 2008 financial crisis collapsing the legitimacy of expert institutions, was it the post-1989 unipolar moment running out of road? Without that, "postmodern presidency" is a label, not an analysis.

The other tension is internal. Linker wants postmodernism to be both (a) a left-academic project that critiqued universalism and (b) the operating ideology of the contemporary right. The bridge he proposes — that both share the view that there's no neutral truth and politics is everywhere — is real but underspecified. Allan Bloom's "Nietzscheanization of the left" warning, which Linker invokes as vindicated, was specifically about the left embracing relativism. What we got instead was the right embracing a stripped-down version of the same anti-universalism. That's a much more interesting structural finding than Linker's "the critics were right" framing acknowledges, because it means the diagnostic vocabulary needs to handle the transmission — how anti-universalism crossed the political spectrum and came to power on the right rather than the left it was supposed to liberate. Linker is too close to the original Bloom polemic to fully see this.

The closing turn to Kantian regulative principles is intellectually serious but pragmatically thin. Saying "we need to affirm some form of universalism" doesn't tell anyone how to do that, and Linker knows it — he hedges with "regardless of whether that would catch on." The essay is more useful as a diagnosis (the Iran War example particularly) than as a prescription.

Trial-source eval: This piece would survive on the roster — Linker brings a register the roster currently lacks (mid-20th-century political-theory frame applied to the present), and the Iran-War-as-postmodern-spectacle observation is sharper than the standard "Trump lies a lot" critique. The risk is the same as Fukuyama's: if every Linker piece is "X moment in Trump-2 illuminated by Y mid-century theorist," it becomes predictable. Worth trial-tracking for the same reason.

↩ Back to TOC

Ian Bremmer on the Risks America Poses to the World: The Ezra Klein Show

Ezra Klein · 2026-06-02 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the past month's two dominant foreign-policy stories — the U.S. war with Iran (Crosscurrents 5/30 had heavy coverage) and the Trump-Xi summit — which Klein opens by arguing have revealed "absolute incoherence" in American foreign policy, with no clear sense of what either action is meant to achieve.

In a long interview with Eurasia Group founder Ian Bremmer, Klein presses the question of what Trump represents structurally. Bremmer's frame is that Trump is "first and foremost a symptom, not a cause" of long-running American grievances (skepticism of free trade, foreign wars, open borders) that Obama and Biden also had to navigate — but that Trump then becomes a cause through a deliberate political revolution Bremmer compares to Franklin D. Roosevelt's in scale, except aimed at destroying the administrative state and post-war global order rather than building them. The two then debate the "good America vs. bad vibes" puzzle: even after a long period of strong macro performance (Mississippi per-capita income exceeds Europe; bottom-half income growth; A.I. leadership), consumer sentiment registers below Great Recession lows. Bremmer attributes this to four overlapping factors: (1) a frayer safety net than peer democracies even after recent improvements, (2) collapsing class mobility (U.S. fell from top of OECD in the 1970s-80s to far below European peers today), (3) grievance-based political incentives where leaders on left and right (Mamdani-at-Ken-Griffin's-building as a parallel to Trump) demonize wealth rather than diagnose structural decline, and (4) the disengaged stewardship of the richest Americans (Musk, Bezos, Zuckerberg) compared to the postwar industrial elite. The conversation's analytic payoff is in the framework Bremmer offers in place of the Thucydides trap (the Xi-favored "rising power challenges declining hegemon → war" model): the Gracchi trap, from the late Roman Republic, in which internal grievance-driven political revolutions failed serially while permanently weakening institutional norms and convincing allies the system was undependable, ultimately collapsing the Republic from the inside. Bremmer's bottom line — and the title's claim — is that the U.S., not China, is the principal driver of geopolitical risk today, through unilateral withdrawal from alliances (Taiwan, Ukraine, Japan/South Korea), abandonment of the free-trade architecture, and the destabilization of the world's largest market via tariffs and industrial policy. China, in this telling, is not building an alternative architecture; it is simply becoming the dominant influencer of the institutions (UN, WHO) the U.S. is vacating.

[NOTE: This archive contains the opening third and concluding section of the 87,708-character transcript; an extraction constraint omitted the middle section. The Gracchi-trap framework that the title and summary lean on is present verbatim. Full transcript at the canonical URL.]

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The Gracchi-trap framework is the analytic payload of this episode and the reason it earns more weight in the roundup than a typical Klein interview. The frame is doing real explanatory work: it solves the puzzle of why a country with strong macro outputs and clear hegemonic structural position is experiencing a self-inflicted retreat. The Thucydides-trap model (which Xi pushed in the summit and which dominates D.C. China-hawk discourse) misdiagnoses the situation as external — a rising power challenging a declining one — when the actual story is internal political dysfunction weakening institutional norms in ways that compound over multiple administrations. Bremmer's tell that it is the right frame: even when Trump's specific revolutionary acts fail (court-packing equivalents, alliance-breaking that gets walked back), the precedent that they were attempted at all gives subsequent leaders permission, and the allies who watched the attempt no longer treat the U.S. as a reliable partner. That is the Gracchi pattern. Whether it is the right historical analogy is debatable — the late Roman Republic also faced massive structural pressures (Marian military reforms, the social wars, the cost of overextended provincial administration) that the Gracchi-grievance frame undersells — but it is at minimum a useful corrective to the Thucydides framing's exteriorization of blame.

The piece's structural weakness is the same one Klein's interviews often have: the host's framing and the guest's framing reinforce each other without sufficient pushback. Klein clearly believes the "American macro is fine but vibes are terrible" puzzle is real and is reaching for the structural-political-economy diagnosis that lets him stay in his abundance-liberal frame while accepting populist energy as legitimate; Bremmer is offering exactly the package Klein wants. The two of them do not steel-man the counter-position, which would be that American macro performance is much weaker than the headline numbers suggest once you correct for housing-cost increases, healthcare costs, the differential between consumer-good price levels (PCE) and the basket of services people actually need to buy (childcare, college, healthcare, housing), and the post-2020 hollowing of public-service capacity. On that view, "vibes" track reality and the puzzle dissolves. The interview's Mississippi-per-capita-income datapoint is the canonical case: it survives because the speakers do not press on what Mississippi-priced-services actually buys in lived experience versus the Netherlands. Bremmer himself gestures at this with the social-contract point but doesn't ground it in numbers.

A second analytical move worth flagging: the Mamdani-at-Ken-Griffin's-building example is doing a lot of work in Bremmer's argument that grievance politics is bipartisan. The example is illuminating but Bremmer's commentary on it — that Mamdani is misdirecting blame at one of the better New York billionaires — is exactly the kind of structural-power blind spot that the framework otherwise diagnoses elsewhere. Whether Griffin is personally more philanthropic than Bezos or Musk is not the point Mamdani is making; the point is about the rule-set, not the individual. Bremmer's treatment of Mamdani as a left-Trump parallel is plausible at a structural level but undercut by his lack of engagement with the actual policy substance Mamdani is running on.

The third move — the four-billionaire stewardship critique (Musk + Bezos + Zuckerberg, with Griffin implicitly excluded) — is the most heterodox part of the conversation. Bremmer's claim that the postwar U.S. industrial elite saw themselves as stewards of national institutions and the current tech elite does not is empirical and (largely) true. It also lands harder coming from Bremmer, whose Eurasia-Group business depends on access to exactly this class. But the steel-man counter is that the postwar elite was operating in an oligopolistic-corporatist regulatory environment that channeled their incentives toward stewardship; the current elite is operating in a winner-take-most platform-economy environment that doesn't. The diagnosis "they don't have stewardship values" tells less than the diagnosis "the economic structure doesn't reward stewardship," and Bremmer occasionally elides the difference.

Where the conversation is strongest is at the foreign-policy concrete level: Bremmer's observation that China is not building an alternative architecture but simply absorbing the dominant influencer position over the institutions the U.S. is vacating (UN, WHO) is exactly the kind of practitioner-level point that pure-theory China-hawk discourse misses. That's the analytic anchor that earns this episode its weight in the roundup.

↩ Back to TOC

Anna Paulina Luna Wants Everything Disclosed: interesting times

Ross Douthat · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: original commentary on the emergence of "disclosure populism" within MAGA conservatism as a unifying coalitional energy — illustrated through Rep. Luna's specific reform agenda and her chairmanship of the House Task Force on Declassification of Federal Secrets.

Douthat opens with his thesis-claim that a new trend within populist conservatism — the conviction that government is keeping secrets and protecting the powerful — has emerged in the deep institutional-distrust era, and presents Luna as an exemplar. Luna (a Florida Republican congresswoman, military veteran, twice endorsed by Trump) describes her politics as "conservative with a populist streak" and her agenda as 80/20 issues like banning congressional insider trading, voter ID, sexual-harassment ethics enforcement against both parties' members, and pre-MAHA work on food additives. The interview's structural payoff is the framing Douthat applies and Luna confirms: that there is no internal tension between MAGA loyalty and an aggressive disclosure ethic that targets the security state and Republican leadership as readily as Democrats. Luna walks through her tactical methods (using committee hearings to play footage that has been classified, building bipartisan coalitions for the U.A.P. Disclosure Act and whistleblower protections), and the conversation surfaces a recurring frustration: presidents from Obama through Trump have signed executive orders pushing for JFK-record release with limited results because of "entrenched … bureaucracy of people that don't want this information to be released." Douthat presses on whether by the end of Trump's second term someone could give him a definitive answer on UAP secret programs; Luna's response — "I would hope" — implicitly concedes the entrenched-bureaucracy problem will likely outlast the administration's tools. Douthat closes by noting that the coalition crossing this issue includes Obama, Schumer, Luna, Hillary Clinton, and Trump — a "from-all-sides" coalition that he reads as evidence the disclosure energy is structurally non-partisan even when its political language is MAGA-coded. The episode functions as part of Douthat's running argument that the operative political division of the late-Trump era is not left/right but inside/outside the institutional establishment, with the disclosure ethic as the cross-coalitional populist energy.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Douthat using the interview format to argue something he could not credibly write in a column — that disclosure populism is a coherent and serious political project rather than a conspiracy-theory grab bag — by letting Luna's tone do the persuasive work. The framing is doing real analytical work: Luna is not Marjorie Taylor Greene, and the policy-substance side of her agenda (insider-trading bans, voter ID, food-additive regulation, sexual-harassment ethics enforced bipartisanly) is in fact the 80/20-issue terrain Democrats lost in 2024 by refusing to engage. Douthat is asking the reader to take seriously that the disclosure-populism coalition — which extends across the Hillary-Clinton-to-Trump spectrum, including the JFK-records advocacy of left-of-center figures — is structurally not the same as the conspiracy-theory base, even when its rhetorical surface looks similar. That is a real heterodoxy within mainstream center-right discourse, where the standard move is to lump Luna's disclosure ethic with Q-anon-adjacent grifting and dismiss both.

That said, the interview has a structural problem characteristic of Douthat's podcast: the steel-manning runs in only one direction. He flags his own UFO-curiosity-but-uncertainty at the top — "I personally am convinced that something is being kept secret. I'm just damned if I can figure out what it is" — which lets him interview Luna as a curious investigator rather than as an adversarial check. The result is that Luna's claims about specific cover-ups (the Epstein files, what's being withheld on JFK, the U.A.P. footage withheld from her committee) are not pressed for evidence. The harder questions are not asked: which specific Epstein-file content does Luna believe has been withheld and on what evidentiary basis; which sex-trafficking-network claims that came out of the disclosure discourse turned out to be sourced to Q-anon-tier material and which had documentary backing; whether the U.A.P. disclosure framework she is pushing distinguishes (operationally) between footage that legitimately raises questions about U.S. military aviation and footage that has had alternative explanations published in Scientific American-tier outlets. The interview format treats all of these on the same plane.

A second analytical move worth flagging: Douthat's "across-the-aisle disclosure coalition" framing (Obama, Schumer, Hillary Clinton, Luna, Trump) does work that doesn't fully survive scrutiny. The four left-coded figures named have all made specific, narrow claims (Obama on UAPs in podcast appearances; Schumer on Section 504 of the U.A.P. amendment in 2023; Clinton on UFOs as a stated 2016 campaign-trail interest; Biden on JFK records) that are not the same thing as a coalition for broad government-secrecy disclosure across security-state files. The conflation matters because Luna's project is much broader: she is arguing for a systemic dismantling of the secrecy bureaucracy, not for selective declassifications on three named topics. Treating these as one coalition makes the project look bigger and bipartisan-er than it is. That doesn't disqualify the project, but it requires more rigorous mapping than the interview provides.

The most analytically interesting implicit move in the episode is Douthat's positioning of disclosure populism as the unifying late-Trump-era energy. If true, it suggests the operative axis in American politics is not left/right or progressive/conservative but inside/outside the institutional security and administrative-state establishment — which would explain Luna's specific bipartisan coalition-building (Hillary's UFO interest, the Schumer-Rounds U.A.P. amendment, the Klippenstein-coded left-investigator network that comes from a different ideological direction but the same suspicion-of-secrecy ethic). This is consistent with the Crosscurrents project's running observation about Klippenstein-IRS / Klein-Madoff unexpected-aligned-pole convergences. The episode does not name this analytic move explicitly — Douthat is letting it emerge through the interview — but the implicit argument is that the late-Trump-era political reorganization is around this axis, and the disclosure ethic is its first stable cross-coalitional expression.

The piece's other underargued claim is that this is a populist coalition rather than an elite-faction coalition. The figures named (Obama, Schumer, Luna, Clinton, Trump) are not populist; they are political principals. The base-level disclosure energy that animates the discourse is real, but the coalition that actually moves disclosure legislation is intra-elite. The interview elides this distinction, which matters because the project's political theory depends on it being a populist project — if it's a faction-elite project, its political-economic implications are very different. Whether Douthat means to elide it or whether the interview just runs out of room before that question gets addressed is unclear; but the next step in his argument needs to confront it.

↩ Back to TOC

UN Security Council: Germany left empty-handed

Euro|topics (curated debate) · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The June 4, 2026 UN General Assembly vote in which Germany received only 104 votes (vs. 134 for Portugal and 131 for Austria) for two non-permanent UN Security Council seats reserved for the Western European and Others Group for 2027-28 — Germany's first such defeat in a routine eight-year-cycle bid.

Euro|topics curates five readings of the loss across the Swiss, German, Austrian, and Latvian press, arranging them along a clear axis of explanation. The Swiss Aargauer Zeitung and Latvian Diena treat the result as substantively meaningful — a Global-South rebuke of Berlin's relatively muted criticism of Israel's conduct in Gaza, and more broadly of a German foreign policy "inextricably linked" to a Western-dominance agenda the non-Western majority increasingly rejects. Germany's Handelsblatt converges on the same diagnosis from inside the country, framing the loss as exposing Germany's habitual overestimation of its international clout despite being among the UN's largest financial contributors. Germany's Die Welt offers the counter-reading: the defeat is no shame because the UN itself has become an anti-Western, anti-Israel, anti-democratic body whose votes no longer carry moral weight. Austria's Salzburger Nachrichten reframes the result as a positive vote for Austria's fifteen-year campaign emphasizing small-state representation rather than as a negative vote against Germany. The selection's center of gravity tilts toward the Global-South-rebuke reading: three of five quoted papers endorse some version of it, with only Die Welt offering the system-illegitimacy escape hatch.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The interesting question about a Euro|topics digest is rarely the individual quotes — they are competently chosen and faithfully excerpted — but the editorial selection and the framing implicit in what the digest treats as the live debate. Here Euro|topics has constructed a debate where the live argument is why Germany lost, with four of five papers operating inside a frame where the loss is a real signal about Germany's standing rather than noise from a debased institution. Die Welt's "the UN is illegitimate" position is included but isolated; the surrounding quotes effectively treat it as the cope rather than the diagnosis. That curatorial choice is itself a stance — and one that an American reader steeped in the Free Press / Commentary / Compact reading of UN votes (the UN is anti-Israel and so its rebukes don't count) would do well to register as the European mainstream's working interpretation. The selection treats "the Global South has aligned consequences with its rhetorical positions on Gaza" as a structural finding about post-2024 multilateralism, not a one-off.

The non-American framing also surfaces something the US roster's discussions of Israel/Gaza tend to elide: the cost, in concrete diplomatic capital, of the German position. American debates about Israel typically frame the question as moral (is the conduct defensible?) or domestic-political (what does this do to the Democratic coalition?). The European mainstream press, in this curation, is asking a third question — what does it cost to be perceived as Israel's restrained-criticism wing in a UN body whose median voter is now the Global South? Handelsblatt's framing — Germany "of all countries, which likes to lecture the rest of the world on how there is no alternative to liberal Western democracies" — is a particularly sharp version of the materialist objection: the lecture-and-pay model of multilateral engagement that worked when the Western bloc was the median has stopped working, and Germany's continued performance of it is now visibly counterproductive. That's a structural argument about the changed median of multilateral institutions that the project's American sources rarely surface (Tooze comes closest, but at a more macro level).

One thing the digest does not surface is the obvious counter-question: if Germany lost because of its Israel position, why did Portugal — also an EU member, also broadly aligned with the Western bloc — win? Salzburger Nachrichten gestures at it (Austria's small-state framing) but the digest doesn't push the question of whether the "Global-South verdict on Berlin's Israel stance" reading is over-determined, or whether Portugal's relatively critical-of-Israel positioning is what's actually doing the explanatory work. A sharper editorial would have included a quote from a paper challenging the Israel-blame thesis on these grounds; Euro|topics here lets the consensus solidify without that pressure test. The Latvian Diena quote is doing the lifting on "this is structural, not contingent" — but its argument is essentially that the loss confirms a thesis already held rather than that the loss is evidence for the thesis as against alternatives. That's a real gap in the digest's evidentiary structure, even granting that the operative reading is probably correct.

↩ Back to TOC

Institutional capacity audit: judges, voting, bridges, defense

The Supreme Court Has Invented a Right to Discriminate

Adam Serwer · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Supreme Court's emergency-docket ruling allowing Alabama to proceed with a congressional map that a federal district panel (including two Trump appointees) had already found intentionally racially discriminatory, after Alabama defied the Court's own 2023 ruling reaffirming Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act (VRA).

Serwer reads the new Alabama order in light of the Court's April decision in Louisiana v. Callais — where Justice Alito held that "race and politics are so intertwined" that the Fifteenth Amendment's bar on racial discrimination in voting almost never actually applies — and argues the two together constitute a constitutional inversion. Callais was nominally a statutory case about Section 2 of the VRA; the Alabama order extends it to a case where intentional discrimination had already been found by a lower court, on the theory that partisan motive launders racial motive. The result: a state legislature can openly defy a federal injunction, the Court will reward it, and any plaintiff challenging a map must produce an alternative map that achieves the same partisan outcome — putting the burden of districting compliance on the victims of discrimination. Serwer traces this back to Chief Justice Roberts's career-long opposition (going back to his Reagan-era DOJ work) to the VRA's effect-or-purpose test, and argues that the Court's logic would have validated poll taxes, literacy tests, and grandfather clauses had it been the operative standard during Reconstruction. He grounds the constitutional argument historically — the Fifteenth Amendment was passed precisely because white-supremacist Democrats were using partisan disenfranchisement against Black Republicans, so a partisan-pretext exception nullifies the amendment's original purpose entirely.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Serwer is doing the legal/constitutional analysis the roster doesn't do — and doing it on the strongest empirical footing available, which is the actual record of intent in the Alabama case (a district-court panel including two Trump appointees finding intentional racial discrimination), the prior holdings the new ruling deviates from, and the Reconstruction-era history the majority's logic would have validated. This is the structural mirror image of McCarthy's National Review column this week: Serwer (center-left, Atlantic staff writer, longtime Roberts Court critic) makes the case that the Court has reinvented Jim Crow voting jurisprudence under a partisan-pretext veil. McCarthy (center-right, NR contributing editor) makes the case that the DOJ is running selective enforcement against political enemies while still pursuing real misconduct. Both are operating in a register the roster mostly doesn't reach — the actual mechanics of how legal institutions are processing the second Trump administration — and the unexpected-aligned-poles convergence is: legal-process actors on both sides are losing confidence in selective application of neutral rules.

What's strong: the historical anchor. Serwer's read of the Fifteenth Amendment's drafting context — that Reconstruction Republicans passed it precisely because partisan and racial disenfranchisement were the same thing (Confederate Democrats targeting Black Republicans), so reading "partisanship" as a Fifteenth Amendment shield nullifies the amendment — is the column's strongest argument and it is genuinely hard to answer. The Brennan Center quote from Kareem Crayton (a voting-rights specialist) gives the column a concrete authority claim that the abstract argument needs ("Out of all the cases you can talk about during the civil-rights movement... none of them that I know of had been at the Supreme Court where the Supreme Court says, It's okay if you just choose to defy a federal order"). The argument that the majority's alternative-map requirement places the compliance burden on the victims is mechanically correct and rarely stated as plainly.

What's missing: the strongest counter from the conservative legal side. The Roberts Court's defenders would argue that Shelby County v. Holder (2013) already removed Section 5 preclearance, that Allen v. Milligan (2023) was an outlier reaffirmation that the Court is now properly walking back, and that the underlying problem is that any race-conscious districting standard requires the Court to take race into account, which the Fourteenth Amendment's color-blind reading bars. Serwer engages the partisanship-as-pretext argument but doesn't engage the color-blind-Constitution reading head-on, which is the position the Court's defenders would actually take rather than the "partisanship launders racism" position Serwer attributes to them. A sharper version of the column would distinguish "Alito is endorsing partisan-pretext discrimination" from "Alito is endorsing a color-blind reading whose effect happens to permit partisan-pretext discrimination" — different framings, different responses.

What's also worth flagging: this is the operative Roberts Court ruling of the week and almost certainly the most consequential single piece of news in the roundup that the roster missed. The next congressional districting cycle will run under this rule. Atlantic's legal coverage — Serwer in particular — is one of the few channels through which the project's reader can track this; the roundup should treat the discovery layer as the primary mechanism for catching it, given the gap.

↩ Back to TOC

How the Trump DOJ Extracted a Guilty Plea from John Bolton

Andrew C. McCarthy · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Reports that John Bolton has agreed to plead guilty to a single felony count of illegal retention of classified information, in connection with notes used to assemble his 2020 anti-Trump memoir The Room Where It Happened.

McCarthy, a former federal prosecutor and longtime National Review legal columnist, argues the Bolton plea must be read on two registers simultaneously. On one hand, the Trump DOJ's pursuit of Bolton is plainly part of a "lawfare campaign" against the president's political enemies — the government dragged out the original clearance review for political reasons, and the felony charge is harsher than the misdemeanor plea David Petraeus received under Obama for transmitting more sensitive material. On the other hand, the underlying facts — Bolton typing 1,000+ pages of classified notes into Word documents, transmitting them to family members through unauthorized channels, and basing the memoir on them — describe real misconduct that the DOJ has concrete evidence for, and the post-clearance-review excisions confirm the intelligence had left government safekeeping even when it didn't make it into the published book. McCarthy contrasts this with Bolton's broader record (a National Review contributor, target of an IRGC assassination plot, decades of service) and argues the prosecution is qualitatively different from Trump's other politically motivated cases because the evidentiary basis is real. He ends asking the sentencing judge to weigh "the full ledger" rather than imposing the maximum.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:
