McCarthy is doing something most center-right outlets do not do this week: he is conceding that Trump's DOJ is running an enemies list while simultaneously not letting Bolton off the hook on the merits. That is the load-bearing move in the column, and it's worth taking seriously because the symmetric move — "yes the Mar-a-Lago documents case had real evidence, AND it was selectively prosecuted" — was almost never voiced from the same publications during the Biden years. The column is, in effect, a stress test of whether NR's legal coverage can carry "selective enforcement of a real law is still selective enforcement, but it also doesn't make the underlying conduct fictitious" as a stable position, applied to allies as well as antagonists. McCarthy passes that test — barely — but the Petraeus comparison reads as the operative core of the piece, and he leaves the harshness disparity sitting there without explicitly calling it what it is (a political-favoritism floor that the misdemeanor-vs-felony gap exposes).

What's strong: the specifics. McCarthy is a former US Attorney for the Southern District of New York, and the column does what almost nobody else in the roundup is doing — explaining the actual statutory and procedural mechanics that produce a $2.25 million potential fine (Section 3571 doubled-pecuniary-gain rule, leveraging 800,000 first-week book sales as the profit basis); the 18-count indictment dropping to a one-count plea; the cleared-but-excised pattern as the actual evidentiary spine of the case. None of the roster's writers can do this — neither the Yglesias-Silver wonk axis, the deBoer-Bruenig left axis, nor even Klippenstein's investigative beat treats federal prosecutorial mechanics as a primary subject. McCarthy is filling a real gap.

What's missing: the column ducks its own implication. If the Petraeus comparison is right, and if the Trump administration's clearance-review delays were politicized, then the operative charging decision is itself selective — and the right response is not just "the judge should weigh the full ledger at sentencing" but "the charging-decision standard the DOJ applied here is not the one it would apply to a Trump ally with identical facts." McCarthy gestures at this ("lawfare and misconduct are not mutually exclusive") but doesn't commit to it. The "sad news" framing — Bolton has appeared in NR's pages, was the target of an IRGC plot, served the country honorably — also functions to soften the institutional critique into a personal one: this is bad because Bolton is a friend, not because the prosecutorial standard is corrupt. A sharper version of this column would say the second thing directly. The piece's value to the roundup is the legal-mechanics gap-fill plus the fact that a center-right ex-prosecutor is willing to call this lawfare in writing, even with the softening.

The strongest pushback from the other direction: McCarthy never engages with the strongest defense of the prosecution, which is that classified-information cases really do turn on whether the person sent the documents to people who weren't cleared to see them, and that the "family members via unauthorized channels" allegation, if true, is exactly the conduct the statute exists to punish — irrespective of whether the prosecutor is acting in bad faith elsewhere. Granting that, the right standard is whether Hillary Clinton's similar conduct (the comparison McCarthy himself raises) would now also be chargeable under Trump-DOJ practice. McCarthy implies yes by listing her and Biden alongside Trump in the unprosecuted-comparator set, but doesn't follow through to say the consistent application of his own standard would mean reopening those cases — which is the actual policy implication and the place the column would land if it pushed through.

↩ Back to TOC

Why We Need Judicial Independence

Francis Fukuyama · 2026-06-06 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Trump's April 1, 2026 public attack on Republican-appointed judges, his in-person appearance at the Supreme Court hearings on his birthright-citizenship executive order, and the recent wave of lower-court injunctions against his Kennedy Center renaming, the $1.8 billion January 6 "slush fund," and the Kilmar Abrego Garcia prosecution.

Fukuyama uses Trump's complaints about "stupid" independent judges as a hook to deliver a compressed institutional history of judicial independence. He traces it to the 11th-century Investiture Controversy (Pope Gregory VII vs. Henry IV) and the 1122 Concordat of Worms, which left the Catholic Church — guardian of the rediscovered Justinian Code — controlling its own appointments and thus the law independent of secular rulers. From there he runs the Henry VIII / Star Chamber / English Civil War / 1688-89 Glorious Revolution sequence, ending with the principle that monarchs sit below the law, not above it. The substantive payoff: societies that fused legal and executive authority — China, Persia, Byzantium, what Fukuyama calls "Caesaropapism" — could not develop modern property and contract regimes, while the Anglo-American common-law tradition with stare decisis did. Trump's view that election authorizes judicial control therefore rejects not just an American norm but the institutional architecture of Western economic modernity itself.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a Fukuyama 101 essay — institutionalist big-history applied to the political moment — and it does what it does competently without breaking new ground. The Investiture-Controversy-to-Glorious-Revolution arc is straight out of The Origins of Political Order; readers familiar with his book series will recognize the entire frame, the same Caesaropapism comparison, the same "rule of law vs. rule by law" distinction. What's worth noting is the choice to compress that arc into 1,300 words and aim it at a 2026 audience that may not have read the books. As pedagogy, the essay earns its space; as analysis, it adds little to what Fukuyama has been saying about Trumpism since 2016.

The structural weakness is at the level of the causal claim, not the historical one. Fukuyama asserts that judicial independence "made possible the modern economic world" and that Caesaropapist regimes could not develop modern property regimes — but the modern Chinese economy is a substantial counter-example. China has produced 40 years of compounded growth without judicial independence in the Fukuyama sense; whatever you think of the durability of that growth, the claim that property-rights enforcement requires independent judges (rather than reliable, even-handed enforcement that can in principle issue from a unified party-state) is contested by the contemporary record. The essay would be stronger if it engaged that case — even briefly — rather than treating the 19th-century-economic-history finding as a settled matter. As written, the central comparative claim is asserted, not argued.

There's also a paratextual move worth flagging. The essay's authority depends partly on Fukuyama's standing as the author of Origins and Political Order and Political Decay. A reader who hasn't read those books gets the historical sweep without the qualifications and caveats Fukuyama himself attaches to the story in the long form. That isn't dishonest — column-length history is always compressed — but it does mean the piece functions more as institutional-decay alarm-sounding than as fresh argument. For the roundup's institutional-decay thread (Aurelien, Tooze, Klein's institutional-capture pieces), Fukuyama's strength is that he names a specific positive institution — independent judiciary — rather than gesturing at "norms" or "democracy" in general. That's a useful narrowing.

Trial-source eval: This piece would survive on the regular roster — Fukuyama provides a specific institutionalist register (judicial-independence-as-civilizational-precondition) that no current roster writer matches. Klein does institutional-capture; Aurelien does state-capacity decay; nobody is doing the long-arc constitutional-history register from a recognized political-science authority. The risk is that future Fukuyama pieces in this venue will all sound like this one — institutional history applied to whatever Trump did this week — which would become predictable fast. Worth tracking for two more weeks before promoting.

↩ Back to TOC

How Long Does It Take to Plan a Bridge?

Brian Potter · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the recurring "we used to build bridges fast" discourse (Golden Gate in four years, Empire State in one) that compares construction-only timelines from the past with planning-included timelines today. Potter argues this is a like-for-like failure — the Golden Gate Bridge took four years to build but had been in planning since 1921, twelve years before construction.

Potter assembled a dataset of 67 major US bridges built since ~1900, recording the year planning began, year construction began, and year of service opening. He defines "planning began" as the first official proposal or study by a transit-planning organization (acknowledging this date is harder to pin down than construction milestones; the data should be taken with a grain of salt). Three main findings emerge. First, on planning: 1900-1919 averaged 12+ years of planning; the 1960s-70s low was ~6 years; the 1980s-1999 period rose back to ~10 years, and post-2000 looks shorter (6.2 years) but is downward-biased because long-planning projects haven't completed yet. So modern planning timelines are not obviously worse than most historic ones. Second, construction times have clearly worsened: 1940-1959 averaged 3.1 years; 1980-1999 averaged 6.5 years; post-2000 is 5.4 years — still well above mid-century norms. Third, planning and construction times show little correlation with each other within the data. A notable side observation: 14 of 17 post-2000 bridges on his list are replacements for existing bridges (collapse, damage, end-of-design-life), and replacement bridges plausibly get built faster than greenfield ones because removing an existing traffic route creates visceral public urgency that a never-built bridge cannot. He cites the Atlanta overpass rebuilt in six weeks after a fire, plus similar rapid rebuilds in LA and Pennsylvania, with the Francis Scott Key Bridge in Baltimore as an outlier. The concluding hypothesis: "organizational success is often downstream of a culture of urgency" — by analogy to Japanese and Korean shipbuilding standups — and US infrastructure agencies may build faster only when imminent failure manufactures that urgency.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Potter's piece is doing exactly what the discovery layer was promoted-to-roster to deliver: original quantitative work on the built environment that disciplines a recurring discourse claim against actual data. The data-collection caveats are substantial — "planning begins" dates are genuinely ambiguous, his sample comes from Wikipedia's longest-bridges list supplemented by Claude Opus 4.7 suggestions, and he flags the right-censoring problem for the post-2000 bucket. These are correct epistemic moves; a less careful version would have reported the headline 6.2-year planning time post-2000 as evidence of improvement.

The most important contribution is the disaggregation between planning and construction timelines and the finding that they have moved differently. The "infrastructure takes forever now" discourse generally conflates the two, treating regulatory/planning delay and physical-build delay as a single dysfunction. Potter's data suggests these are at least partly distinct problems with potentially distinct causes. Construction times more than doubling between the 1940s-50s and the 1980s-90s, with only partial recovery since, is the harder finding to explain. He doesn't try to explain it. Candidate mechanisms a downstream analyst might propose: stricter post-OSHA worker-safety regimes; less in-house engineering staff at state DOTs forcing more contract management; replacement-bridge construction often happening over live infrastructure with partial demolitions; declining domestic structural-steel supply chain. The piece leaves this open, which is honest but is also where the analytical center of the discourse should now move.

The replacement-bridge observation (14 of 17 post-2000) deserves more weight than Potter gives it. If the modern dataset is dominated by replacements, and replacements have a different political-economy (forced urgency from collapse risk or visible degradation) than greenfield builds, then the post-2000 planning-time number is measuring something qualitatively different from the early-20th-century numbers. The Bear Mountain Bridge taking 54 years to plan (1868-1922) and the Queensboro Bridge taking 34 years (1867-1901) suggest that long pre-build deliberation is not a modern artifact but the historic baseline for greenfield bridges in contested locations; the speeding up we see mid-century was partly a function of postwar consensus and federal financing flows that may not recur. This would imply the relevant comparison is not "1940 vs. 2026 planning times" but "greenfield bridges in any era vs. replacement bridges in any era," and the dataset can probably support that split.

The closing "culture of urgency" hypothesis is where the piece is at its softest. It is true that imminent collapse manufactures speed (Atlanta, I-95, Northridge 10-Freeway) and that this is interesting to note, but "instilling a culture of urgency" is not a portable policy lever — it's a description of what happens when the alternative is unbearable. The Japan-Korea shipbuilding analogy is also doing more work than the data supports; both of those build-outs had specific institutional drivers (state-directed credit, export-led growth strategies, MITI/MOTIE coordination) that are not vibes-of-urgency at all. The honest version of the conclusion is "we don't yet know why construction times deteriorated and partially recovered, and the recovery seems to require specific institutional triggers (forced replacement, federal emergency response) rather than steady-state organizational reform." Potter is doing the right empirical work; the prescriptive framing imports an entrepreneurial-culture model that the data doesn't actually establish. The piece's value is in the disaggregation and the dataset, not the culture-of-urgency speculation, and downstream pieces should treat the first as load-bearing and the second as suggestive.

↩ Back to TOC

Why can’t California count?

Eli McKown-Dawson · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: California's June 3, 2026 primary elections, three days after which races for governor, LA mayor, and multiple House contests remained uncalled; Secretary of State Shirley Weber's response that "accuracy is far more important" than speed; Trump's truth-social posts using the slow count to gesture at fraud; Gov. Newsom's letter to election officials acknowledging the disinformation cost of slow counting.

Eli McKown-Dawson (writing for Silver Bulletin under Silver's framing intro) shows California is a vote-counting outlier: in 2024, it took until November 8 (three days after election day) to count just 70% of ballots, against a 50-state average of 95% by that date. The standard defenses — large state, mail-heavy voting, one-week postmark-grace period — don't hold up against comparative cases. Florida counts 99% of ballots within hours of polls closing, with 27% mail voting and a robust post-election audit regime. Colombia, Japan, the UK, and India all count nationwide elections in 24-48 hours; India counted 640M votes in a day in 2024. Mail-heavy states like Oregon, Washington, and Colorado all count faster than California; Colorado achieves this by limiting late ballots to exceptional circumstances, and California's data shows its grace period has zero benefit (0.5% of ballots rejected for lateness both before and after the policy). On administration quality, California ranks 41st on the MIT Elections Performance Index. The piece argues slow counting also has political cost: it feeds fraud conspiracy theories (Trump's truth-social post is cited), produces misleading partisan skews in early returns (Republicans returned ballots faster in 2026, producing redder-than-actual early counts), and reduces public trust in elections (citing OSF preprint research). The closing claim: California's slowness reflects underfunded elections offices and process choices, not necessary tradeoffs.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is straight Silver-mode argument: a comparative-data piece on a question where most of the discourse is anecdotal, designed to puncture the dominant California Democratic framing (that slow counting reflects inclusivity). The argumentative move is unusually clean. The piece doesn't just say "California is slow"; it constructs the comparative benchmark (other US states, other countries) and shows that none of the defenses California offers — size, mail voting, late-ballot grace period — actually distinguish it from faster-counting peers with the same characteristics. Florida is the strongest case because it shares the relevant features (large state, real mail-voting share, post-2000 reformed administration) and is the comparator California politicians would least want to be measured against. The MIT EPI ranking (41st) is the load-bearing data point because it operationalizes "quality of administration" in a way that's independent of speed — meaning California isn't just slower, it's worse on the metric Weber would presumably want to use to defend the speed tradeoff.

The piece's strongest analytical move is the wedge it drives between speed and accessibility. Weber's framing — speed vs. accuracy/accessibility — implies a tradeoff that the comparative data refutes. The 0.5% late-ballot rejection rate is identical before and after the grace period, which means the grace period isn't actually preserving votes that would otherwise be lost; it's just shifting the counting window. That's a result that, once on the table, makes the inclusivity-defense untenable: you can preserve the same accessibility (0.5% late rejection) without the grace period. McKown-Dawson doesn't quite spell this out, but the data is right there: the policy is doing no detectable inclusivity work, and the speed cost it imposes is real and measurable. This is the kind of empirical-versus-rhetorical argument that the Silver Bulletin format is built for, and it lands here.

Where the piece is less complete is on the political-economy question of why California has stuck with this regime even as the costs accumulate. The closing gesture toward "underfunded elections offices" is true but partial — California's elections officials are also subject to a political coalition that has, for a decade, treated accessibility-coded policies as load-bearing for the state Democratic Party's progressive credentials, even when the inclusivity benefits don't materialize. The Newsom letter cited in the piece is a quiet signal that the state's Democratic establishment is starting to recognize the political cost (the Trump truth-social post is the proximate trigger), which is the most consequential development the piece flags without quite naming. A future version of this argument should engage the institutional question: what coalition would have to be assembled inside the California Democratic Party to move the state from the "13-day count" status quo to the Colorado/Florida regime? The piece's data is strong enough to motivate that question; it just doesn't ask it.

↩ Back to TOC

Inside Britain’s broken war machine

** Wessie du Toit ** · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: PM Keir Starmer's February speech at the Munich Security Conference promising defense investment, the still-undelivered Defense Investment Plan (now four months overdue), and a March incident in which the Royal Navy could spare only one destroyer to defend Cyprus from Iranian drones — and it took three weeks to get there and needed immediate repairs.

Du Toit, an UnHerd contributor reporting from inside Rowden Technologies (a Bristol-based defense-software startup) and through anonymous sources within the Ministry of Defense (MoD), argues that Britain's military problem is not just budgetary but structural. The Army still relies on Nineties-era tanks and Eighties-era armored vehicles; key capabilities require American sign-off to use; the £6.3 billion Ajax armored-vehicle program is ten years behind schedule after injuring crews during trials; nuclear submarine bolts have been reassembled with glue. The cause, du Toit argues from his sources, is a procurement culture that favors a small number of foreign-owned "prime" contractors (only 10 of 23 of the MoD's largest contractors are UK-domiciled, with 18% average British ownership) because the civil-service tender process — described by a 2023 parliamentary committee as "highly bureaucratic, overly stratified, far too ponderous" with "very poor accountability" — treats every bidder as equally meritorious regardless of track record, which advantages incumbents with deep pockets. Ambitious British startups are relocating to the US, where the "neo-primes" (Anduril, Palantir, SpaceX) get the speed and capital British equivalents can't. The piece's prescription, drawn from interviews with Rowden founder Rob Harper and Blackmoor co-founder John McDonald: pick three to five domains of British technological strength and concentrate ruthlessly; accept dependence on allies for everything else; treat sovereignty as a question of being indispensable in specialized parts of the supply chain rather than self-sufficient across the board. Du Toit closes noting that "after the shenanigans of the Trump administration," indispensability to America is no longer the bet it once was.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

Du Toit is doing actual on-the-ground defense reporting — visiting a Bristol industrial park, walking through a semi-anechoic chamber, interviewing the founder of a startup that is trying to scale into MoD contracts, and triangulating those founder claims against an anonymous civil servant inside the procurement process. This is mode-1 (original reporting) and mode-4 (specific domain expertise) on the project's content-type taxonomy, and it fills two roster gaps at once: defense as a primary beat (no roster writer has it; Tooze approaches it from financial-plumbing distance and Freedman is in the discovery layer but didn't publish this week), and British politics not refracted through the US culture war (Mary Harrington's UnHerd piece on Reform-vs-Labour is the only other candidate this week and is more polemical).

What's strong: the reported texture. The Cyprus-defense vignette (one destroyer, three weeks, immediate repair on arrival) is the kind of detail that makes the structural argument concrete in a way an op-ed cannot. The Ajax-program-ten-years-late, glue-on-submarine-bolts, 18%-British-ownership figures are not in dispute — they're parliamentary-committee and National Audit Office findings, and du Toit uses them to anchor the argument rather than gesture at it. The "MoD as immune system" frame from Harper is the kind of phrase the synthesis can actually use because it names a specific institutional dynamic (the rejection of outsiders, the disqualification of founders who lack rank and accent and dress) rather than just decrying decline. And the closing observation — that indispensability to America is no longer a stable bet — is a substantive geopolitical claim, not a throwaway.

What's missing: an honest reckoning with the specialization argument's downsides. Du Toit treats Harper's "pick five companies and make them huge" prescription as basically self-evident, but flags only in passing that this would create a new generation of British neo-primes with the same incumbency advantages he just spent the piece criticizing. The piece doesn't engage seriously with the question of what happens when the specialized capability you bet on becomes obsolete, which is the standard objection to specialization-as-sovereignty in defense economics. There's also a curious gap on China: Britain's drone capability is described as "dependent on Chinese components," and the entire piece is about reducing dependence — but the specifically Chinese dimension of the supply-chain problem (and the specifically British political difficulty of decoupling from it given the City of London's exposure) is not engaged. The piece is structured as a UK-vs-US-vs-Europe analysis, with China as a passing aside.

What's also worth flagging: this piece reads as a procurement-reform argument, not a pacifist or interventionist one — du Toit takes for granted that Britain should be able to fight a serious war, and the argument is about how to do it given the country's actual industrial constraints. That's a register the roster's foreign-policy commentary (Persuasion's pieces on Iran and Russia this week, Klippenstein's defense-budget pieces) doesn't operate in: it assumes a defense-industrial problem worth solving and reports on the people trying to solve it. The value to the roundup is less the policy prescription — Britain isn't America — and more the model: this is what defense-industry reporting from inside a struggling power looks like when the writer takes both the constraint and the capability seriously.

↩ Back to TOC

The family / wage / culture cluster: masculinism, heteropessimism, childcare, citizenship, honor

The New Right's Very Old Vision of Men: The Ezra Klein Show

Ezra Klein · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Helen Lewis's June 2026 Atlantic cover story on the masculinism movement, plus a clip of Boise State political theorist Scott Yenor describing women in mid-level bureaucratic jobs as "medicated, meddlesome and quarrelsome … agents of the new world but not new life."

Klein and Lewis (Atlantic staff writer, author of Difficult Women and The Genius Myth) treat "masculinism" as a distinct ideological project — not the Andrew Tate manosphere — that has unified the MAGA coalition even as Iran-war, Israel, and protectionism debates fragment it. The clearest empirical claim is institutional: Bronze Age Pervert (Costin Vlad Alamariu) is read by young Trump staffers, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth invited Christian Reconstructionist pastor Doug Wilson to preach at the Pentagon, and Tucker Carlson uses Carlson-tier influence to broadcast the 1950s-restoration line. Lewis distinguishes two strands — Christian Reconstructionism (Wilson, the Doug Wilson "headship" theology, hostility to the 19th Amendment) and pagan vitalism (Bronze Age Pervert's longing for pre-Christian heroic masculinity) — both converging on the policy proposal that women should be homemakers, men breadwinners, and modernity has thwarted both. Yenor's most concrete policy proposal — a "family wage" that would restore explicit hiring discrimination in favor of married men — illustrates the legal-instrument turn. The episode's analytic core is a debate over whether the movement's appeal rests on a real grievance (Lewis concedes boys/men do face declining wages, falling testosterone, 5x rise in friendlessness, 4x suicide rate vs. girls/women) and what's needed to engage that grievance without ceding the framework. Klein presses on a Helen Andrews viral essay arguing "wokeness is simply an epiphenomenon of demographic feminization" — the claim that any organization exceeding ~60% women collapses into bitchy bureaucratic dysfunction — and Lewis counters with Kafka and Gogol: bureaucratic dysfunction is bureaucratic dysfunction; the feminization frame is a backfilled diagnosis. The piece's most arresting closing move is the Nick Fuentes problem: a movement built around "we say the things nannying women don't let us say" has no internal stopping mechanism, which is why Fuentes's "women should be in gulags" line drew no resistance from the masculinism camp; only the antisemitism in a separate Fuentes appearance did, and only because that line was enforced by a different power coalition (Israel/Jewish donors). The implicit warning Lewis offers: this movement is not a Tate-style algorithmic phenomenon but an institutional project with intellectuals, policy proposals, and explicit reach into the Pentagon and Trump staff.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The episode's primary contribution is the institutional-mapping move — separating "masculinism" as an ideological project with named theorists (Yenor at Boise State, Wilson at Christ Church Moscow, Alamariu as Bronze Age Pervert, Helen Andrews at Compact/elsewhere) and policy proposals (family wage; suffrage-rollback theology; explicit Pentagon access) from the Tate manosphere, which is much easier for liberals to dismiss as algorithmic flotsam. That distinction is doing real work. Lewis's frame — the movement as the unifying ideology of an otherwise fragmenting MAGA coalition — is the kind of claim that's hard to falsify ex ante but tracks the trajectory of post-2024 right-wing intellectual infrastructure (American Reformer, the New Founding ecosystem, Christ Church Moscow's policy reach into Hegseth's Pentagon). The strongest sentence in the conversation is Lewis's institutional point: "If you asked, 'Do you think feminism has gone too far?' how many people in the MAGA coalition are going to push back on that?" That's both empirically right and analytically clarifying — it explains why the masculinism layer survives debates that fracture every other layer.

That said, the episode has a recurring pattern that Klein's interview format reinforces: he and Lewis are mostly aligned, and the strongest possible counter-arguments to the framing don't get articulated, much less steel-manned. Three places that matters: (1) The empirical question of whether the boys/men crisis is real and structural — Lewis concedes the data but treats the masculinism movement's response as the question, not the underlying grievance. The piece would be stronger with explicit attention to which of the masculinism camp's policy proposals (heterodox education reform for boys, vocational tracks, mental-health interventions, single-sex schools) might actually work on the merits, separately from their packaging. Without that, the implicit liberal frame is "we can engage the grievance without the framework," but the operative question for politics is which engagement actually changes outcomes. (2) The Helen Andrews thesis gets dispatched with the Kafka quip, which is rhetorically satisfying but does not engage Andrews's underlying empirical claim — that the institutions feminism considers its biggest wins (university administration, K-12 teaching, HR, much of nonprofit management) have become culturally dysfunctional in identifiable ways, and that this happened on a measurable timeline. Whether the mechanism is "too many women" (her claim) or "too much administrative bloat orthogonally distributed across professions feminism happened to enter" (the steel-man alternative) requires actually looking at counterfactuals; the conversation doesn't. (3) The Christian/pagan distinction Lewis draws is interesting but undertheorized. Christian Reconstructionism (Wilson, the Federal Vision strand he comes out of) has a 50-year intellectual lineage with explicit positive content — covenantal theology, paedo-communion, postmillennial eschatology — and treats Bronze Age Pervert as fundamentally mistaken about the highest good. The two streams are alliance partners, not co-thinkers; the masculinism convergence may be more contingent than Lewis's framing suggests. The episode flattens these into "the New Right" in ways that make for clean radio but lose the analytical leverage.

The Fuentes-no-line argument is the strongest single critique the episode makes and the place where Lewis's coinage earns its keep. Her point — that a movement built on the proposition "nannying women won't let us say the true things" has no internal mechanism for distinguishing true heterodoxy from mere offense, and so cannot enforce any limit without contradicting its founding move — is structurally devastating and does explanatory work for what would otherwise look like a movement-discipline failure. The fact that the only line that did hold was on antisemitism, and only because a different power coalition enforced it, is the kind of observation that the right-internal conversation usually elides. That observation alone earns the episode a place in the roundup.

Where the episode misses is the question of whether the broader political-economic-cultural conditions that gave rise to masculinism's resonance are themselves fixable from within mainstream liberal democracy. If real wages for non-college men have stagnated for 40 years, if marriage has bifurcated by class, if boys' education and mental-health data really are diverging from girls' — and the masculinism camp's solution is wrong — the natural next question is what the right-of-Klein liberal response actually looks like in policy terms. Klein gestures at this in places but doesn't push it, in part because Lewis isn't there to argue policy. The episode is best read as diagnostic mapping; the policy question hangs unfinished.

↩ Back to TOC

Heteropessimist Horror

Matthew Schmitz · 2026-05-31 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The box-office sensation of the film Obsession (directed by 26-year-old Curry Barker), the third season of HBO's Euphoria, Hulu's The Testaments (a Handmaid's Tale follow-up), and Netflix's Something Very Bad Is Going to Happen — all from spring 2026 — plus Asa Seresin's 2019 New Inquiry essay coining "heteropessimism" and Sally Rooney's 2021 novel Beautiful World, Where Are You.

Schmitz reads the wave of recent young-skewing horror about heterosexual coupling as the cinematic register of a real demographic and behavioral shift: marriages later and less frequent (one-third of today's young people projected to reach 45 unmarried), declining sexual frequency among young adults, even high-school homecoming dances being canceled for lack of interest. He traces the "heteropessimism" frame to Asa Seresin's 2019 essay (focus on men as the root of the problem; coupling between men and women as passé) and notes that even Pope Francis declared in 2016 that "the great majority of our sacramental marriages are null." He distinguishes the recent films from prior horror about children (Rosemary's Baby, The Exorcist, The Omen) — the new genre is about marriage and partnership itself as horror. The piece's analytical move is to read the films through the Obsession contrast between Bear (the date-asking, formality-bound protagonist) and his more confident friend Ian (who succeeds in casual "situationship" mode): the deregulation of the sexual marketplace, Schmitz argues, distributes costs and benefits unequally — the more capable, beautiful, and prosperous prosper, while "the less capable, the less beautiful, the less prosperous" need antiquated forms. He closes by pulling in Sally Rooney's Beautiful World, Where Are You, in which Rooney has her character Eileen fall for a Christian man whose paternalism she finds charming — a hint, Schmitz suggests, that maybe patriarchy isn't such a bad thing — but concedes that simply hymning marriage won't produce desirable men at scale; what's needed is "a change in economic and social conditions, such that men have more to offer women than they currently do."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece is operating on a genre-criticism register, and the central observation — that there's a discernible cluster of recent films/TV using horror conventions to represent heterosexual coupling — is empirically solid. Schmitz names four specific titles (Obsession, Euphoria S3, The Testaments, Something Very Bad Is Going to Happen) and a director's on-record quote ("heterosexuality is the real horror") about subliminal messaging, which is the right kind of evidence for a "this is a genre, not a coincidence" claim. The 1968-1976 children-horror parallel (Rosemary's Baby, Exorcist, Omen during the abortion-debate years) is also genuinely illuminating — it suggests that horror is a reliable lagging indicator of demographic anxieties, which is a more interesting structural finding than the piece develops.

The strongest analytical move is the unequal-distribution observation: deregulating courtship doesn't help everyone equally, because the gains accrue to the already-attractive (Ian succeeding at casual situationships) while the losses accrue to those for whom formal courtship was a leveling mechanism (Bear, the awkward asker-out who needs structure). This is a real materialist observation embedded in a culture-war frame. The Sally Rooney passage works as evidence for Schmitz's argument because Rooney is a left-coded, sex-positive, Marxism-curious novelist whose work is registering the same anxiety — heteropessimism isn't just a right-wing complaint but a structural feature of the post-courtship landscape. Citing Rooney's Eileen-and-Simon arrangement (a left-wing intellectual finding paternalism charming) is the kind of left-can-see-it-too move that prevents the piece from collapsing into pure cultural conservatism.

Where the piece overreaches: the closing pivot from cultural criticism to political-economic prescription ("a change in economic and social conditions, such that men have more to offer") arrives in the final paragraph and isn't earned. The economic-conditions argument is the right kind of materialist closer for a Compact piece (the post-liberal magazine's signature move is to ground cultural-conservatism complaints in left-style economic analysis), but Schmitz doesn't say what economic conditions, what changes would produce them, or what the relationship is between male earning power and partnership formation. He gestures at a real research literature (Autor on declining male wages and marriage rates; the China-shock-and-marriage literature; Wilcox on male economic stagnation) without engaging it. The piece would be stronger either dropping the prescription and ending on the cultural observation, or actually developing the economic argument with one paragraph of concrete mechanism.

A second weakness: the "marriage destroys people" line from the Something Very Bad finale is presented as evidence of heteropessimism, but it's a fictional character speaking inside a horror movie — the rhetorical equivalent of citing dialogue from a Saw film as evidence of cultural anxiety about home invasion. Schmitz acknowledges this when he calls the message "supraliminal" but doesn't fully grapple with the possibility that genre is genre — horror exaggerates, that's what it does — and the leap from "Gen Z horror exaggerates the dangers of marriage" to "Gen Z believes marriage is dangerous" requires more than the piece supplies. The polling and behavioral data Schmitz cites (declining marriage rates, less sex, fewer dances) is the real evidence; the films are at best illustrative.

The piece survives roughly on the strength of (a) the genre observation (the films are real, the cluster is real), (b) the unequal-distribution diagnostic (deregulated markets reward the already-advantaged), and (c) the Rooney-as-witness move (heteropessimism crosses the political-coalition line). The piece's prescription is its weakest part; its diagnosis is its strongest. As a Compact editorial signature piece, it's recognizable Schmitz — culture-war reading paired with a vestigial economic populism — and would land in the roundup's "Culture and the Sexes" thematic cluster if one emerges this week.

↩ Back to TOC

Two Drinks with . . . the Mom Who Prodded Trump About Childcare

Kara Kennedy · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Trump's April 2026 remark at a private luncheon that childcare can't be federally funded because "we have to take care of one thing: military protection," and the Mamdani-Hochul $1.7B NYC childcare announcement earlier in 2026. Light-format interview tied to the June 15 release of Saujani's documentary No Country for Mothers.

Kennedy interviews Reshma Saujani — founder of Girls Who Code and Moms First — at the Hotel Chelsea over margaritas, ostensibly about the viral 2024 clip where Saujani asked Trump how he'd make childcare affordable and got a two-minute non-answer. Saujani's substantive claim is that universal childcare would be politically dominant if either party ran on it cleanly: she cites Mamdani's NYC mayoral win on universal childcare, the $1.7B Hochul-Mamdani pilot already enrolling ~100,000 families, and her own documentary-making travels finding "MAGA moms" who want the same policies as left-coded mothers. She blames the failure on two cultural enclosures: a Republican coalition focused on the cultural side of the pronatalism question (Musk, Vance framing low birthrates as women's selfishness) rather than the economic side, and a Democratic squeamishness about the words "mother" and "family" that she says reads as feminist correctness but functions as political surrender. The personal narrative — September 2020 saw 900,000 women leave the workforce; Saujani's own son was born three weeks before lockdown; she quit Girls Who Code to build Moms First — is offered as the prompt for her policy turn. The interview's tagline ("we love girls, but we hate women") frames the gap between Girls Who Code's celebrated mission and the unsupported infrastructure those same girls hit on becoming mothers.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

"Two Drinks With..." is a light-format column — atmospheric texture (Leonard Cohen, Prada models, margaritas at the Hotel Chelsea), profile-as-PR for an interview subject promoting a documentary, structured around quotable lines rather than tested claims. Read in that genre, this is a competent execution that surfaces one genuinely interesting political claim worth engaging.

The interesting claim is the bipartisan-childcare-as-political-dividend argument: that universal childcare polls well across the MAGA / progressive divide, both parties have failed to capitalize on it, and the Mamdani-Hochul NYC rollout demonstrates an actual proof-of-concept. This intersects directly with longstanding Slow Boring (Yglesias) "popularist" arguments about Democrats abandoning materially-popular policy for culturally-fraught framing, and with the Vance-Musk pronatalism strain that the New Right roster has been wrestling with for two years. Saujani's diagnosis — Republicans focus on culture (women are selfish for not having more kids), Democrats avoid the noun "mother" — is a clean compression of the political stalemate, and the operative empirical question is whether the Mamdani NYC numbers actually validate the claim that "anyone running on this would win." The 100,000-family signup figure is suggestive but not yet election-tested at scale; the test is whether universal-childcare-as-platform survives contact with the federal budget arithmetic Trump invoked (military vs. day care). Pence's piece in the same issue indirectly corroborates Saujani: Pence's catechism includes "the traditional family is a fundamental building block of society" but specifies no childcare position, which is precisely the omission Saujani is naming.

The piece's evidentiary weaknesses are typical of the genre and worth flagging. The "patchy evidence that a kid with a strong attachment to its mother is less likely to be delinquent and more likely to have good mental health" parenthetical does heavy work for the claim that childcare-as-economic-policy reduces crime and improves health outcomes, and the reporter (Kara Kennedy, Free Press) names it as patchy without pressing further. The Reese Witherspoon AI-displacement statistic ("jobs women hold are three times more likely to be automated by AI") is quoted secondhand without sourcing — it appears to derive from a 2023 IMF / McKinsey paper but is presented as if uncontroversial. The "five billionaires are calling all the shots" framing of the AI industry is rhetorically effective but Saujani is herself a tech-leader-turned-advocate; she's positioning, not analyzing.

The genre tension worth naming is between the interview's policy substance (childcare, paid leave, the materialist case for family policy) and its framing as a margarita-and-celebrity-bartenders profile piece. The Free Press has been positioning itself in the post-liberal-feminist space — running pieces sympathetic to trad-wife critiques, IVF-skeptic arguments, and pronatalism — and the choice to profile a Democratic-aligned, Girls-Who-Code-coded subject in a friendly format reflects an editorial bet that motherhood-as-policy can be a cross-coalitional Free Press beat. Saujani is being positioned by the venue as the figure who could carry that beat: a Democrat critical of the Democratic Party's family-policy avoidance, a feminist who criticizes both girlboss and trad-wife extremes, a tech founder ambivalent about the AI industry. Whether that positioning predicts an actual cross-coalition policy coalition — or just produces a vibe-shift in the discourse — is the question the piece can't answer because it didn't ask. The "Mamdani won on this" claim is the cleanest testable prediction; watch what happens in the 2026 House races where Democrats actually run the platform.

↩ Back to TOC

Consent-based laws and aggregate fertility

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-03 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Adrian Mehic's paper in the Journal of Health Economics studying how the expansion of the legal definition of sexual assault to "tacit consent" standards in a panel of European countries affected fertility and sexual behavior.

The Mehic paper finds that switching from explicit-consent to tacit-consent-based legislation reduces fertility by about 4% relative to the mean, driven by a decrease in couple formation and an increase in abortion rates. The mechanism is a behavioral one: more risk-averse individuals withdraw from dating and partner markets following the reform, shifting the composition of the remaining active pool toward people who are less precautionary. Consistent with this compositional shift, contraceptive use rises among younger women but falls among older age groups, and condom use falls among young men. The paper also analyzes Sweden's appeals-court verdicts after consent-based legislation adopted and finds a decline in unanimous guilty verdicts, suggesting tacit-consent assessment is harder for courts. Cowen quotes the abstract in full and adds only one word — "tekl" — apparently a shorthand reaction ("teh-keehl" or similar, signaling registration of significance) but offering no analytical commentary of his own.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is one of the posts where the project memory rule on Cowen Straussian posts applies: the surface text is a quoted abstract with the single-word reaction "tekl," and the implicit argument lives in which paper Cowen chose to surface and what he chose not to say about it. The Mehic paper's empirical claim — that tightening legal definitions of consent reduces fertility through a risk-averse-withdrawal mechanism — is the kind of unintended-consequences finding that has policy salience but cuts against the dominant left-coded framing of consent-law expansion (which treats it as costless or positive on welfare grounds). Cowen surfacing this paper, in the same week as the Cass/American Compass natalist-citizenship manifesto and Cowen's own MR posts on related demographic-cultural themes, is doing implicit-coalition work: signaling to right-coded readers that there's a credible empirical case that consent-law expansion has fertility costs, without himself making that case in his own voice. The "tekl" reaction is the closer that lets him register significance while keeping his own analytical hands clean.

The methodology in the abstract has some real strengths and some questions the surface treatment doesn't surface. Strengths: panel-of-European-countries design with a defined legal-change treatment, behavioral mechanism (risk-averse withdrawal + compositional shift) that's empirically testable through the contraceptive-use and verdict-pattern auxiliary results, and the Sweden appeals-court analysis as a real-world legal-system corroboration. Questions Cowen doesn't surface: how many countries are in the panel and what timing is being used (small-N panel results are fragile); whether the 4% fertility decline is being driven by the consent-law change specifically or by correlated cultural changes the legal reform tracked; whether the compositional-shift mechanism is the right one or whether the apparent withdrawal is selection by some other mechanism (e.g., changes in housing affordability or labor-market participation that move with the legal-reform cohort). These are the standard credibility-questions for a difference-in-differences-style panel result, and Cowen's framing doesn't engage them — which is precisely the move that makes the post Straussian rather than expository.

The substantive policy implication, which Cowen telegraphs by surfacing the paper without engagement, is that consent-law reform involves a tradeoff (lower assault rates? higher conviction quality? — these would be the welfare benefits to compare against) and that the standard discourse on these reforms doesn't include the fertility costs the paper measures. That's a defensible analytical claim, and if Cowen had made it directly it would be a productive contribution to the policy literature. By making it indirectly — through the citation choice — he gets the benefit of having registered the finding for his readers without owning the policy position the finding implies. This is the most clearly Straussian-mode MR post in the week's sample, and per the memory rule, the implicit claim should be named: Cowen is suggesting that tacit-consent legal reform has under-discussed fertility costs that should weigh in the policy assessment.

↩ Back to TOC

Mike Pence: Conservatives Have Forgotten What They Believe

Mike Pence · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Donald Trump's second-term policy turn (tariffs, Ukraine wavering, appointment of a pro-abortion HHS secretary) and Pence's own forthcoming book What Conservatives Believe (Center Street, 2026), framed via Barry Goldwater's 1960 The Conscience of a Conservative.

Pence, writing in the Free Press's "Things Worth Remembering" column as an excerpt from his new book, argues that Trump's second term has decisively broken with the Goldwater-Reagan conservative tradition that Trump's first term still honored. He praises Trump-1 (tax cuts, deregulation, Dobbs-enabling judges, ISIS defeat, deterrence of Russia/China/Iran) but indicts Trump-2 on four counts: complicating rather than extending the tax code, imposing broad-based tariffs Pence calls "the largest peacetime tax hike in American history," "isolationism" that has wavered on Ukraine and "emboldened" Russia, and marginalizing the pro-life cause by installing "a pro-abortion secretary" at HHS. The remedy, Pence argues, is generational: young people raised under Trump know "right-wing populism and big-government Republicanism" but not the principles of Goldwater and Reagan, so the movement needs a "21st-century version of The Conscience of a Conservative" — which is, by implication, Pence's own book. The piece closes with a litany ("Conservatives believe...") restating the catechism: God-given rights, life, limited government, federalism, school choice, free markets, peace through strength, Israel as "most cherished ally," and traditional family.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a positioning document, not an argument — and reading it as the latter underrates what it's actually doing. Pence is publishing a book and excerpting it in the Free Press because he is trying to claim authorial ownership of a post-Trump conservative restoration narrative: the man who refused to overturn the 2020 election now writes the manifesto for the conservatism that follows the populist interregnum. The implied addressee is not Trump voters (Pence has no purchase there) but donor-class Republicans, conservative-movement institutions (Heritage, AEI, the legal-conservative network), and the specific cohort of young conservatives who came up under Trump and have no living memory of pre-Trump Republicanism. The Goldwater frame does a lot of work here: Goldwater lost catastrophically in 1964 but was retroactively credited (per Pence's George Will quotation) with winning the 16-years-later Reagan victory. Pence is asking to be cast as Goldwater — defeated in the short term, vindicated in the long. The reader is meant to file this for 2028 or 2032.

The substantive critique of Trump-2 is sharper than Pence's public role usually allows, and worth taking on its own terms. The "largest peacetime tax hike in American history" framing of tariffs is a real argument that connects to Yglesias's and Noahpinion's quantitative work on tariff incidence — Pence is borrowing economist framing to indict a Republican president, which is unusual for him and indicates he understands his post-Trump audience is the wonk-conservative network, not the populist base. The Ukraine line ("emboldened Russia to continue its brutal invasion of a Western ally") is the strongest claim in the piece because it links a foreign-policy failure to a moral category — abandonment of an ally — that Reagan-conservatives are organized around. The HHS / "pro-abortion secretary" complaint (presumably RFK Jr., though Pence won't name him) carves out the pro-life movement from the broader MAGA coalition and reminds donors that the social-conservative infrastructure that funded the Dobbs project has been quietly stiffed by Trump-2.

What the piece can't or won't do is interrogate the connection between Trump-1 (which Pence defends) and Trump-2 (which he attacks). The Pence framework holds that Trump was a Goldwaterite from 2017 to 2021 and stopped being one in 2025. But the through-line — disregard for institutional norms, personalist coalition-building, demagoguery against immigrants, the J6 episode that ended Pence's own political career — was present in the first term too; Pence is selecting on outcomes (tax cuts good, tariffs bad) rather than on the underlying disposition that produced both. This selection lets him preserve his book's commercial premise — Trump-2's bad turn requires a Goldwater-style restoration — without confronting whether the conservative tradition he wants to restore had already failed its character test when its standard-bearer tried to throw out an election. The closing catechism ("Conservatives believe in character, civility, and statesmanship") is striking precisely because the man writing it was nearly killed by a mob the previous Republican president sent. He invokes the principles without naming the violation; the reader has to do that math.

The Goldwater analogy also has a buried tension: Goldwater's The Conscience of a Conservative (ghostwritten by L. Brent Bozell Jr.) was a movement-building text written when the conservative movement was nascent and ascendant. Pence is writing in the opposite situation — the movement is in power, it has reshaped itself in ways he opposes, and he is asking it to revert. That's a Burkean restoration project, not a Goldwaterite insurgent project, and the texts those projects require are different in kind. Pence borrows the brand without taking on the harder argument: what to do when the apparatus you helped build has been captured by someone who shares your party but not your principles, and the base prefers the capture. The book's actual reception will depend on whether Pence can stage the answer he ducked here.

↩ Back to TOC

Reclaiming American Citizenship

American Compass · 2026-06-03 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: American Compass's launch of a new project, "Reclaiming American Citizenship," unveiled at the New World Gala in Washington on June 2, 2026.

The manifesto frames American decline not as economic underperformance but as citizenship-erosion: the displacement of a "thick" reciprocal relationship (obligations, mutual stake, place-rootedness) by a consumer-subject identity in which voters became "passive subjects of an empire that promised safety and comfort while draining away liberty, dignity, and prosperity." The elites it indicts are the corporate-political fusion that "undermined tradition, respect, and solidarity," dismissed family/community/borders as constraints on market freedom, and "released themselves from their own obligations." It identifies three convergent collapses — fiscal (consuming more than producing), generational (family-formation failure), and institutional (markets, media, government) — and presents the populist surge as a "natural and much-needed rebuke" but not a sufficient response. The positive program: vocational education mandatory for all students, social insurance/housing/labor law reoriented toward supporting marriage and childbearing, place-based research funding distributed across public universities rather than concentrated on coasts, secured borders with immigration policy rebuilt around "the national interest," and an explicit rejection of "performative nihilism" on the populist right. The closing move: "Decline is a choice"; the Constitution's preamble already specifies what citizenship is for, and a citizenry committed to reconstituting itself "still controls its own fate."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the foundational statement for what American Compass — Cass's think tank, founding sponsor of the Commonplace magazine — wants to be its operating concept for the next political cycle. The substantive move is to abandon "economic anxiety" framings (which read coalitional support as a function of falling material conditions) in favor of a "lost citizenship" framing (which reads coalitional support as a function of identity-displacement). This is a genuine intellectual choice with consequences: the citizenship frame supports restrictionist immigration policy, vocational-track education, and industrial policy as continuous parts of one program — whereas an economic-anxiety frame would have to justify each separately on welfare grounds. The manifesto's clearest argumentative move is conceding that "policy alone will not refill the pews" while insisting "policy does shape culture." This is the right position to take if you want to escape the standard left criticism (that post-liberal nationalism is theocratic culture war by another name) and the standard libertarian criticism (that it's industrial planning by another name) — it lets the program operate in the secular-policy register while keeping the cultural-conservative substantive ends.

The piece's empirical claims are mostly assertive, not evidenced — which is what a manifesto does. Where it's strongest is in naming the convergence it claims to address: the simultaneous left-collectivist and right-nihilist failures it identifies on the political fringes is a real diagnosis of the current GOP and Democratic primary scenes. Where it's weakest is the elision between "citizenship" as a thick-relational concept (which would include people whose membership in the polity is established by long residence and contribution) and "citizenship" as a legal-status concept (which is what the immigration-policy parts of the program actually operate on). The piece never quite confronts the tension: a thick-citizenship frame would argue that long-resident undocumented workers are more citizens (in the thick sense) than absent-but-passport-holding members of the coastal elite, but the manifesto's immigration-policy implications rest on enforcing the legal-status version. The same elision underwrites the "we choose instead a youthful determination" passage, which assumes a we whose composition is the question the manifesto's policy program would in fact settle.

The strongest near-term test of whether this is operative or merely rhetorical is the Compass-aligned cohort's behavior on the next live policy choice that splits the citizenship frame from the libertarian-conservative coalition — most likely H-1B reform, agricultural-labor visas (which the surrounding Cass newsletter actually flags as a failure case), or the Bessent shipbuilding-offshore proposal that Rep. Golden's amendment just blocked. If the Compass program operationalizes against the visa-driven cheap-labor regime in agriculture and tech (the harder fight against in-coalition resistance) rather than just against immigration broadly (the easier fight), the manifesto will have done the work of moving the GOP coalition's actual operating program. If it stays at the easier rhetorical level, the citizenship frame will read as a sophisticated relabeling of a coalition that hasn't actually internalized the obligations side of the reciprocal bargain.

↩ Back to TOC

For the Middle Class, US Labor Taxes Are Similar to Nordic Labor Taxes

Matt Bruenig · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The conventional cross-country tax comparison — most prominently the OECD's "Taxing Wages" tax-wedge series — which finds Nordic labor tax rates considerably higher than US rates, and which is routinely used in US policy debate to argue that European-style social provision would require American taxes far above current levels.

Bruenig argues this conventional comparison is misnormalized. The OECD tax wedge (the share of total labor cost going to tax, both employee- and employer-side) treats Nordic public-health-insurance contributions as taxes but does not treat US employer-sponsored health insurance (ESI) premiums as taxes — even though both are mandatory levies on labor income that finance the same good. He notes that the OECD's own System of Health Accounts now classifies almost all post-Obamacare US private premiums as "compulsory private" (a quasi-tax) since the employer and individual mandates went into force in 2014, and that the CBO almost adopted the same scoring before pulling back in 2009. His normalization: add average ESI premiums (single plan and family plan) to the labor-cost denominator and the tax-side numerator, count the employer portion toward labor cost and the full premium toward "tax." Once this is done, American workers at the average wage pay as much or more in effective labor taxes as Nordic workers at their own average wage. He also normalizes the child-benefit regimes (US EITC/ACTC/CTC vs. Nordic cash child benefits) consistently. The piece's closing move: "public squalor does not always mean private opulence. For ordinary people, it often means private squalor as well."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Bruenig doing exactly what he is best at: the data-disciplined left technical argument that makes a familiar progressive talking point empirically harder to dismiss. The OECD tax wedge is the standard cross-country labor-tax measure cited in everything from Brookings to the Tax Foundation to Saez/Zucman to Yglesias's own posts, and the asymmetric treatment of public vs. employer-mandated health financing is a real and underexamined defect. The empirical claim has been made before in various forms — by Jacob Hacker, by Suzanne Mettler in The Submerged State, and in the comparative health-financing literature — but Bruenig's version is unusually clean because it ports directly into the OECD's own methodology, requiring only the addition of one line item (ESI premiums) to make the comparison apples-to-apples. The OECD's System of Health Accounts classifying post-2014 US premiums as "compulsory private" is the analytical lever — once the OECD itself treats these as quasi-taxes for health-spending purposes, refusing to add them to the tax-wedge series for labor-tax purposes is just an accounting inconsistency.

The argument's strongest implication is one Bruenig doesn't press in this piece but is clearly setting up for: if US middle-class labor-taxation is already at Nordic levels, then the standard objection to single-payer or to expanded public benefits ("but the taxes would have to be European!") loses much of its force, because the relevant tax burden has already been imposed — it's just being routed through a less efficient channel that doesn't redistribute, that fragments risk pools, and that locks workers into specific jobs. The "private squalor" closing line is Bruenig's way of inverting Galbraith's "public squalor, private opulence" line: for the median worker, the relevant tradeoff isn't between public taxation and private consumption but between two different forms of compulsory levy, only one of which produces universal social provision.

The piece's main vulnerability is at the methodological joints rather than at the conceptual core. Treating ESI premiums as a uniform tax requires choosing an "average plan" — Bruenig uses a national average — but ESI cost varies enormously by employer size, industry, region, and individual choice; some workers face very high effective rates and many (especially low-wage workers without ESI access) face zero. The Nordic system is uniform; the US ESI system is not, which means the average comparison can be made to work but the median or the median-worker-by-decile comparisons will move around. A reader who wanted to attack the piece would note that ~30% of US workers don't get ESI from their employer at all (Medicaid, exchange plans, family coverage through a spouse, uninsured) and that the comparison Bruenig draws applies most cleanly to the ESI-covered worker. He notes this caveat in passing ("not all workers receive health insurance through their employer") but the load-bearing version of the argument should specify which workers are inside the comparison and which are outside.

A second pushback worth surfacing: counting both the employer and the employee portion of ESI premiums as "tax" assumes the incidence of the employer share falls on the worker through lower wages. This is the standard labor-economics finding and Bruenig is right to invoke it, but it depends on assumptions about labor-market competitiveness that are themselves contested in some sectors. In tight labor markets and for low-wage workers with binding minimum wages, the employer share may not fully fall on the worker. Again — the argument survives, but the cleanest defensible claim is "the average US worker who receives ESI has labor-tax-equivalent levies similar to Nordic workers."

These are refinements, not refutations. The piece's central contribution is the methodological move: forcing the OECD's own SHA classification to do the work the OECD's tax-wedge series doesn't. That move is portable, it can be replicated by any policy shop, and it is exactly the kind of cross-country normalization argument that should be doing more work in US health-financing debate than it currently does. Bruenig also surfaces a piece of policy history worth carrying forward — the 2009 CBO scoring debate over whether post-ACA private premiums would count as federal revenue. That the CBO almost classified them this way and then backed off is itself a useful artifact of the political-economy constraint on how the American state accounts for its own redistributive activities.

↩ Back to TOC

Elizabeth Warren’s “new“ child care push doesn’t make sense

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Sen. Elizabeth Warren's IDEAS Conference remarks calling child care a midterm priority and her re-introduced Child Care for Every Community Act (now co-sponsored by AOC after Sherrill became NJ governor); plus a $50M effort to center child care in Democratic campaigns. Yglesias argues this is a dysfunctional pattern where progressive advocates win policy-prioritization arguments by first winning messaging arguments — and child care, like climate, polls as a niche concern (per Navigator battleground data). It's salient to the dual-income, professional-class subset overrepresented in Democratic staffer ranks but not to most parents of young kids, who per a New America survey prefer more time with kids over more time at work. Warren's bill compounds the problem: its sliding scale subsidizes the working-class families who'd often prefer to cut hours rather than enter formal day-care markets, risks bidding up unsubsidized prices for the professionals who actually want full-time center care, and includes an everything-bagel wage-parity requirement that exists mainly because AFSCME (which unionizes the owners of subsidized day care centers in California) supports the bill. Better alternatives: an expanded Child Tax Credit (especially for under-5s), housing-cost reform via land-use changes, looser blue-state day-care staffing ratios (Gorry-Thomas finds these raise costs without quality gains), and reviving the au-pair program that Biden tightened and Trump has further handicapped.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias doing what he does best — using polling data to discipline elite issue-prioritization, then naming the interest-group coalition that drives the actual policy design. The Navigator polling chart showing child care as a niche battleground concern is the load-bearing piece of evidence, and the New America survey breaking down preferred mothers-of-young-kids work arrangements (only a small share want full-time work; most want to cut hours; "tradwives" are also rare) gives him the demographic argument: the bill targets the preferences of a narrow slice of professionals who happen to dominate the Democratic staffer class. That's a popularism move, but it's grounded in real survey data rather than vibes.

The AFSCME-organizes-day-care-center-owners detail is the most analytically valuable contribution. Yglesias is naming the specific interest-group structure behind why the program emphasizes center-based formal care over cash assistance: it's a sectoral organizing play, not a parental-preference play. That fits his long-running "everything-bagel" critique (per the Klein-Thompson abundance frame), where Democratic programs become design Frankensteins because each constituency gets a piece written in. The wage-parity-with-public-school-teachers clause, which he candidly admits he doesn't fully understand, is exhibit A: it's there because someone needed it to be there.

The constructive alternatives are vintage Yglesias and double-down on his standing positions: CTC over in-kind benefits (cash is more efficient and preference-respecting), housing supply via land use reform (his abundance hobby horse), deregulation of staffing ratios where there's no evidence of quality gains, and au pair expansion as the politically-uncontroversial form of immigration. That last move is particularly Yglesias-coded: he's threading the needle between progressives (immigration good) and restrictionists (orderly programs only), framing au pairs as a win-win that both Biden and Trump have managed to screw up. It's also a class-honest move — au pairs are a service used by upper-middle-class families, and he's not hiding it.

The piece's weakness: it under-engages with why the bill's target matters politically. Even granting the Navigator polling, child care is highly salient to a specific voter segment (suburban professionals with young kids) that Democrats need to hold, and Yglesias's preferred CTC alternative doesn't actually address the supply-side cost of formal care that's the bind for those voters. He gestures at this (CTC plus regulatory fixes plus au pairs would help on the cost side) but doesn't fully reckon with why the political coalition for CTC-only has been weaker than the coalition for the everything-bagel bill — namely that CTC is a benefit that disperses constituencies, while center-based subsidies concentrate them around a sector. That's a real political problem the piece doesn't solve.

↩ Back to TOC

Restoring Honor Culture in the U.S. Military

Peter Mitchell · 2026-06-03 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A cascade of recent military scandals — admiral bribery, conspiracy to steal optics before deployment, Fort Cavazos supply theft, financial counselor defrauding Gold Star families, the Pentagon's eighth consecutive failed financial audit — read together as symptoms of a culture problem rather than discrete enforcement failures; also to VMI's 2021 decision to stop naming expelled cadets during drum-out ceremonies under pressure from a state-ordered racism investigation.

Peter Mitchell, writing in Claremont Institute's American Mind, opens with a litany of recent military corruption scandals and argues this is not a failure of policy or instruction but a failure of formation in the MacIntyrean sense: the institutions still recite words like "honor," "duty," and "integrity" but have lost the practices, communities, and consequences that once gave those words content. The Army Values poster, in Mitchell's reading, is what MacIntyre's After Virtue described — fragments of a moral catechism salvaged from a vanished civilization that knew what they meant. The piece's load-bearing historical claim is that West Point's and Annapolis's pre-1970s honor systems worked because expulsion was swift, public, and shame-saturated, and that post-Vietnam civilianization (Goldberg v. Romeo-era due-process protections extended to cadets) plus 1990s DEI-era concern with disparate-impact analysis combined to convert formative institutions into credentialing institutions. The recommended restoration is concrete: cadet-run honor boards with genuine separation authority subject only to minimal review; reinstating non-toleration clauses with social-death consequences; reviving VMI-style drum-out rituals where the corps formally renounces the expelled cadet's name. Mitchell concedes the academies cannot restore military culture alone but treats them as the last "total institution" (Goffman) in the US military enterprise where formation might still be possible. The piece closes with a Charlie Chaplin "Great Dictator" reference and language about a national renaissance keyed to America 250 — placing it within the broader Claremont-aligned project of post-2024 institutional reconstruction.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece's strongest moves are diagnostic. The MacIntyre frame — that honor cannot be taught through PowerPoint stand-downs because virtue is transmitted through practice within communities of shared purpose — explains why every "ethics scandal → stand-down → updated policy → identical-flavor scandal six months later" cycle in the US military leaves the underlying problem untouched. The observation that "no one says 'that's dishonorable' anymore" except as a legal term of art is the kind of linguistic-evidence noticing that academic philosophers of language and ethnographers of institutions sometimes get to and operations researchers rarely do; it's a real datum about cultural decay rather than a slogan.

The piece's central weakness is its monocausal explanation of the decline. Mitchell identifies post-Vietnam civilianization plus 1990s DEI plus extended due-process protections as the three pressures that, taken together, "achieved something none of them individually intended." But the same period also produced: the all-volunteer force (which fundamentally restructured the relationship between officer and society); the explosion of officer-corps managerialism imported from MBA culture; the post-Cold-War mission proliferation that converted the services into general-purpose foreign-policy instruments; and the privatization wave that pushed warfighting tasks to contractors operating under no honor code at all. Each of these would erode honor culture even with the strictest pre-1970s academy honor codes intact. By collapsing the explanation onto due process plus DEI, Mitchell lets the broader political-economic transformation of the US military off the hook in a way that is convenient for a Claremont-aligned essay (the villains are familiar; the prescription is achievable through internal reform) but analytically weak.

The "total institution" claim is also doing more work than the source can bear. Goffman's Asylums describes total institutions as sites that absorb the totality of inmates' social life, and his canonical examples (mental hospitals, prisons, monasteries, ships at sea) are notable precisely because the modern world has steadily reduced their domain. Mitchell argues the service academies are the last remaining total institution in the US military enterprise — but the boot-camp-and-deployment cycle of enlisted infantry units, special-operations selection courses, submarine commands, and forward-deployed combat units all have substantially more formative authority over the totality of a person's life than four years at West Point with weekend liberty and email access. The academies are the last officer-formation total institution; that is a smaller and more defensible claim than the one Mitchell makes.

The pragmatic prescription — reviving public shaming and drum-out rituals — is also more interesting than Mitchell allows himself to make it. The VMI drum-out he describes ("his name will never again be spoken within the walls of this institution") is genuinely formidable as a social technology of formation, and it survived in attenuated form into the 2010s. The honest version of Mitchell's argument is that bringing it back would require the academies to defend it through litigation against expelled cadets' due-process claims, which would require political cover from the Defense Department, which would require an administration willing to expend political capital on academy-level cultural reform. That is plausibly available under the current administration, and Mitchell is presumably writing in part to encourage it; but the essay would be stronger if it engaged the legal-political mechanics rather than ending on the "national renaissance / 250th anniversary" register, which converts a specific institutional argument into a civic-religious peroration and loses its sharpest reader at the exit.

The piece earns inclusion in the discovery layer because it brings an actual MacIntyrean analytic frame to a beat (military institutional decay) that the regular roster touches only obliquely — Aurelien on Western institutional decay is the closest neighbor — and because it makes a specific, falsifiable historical claim about when and how academy honor culture decomposed that other writers can engage on its merits. Mitchell himself is a writer worth watching for future discovery surfacings if his beat is consistent.

↩ Back to TOC

Post-liberal governance abroad: Hungary, China, Africa, the EU substrate

Hungary's New Power Elite

Thomas Fazi · 2026-06-02 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The early weeks of Peter Magyar's new Hungarian government following Tisza's election victory over Fidesz (after Viktor Orbán's twenty-plus-year tenure), and the two dominant narratives forming around it — liberal celebration of Hungary "rejoining Europe" versus skepticism that Magyar (a former Fidesz insider) represents only cosmetic change.

Fazi proposes a third reading via prosopography of three key ministers.
