From there he argues the structural truth both parties evade: the asylum regime is driven by employer demand for cheap labor and bipartisan dereliction of E-Verify enforcement against employers, that progressive denial about the share of fraudulent claims hurts legitimate asylees by inflating the backlog, that the Biden interior-removals collapse (50K/yr vs Obama's 200K) was a real policy failure, but that Trump's "performative sadism" — family separation, stripping legal representation from 26,000 minors, third-country deportations to Eswatini and El Salvador, ~50 ICE custody deaths since 2025 — neither solves the problem nor justifies itself. His prescription: managed flow, swift removals prioritized by violence not status, E-Verify enforcement against employers, and a reformed asylum that survives at smaller volumes.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The piece's genre is firsthand-account-as-argument, and the credentialing is doing real work: Rojas's specific cases (Humberto saying "I just want to help my family in Honduras," Cuban sponsors preempting the case manager's questions, the Miguel-Amelia indenture network) anchor claims that a desk-bound restrictionist couldn't make without the texture. Strike-the-credentials test: would the argument survive if a Heritage Foundation analyst made the same case from CBO numbers and the NYT's reporting on the 85,000 unaccounted-for minors? Mostly yes on the policy diagnosis (employer demand + dereliction + asylum-fraud signal) but no on the credibility of the national-origin disaggregation — the claim that Cubans were "surprisingly affluent" and least in need of assistance is the kind of observation only a participant could make without sounding like a slur, and Rojas (who is Colombian-American, grew up in South Florida, and writes sympathetically about the harder cases) has the standing to make it. The credentialing is therefore load-bearing in a defensible way, not as a substitute for argument but as the source of fine-grained evidence the argument needs.

The strongest analytical move is the bipartisan-failure account: Rojas neither absolves Democrats by pointing at Trump's cruelty nor absolves Trump by pointing at Biden's enforcement collapse. The E-Verify argument is the right kind of policy proposal because it cuts against both parties' donor incentives (employers fund both parties; ICE is a Republican constituency; immigration lawyers and advocacy groups are Democratic constituencies) and is also straightforward to implement. The interior-removals data (Obama 200K/yr → Trump 100K/yr → Biden 50K/yr) is a real finding that contradicts the Democratic frame of "Trump radicalized enforcement." Rojas is right that no number of additional immigration judges would have fixed this, because the bottleneck wasn't judicial capacity but political will to deport.

The weakest part is the asylum-fraud share estimate, which Rojas frankly concedes is impossible to know — but having conceded that, he still leans hard on "the share of fraudulent claims has been significant" as a load-bearing premise. The Miguel-trafficking case is doing different work than the rest of the piece; it's the strongest single anecdote but it's also the kind of case Rojas's policy framework (employer-side enforcement + E-Verify) would actually catch, so it functions more as evidence-for-his-prescription than as a representative case. The "Cubans lean right and lobby for further intervention" closing observation is honest but pushes the piece into a different argument (about the conservative valence of the immigrant population) than its main one (about asylum-system reform), and feels appended rather than integrated.

For the roster: Rojas is unusual in actually having worked inside the institutions he writes about, which is rare across the discovery + roster layers combined. The materialist-Latin-American beat (he's a regular at American Affairs and writes on the Iberian Peninsula) is also exactly the kind of non-Anglophone-grounded reporting the project's three-lens framework prizes. This single piece is strong enough to suggest a tracker entry if his subsequent Compact pieces sustain the firsthand-evidence-plus-bipartisan-diagnosis pattern.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

As Ezra Jin's Case Gains Attention, His Family Worries China's Retaliating

Frannie Block · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A Tuesday June 2 video filed by Jin's lawyer Guisheng Li outside the Beihai Detention Center after being denied visitation; Trump's mid-May 2026 statement on Air Force One (post Beijing summit with Xi) that Xi was giving "very serious consideration" to Jin's release; the May 2026 Senate resolution calling for Jin's release; Secretary of State Marco Rubio's recent House testimony expressing "cautious optimism."

Block reports an apparent setback in the case of Christian pastor Ezra Jin, leader of one of China's most popular underground churches, arrested in October 2025 in a nationwide raid on charges of "illegally disseminating religious information online." After Trump's mid-May Beijing summit and public optimism about Jin's release, Jin's second lawyer Guisheng Li (the government revoked his first lawyer's license) was denied access to Jin at the Beihai Detention Center on Tuesday after a two-and-a-half-hour wait; Li, on video, called the denial "intentional" and noted that he has been blocked from the government's evidence in the case. Li's previous three meetings with Jin in recent weeks had occurred without issue until Jin's case escalated from the local to city level. Jin's family — including his daughter Grace and son-in-law Bill Drexel, who are the family's public spokespeople — fear Beijing is retaliating against the case's international visibility while still expressing trust in the Trump administration. Block adds a comparison case via attorney Pedro Pizano, former director of the John McCain Freedom for Political Prisoners Initiative: Russia kept journalist Vladimir Kara-Murza incommunicado before convicting and then releasing him alongside Evan Gershkovich in August 2024 — i.e., legal-process acceleration followed by negotiated release is also a plausible reading. The piece closes with the detail that June 4 was both the 37th anniversary of the Tiananmen Square massacre (which inspired Jin's Christian conversion) and the day Jin's daughter gave birth to a son named Ezra for his imprisoned grandfather.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is real reporting: a freshly-filmed lawyer's-side video, an on-the-record family statement from Grace and Bill Drexel, an on-the-record interpretive frame from Pedro Pizano (a credentialed expert on political-prisoner negotiations who worked the Vladimir Kara-Murza case), and a documented U.S. policy timeline (Trump's Air Force One remarks, the May Senate resolution, Rubio's House testimony). Block does not over-claim from the available facts: her headline frames the central question as a question ("Is China Retaliating?"), and Pizano's two competing readings — retaliation versus the Kara-Murza pattern of process-acceleration-as-prelude-to-release — are presented even-handedly. That epistemic discipline is uncommon in foreign-prisoner reporting and worth flagging as a strength.

The piece's analytical limitation is that it operates almost entirely from the family-and-advocate side of the story. The Chinese embassy spokesperson "declined to comment" — fine; that is the genre's normal — but the piece does not engage the broader pattern of how Chinese courts handle accelerated escalation from local to city-level review in religious-charge cases generally, which would tell readers whether the procedural move Block describes is unusual or routine. The Kara-Murza analogy is the analytically strongest move in the piece, but it also points up what is missing: there is a substantial scholarly and NGO literature on Chinese house-church prosecutions (Bob Fu's ChinaAid, the Pew Forum's annual religious-freedom indexing, the U.S. Commission on International Religious Freedom's annual reports) that would help calibrate whether what is happening to Jin is the Chinese system functioning as routinely expected or signaling a deliberate political message in this case. The piece is a snapshot, not a calibration.

The piece's framing is also unusually trusting of the Trump administration's diplomatic competence. The family is quoted calling the administration's efforts "tremendous"; Rubio is quoted as "cautiously optimistic." A more skeptical version of this story would note that Trump's public Air Force One pre-announcement of Xi's consideration ("very serious consideration") was the kind of diplomatic move that authoritarian governments often punish by hardening their position to avoid appearing pressured by a public foreign declaration. If Beijing is in fact retaliating — Block's headline question — Trump's pre-emptive publicizing of the request is at least a contributing factor worth considering. The piece does not raise this hypothesis. Whether that is reporting restraint or a structural disinclination at The Free Press to surface a critique of Trump's negotiation style is the analytical question the piece's framing-by-question keeps open.

What survives: the on-the-record family quote, the timestamped lawyer video, the Pizano framework, and the Tiananmen-anniversary kicker are all primary-reality reporting that adds detail unavailable in wire coverage. The Free Press's beat on Chinese religious persecution (this is the second Block piece on Jin; she also did the prior "China vs. God" piece) is a real specialization the rest of the roundup's roster does not cover — Aurelien, Tooze, Greer all write about China but none on this beat — and the piece earns its slot on those grounds even where its political framing is implicitly aligned with the administration's preferred narrative.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

CA Logic

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: California state laws (or rideshare-platform policies) restricting minors from riding alone in Uber or Waymo while permitting them to drive at 17.

Tabarrok flags an inconsistency: in California, a 17-year-old can legally drive a car but cannot ride alone in an Uber or a Waymo. The closing line ("I long for the days when you could put a kid in the post. We have weakened as a civilization.") signals nostalgia for the late-19th-century moment when parents would post children to relatives through the US Postal Service (a brief 1913 phenomenon), and reads the modern hyper-cautious regulation of child mobility as a civilizational regression.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is the format Tabarrok writes more often than Cowen: a single observation that compresses a libertarian-regulatory-skepticism point into a tweet-length post. The substantive claim is real and worth surfacing: rideshare platforms restrict unaccompanied minors largely on liability grounds (and California law has gone in the same direction), while driver's licenses for 17-year-olds operating multi-ton vehicles in mixed traffic remain freely available. The asymmetry isn't crazy on its face (driver licensing is a state-administered competence test, rideshare-platform liability is a private-litigation-exposure question), but the joint outcome is that we permit the activity with much higher third-party-risk externalities (driving) while restricting the activity with much lower externalities (riding alone in a vehicle driven by a vetted adult or autonomous system).

The "put a kid in the post" joke is doing more work than it looks like. Children-in-the-mail was a real if brief phenomenon (1913, before the post office explicitly banned it), and it's the kind of historical detail Tabarrok deploys to make the point that what counts as a tolerable risk to children is socially constructed and currently calibrated very high. The implicit argument — which Tabarrok doesn't state but which the post invites the reader to construct — is that paternalist regulation has crept into domains where actual risk is low while leaving alone domains where actual risk is high, because the regulatory machinery responds to liability salience rather than to expected-harm calculation.

This is a small post and shouldn't be over-analyzed. It belongs in the roundup as a single-line Tabarrok-mode entry — a roster post on the regulatory-paternalism beat that's part of MR's continuing coverage of how the US has lost the ability to let kids do things. The piece is consistent with Tabarrok's longer-form arguments (the "we used to do harder things" register that recurs in his posts on infrastructure, education, and childcare), and reads as a deliberate stylistic counterweight to Cowen's longer, more allusive posts on adjacent topics.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Should you move to Argentina? (from my email)

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-06 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Recent international coverage of Peter Thiel's reported move to Argentina, and the "Milei as libertarian revolutionary" narrative dominant in international press.

The letter, from Joshua Raoul Neuman (an Argentine-American IR master's student at Universidad Torcuato Di Tella), argues that Milei's accomplishments (lower inflation, fiscal surplus partly underwritten by US Treasury swap lines) are real but exaggerated by both supporters and opponents. The status quo Milei sought to abolish — retenciones (export taxes on agricultural commodities), union control over labor markets, the 40-50% informal-employment sector — largely remains, because Milei needs the retenciones revenue to maintain his signature fiscal surplus. Reforms have had minimal impact beyond Buenos Aires, particularly in northern provinces still run by entrenched Peronista governors. The deeper claim is cultural: Argentine youth aspire more to "la dolce vita" (Spanish/Italian work-life balance with generous welfare state) than to a Weberian Protestant work ethic — "but companies like Paypal, Palantir, and Facebook did not come out of Spain or Italy." Neuman argues Thiel may have been captivated by a "small teleological elite" in Milei's inner circle who don't represent the country they govern; average Milei voters were former Peronists voting out of exhaustion, much as Trump voters in the Rust Belt were former Obama voters. Argentina's genuine case rests on things unrelated to Milei: younger demographics than Europe, world-class human capital, abundant lithium and rare earths, and "geographic isolation from great power conflict." The conclusion: Thiel may be right for entirely the wrong reasons, on a longer timeline, through "considerably more turbulence than the current narrative suggests."

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

Cowen's contribution here is the framing choice — he reposts a guest letter that politely demolishes the libertarian-revolutionary read of Milei without himself committing to either the libertarian or anti-libertarian position. This isn't Straussian in the sense the project memory warns about (Cowen isn't sandbagging a real argument); it's curation in the closer Cowen sense, where the selection is the argument. Several things are interesting about which letter Cowen chose to publish. First, the letter concedes the headline Milei wins (inflation, fiscal surplus) while specifying their fragility — partial dependence on US Treasury support, retention of retenciones (the very tool Milei campaigned against), the persistence of informal labor — which is the version of the Milei record most accessible to skeptics. Second, it identifies the cultural-aspirational gap as the binding constraint: a population that doesn't want a Weberian work ethic won't produce Weberian outcomes regardless of what Austrian-economics inner circle Milei consults. Third, it ends with a defensible long-run case (demographics + resources + isolation) that holds even if everything Milei is doing fails. The composite picture is "Milei is overrated but Argentina remains undervalued" — which is approximately the most calibrated position available, and the one Cowen telegraphs by selecting this letter rather than any of the others he presumably received.

The letter's strongest empirical anchor is the retenciones point. Milei campaigned on eliminating them and has largely kept them because he needs the revenue — this is the kind of policy-vs-rhetoric gap that distinguishes a serious analysis from a press narrative. Where the letter is weaker is in the la-dolce-vita-vs-Weber framing, which is doing a lot of cultural-determinist work without much evidence. The structural claim ("companies like Paypal, Palantir, and Facebook did not come out of Spain or Italy") is true but the comparison is loaded — Israel and Estonia have produced significant tech companies with welfare-state-heavier and more leisure-tolerant cultures than Argentina's. The more careful version of the argument would be about institutional capacity (rule of law, currency stability, intellectual property enforcement) rather than about Protestant work ethic. Cowen, who knows this distinction well, lets the letter make the cruder version of the argument; the selection effect is that he prefers a culturally-confident-but-empirically-soft argument from a local over a culturally-cautious-but-empirically-tighter argument from an economist.

The "geographic isolation from great power conflict" line at the end is the most quietly Cowen-aligned move in the letter: it reads as a vote of confidence in Argentina as a Schelling point for high-net-worth-individuals worried about the next decade's geopolitical risk, which is exactly the kind of forward-looking-second-best-equilibrium reasoning Cowen typically rewards. The letter — and Cowen's selection of it — together amount to "Argentina is a reasonable bet for the wrong reasons; don't believe the libertarian-revolution narrative but do believe the demographic-and-resources case." That's a useful corrective to international press on Milei, even if the letter's cultural framing is shakier than its political-economy specifics.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Should we recriminalize marijuana?

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-04 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: An ongoing debate over the social costs of state-by-state marijuana legalization (excerpted from Cowen's own column in The Free Press, which the post links to).

Cowen — writing for his Free Press column and self-excerpting on Marginal Revolution — argues that the case against marijuana recriminalization isn't that liberalization has been costless (he concedes voluntary choice is overwhelming some users with bad outcomes) but that the state lacks the credibility, information-control, and life-control to make paternalist enforcement work. The world we live in requires "reasonably high levels of self-discipline to do well" (Cowen cites journalist Daniel Akst's 2011 Temptation), and the question is whether to face that world with personal failure as the alternative to nanny-state enforcement, or with a full surveillance-paternalist state backed by AI. Cowen prefers the first: concentrating power in political authorities does not solve the manipulation problem, because political leaders themselves can manipulate weakness ("if marijuana and sports gambling can manipulate weak individuals, so can unscrupulous political leaders"). He gestures at longer-term solutions: GLP-1 drugs (which seem to curb addictive behavior beyond appetite), self-surveillance with self-imposed penalties (an AI or third party docks your bank account each time you puff a joint). If these voluntary tools fail to attract users, Cowen suggests, that itself is evidence that some users' marijuana use is "not a matter of weakness but a very well-established preference, whether we like it or not." The closing concession: "If you have a better solution, I would love to hear it."

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is the rare Cowen piece where the project's "don't apply Straussian label reflexively" memory rule clearly governs — Cowen states the conclusion directly: paternalism is less workable than in times past because the government lacks the credibility, control over information, and control over our lives to pull it off. The piece reads as a substantive libertarian argument-from-state-capacity rather than as a hidden case for the opposite position. The honesty about the costs of legalization (Cowen's concession that voluntary choice is producing bad outcomes for some users) is what gives the argument its strength: this isn't a Hayekian argument that markets always know best; it's a Hayekian-adjacent argument that given the state we actually have, the paternalist alternative is worse in expectation.

The strongest substantive move is the symmetry observation: if individuals can be manipulated by addictive products, they can also be manipulated by political leaders who control the enforcement apparatus. This is a genuinely conservative-libertarian point (Hayek's Road to Serfdom, Friedman on government as a manipulator of consumer attention) that gets less play in current right-coded discourse, which has shifted toward state-capacity advocacy and post-liberal nationalism (see the Cass and American Compass pieces this same week). Cowen is implicitly defending an older fusionist position: that the state's manipulation risk is comparable to the market's manipulation risk, and the choice between them has to be made on the basis of which is more correctable. The GLP-1 / self-surveillance / AI-as-paternalism-substitute paragraph is doing technology-led second-best work the post-liberal right typically doesn't engage with — they tend to argue for state-led enforcement, while Cowen argues for technology-led voluntary self-binding. This is the kind of position that puts Cowen on a different axis from the rest of the project's right-coded roster, including Cass and Slater.

Where the piece is weakest is the closing move ("if they rarely choose such options, then perhaps for many of those people, marijuana consumption is not a matter of weakness but a very well-established preference"). This is doing question-begging work: the whole point of the addictive-substance debate is that "preference" is a complicated category when manipulation operates on the formation of preferences. Cowen's framing assumes preference is observable through choice behavior, which is exactly the assumption the addiction-literature challenges. The Akst citation gestures at this without engaging it. The "if you have a better solution, I would love to hear it" closer is graceful but also a confession: Cowen's argument is structurally a least-bad option, not a positive case, and he knows it. That's the right register for the question, but it also means the piece's policy implications are weaker than its rhetorical confidence suggests.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Learning from Poland’s economic success story

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-03 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Krugman-vs.-Aghion/Bergeaud/Garicano debate over whether U.S. productivity has actually outpaced Europe's, which Yglesias frames as a proxy fight about American progressive policy aspirations. Yglesias uses Poland — now the world's 20th largest economy by nominal GDP, growing faster than the U.S. and closing on Italy/UK — as a test case for what "becoming more like Europe" actually means. Poland's growth model is FDI-driven (especially German manufacturing offshoring), enabled by EU membership, special export zones, and a credible rule-of-law commitment that reassures investors against expropriation; this contrasts with the Studwell/Chang industrial-policy story about Japan/Korea/Taiwan/China developing homegrown national champions. Poland has no global manufacturing champions (the biggest Polish companies are state-controlled utilities and an oil company plus a supermarket chain), but the export sector produces cars, computers, gas turbines, and batteries through foreign-owned factories. The "European" social-model features American progressives admire are all present: a minimum wage equivalent to ~$18/hour in U.S. terms, the NFZ national health-insurance fund (more like American Medicare than Canadian single-payer, allowing private supplementation), low gun ownership, and extensive urban rail in Warsaw/Kraków/Tri-City. Yglesias's punchline: high minimum wages, universal health insurance, mass transit, and gun regulation don't kill tech start-ups — Europe's actual tech-start-up problem is labor-market rigidity (high firing costs), not the high taxes or social spending that American conservatives blame. He notes California has the highest top income-tax rate in the U.S. and still hosts most successful start-ups.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is one of Yglesias's clearest abundance-meets-popularism syntheses, and it's worth taking seriously as a structural argument. The Poland case study is genuinely well-chosen: it's an economy that both conservatives and progressives like for different reasons (conservatives like the up-from-Communism story; progressives like the social model), so it can serve as a credible existence proof that some elements of the European model are compatible with strong growth while others aren't. The implicit target is the muddled American debate where "European-style policy" gets bundled into a single up-or-down vote, when in fact the bundle is separable.

His mechanical claim — that Europe's tech-start-up weakness is about labor-market rigidity (firing costs), not taxes or social spending — is correct in the productivity-economics literature and underrated in American political debate. The California-has-the-highest-top-rate-and-still-has-tech rejoinder is the kind of empirical jab that should embarrass conservatives who treat tax cuts as the only growth lever. But Yglesias is also implicitly correcting progressives who treat "becoming more like Europe" as an undifferentiated good: the European inheritance-mandated-minimum-to-kids policy he flags as "nuts!" is a useful reminder that European social models include features Americans on both left and right would reject.

The FDI-vs.-industrial-policy contrast is the most interesting analytic move and reflects an evolution in Yglesias's growth-policy thinking. He's been broadly skeptical of Studwell-style "develop the local champions" industrial policy applied to mature economies, and Poland gives him a concrete counterexample: you don't need national champions to catch up to the frontier, you just need credible institutions and openness to foreign investment. This is consistent with his abundance-liberal commitments but cuts against the Stoller/BIG faction's enthusiasm for industrial policy and antitrust-as-development-tool. The Mexico contrast — also FDI-heavy, but bifurcated into a small dynamic export sector and a large unproductive informal sector — is where the rule-of-law claim does its work: Mexico's drug-violence-and-corruption problem prevents the dynamic sector from pulling up the rest of the economy.

One real weakness: Yglesias is too quick on the "Poland's high minimum wage hasn't hurt them" claim. Poland's average wage is much lower than the U.S., and a min wage at 50% of the average operates differently than one at 50% of a much-higher U.S. average would — the relevant comparison is to the productivity of the workers affected, and Polish manufacturing productivity has grown rapidly. Mapping that to "an $18/hour U.S. minimum would be fine" requires assumptions about U.S. labor-demand elasticities that the piece doesn't make. The framing is also gentle to the EU-membership story — Poland's growth is fundamentally a story about access to a wealthy single market under credible institutional rules, which is much harder for other developing economies to replicate. The implicit conclusion that other countries could grow this way by copying Poland's policy mix probably overstates how transferable the EU-platform dimension actually is.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

The way we treat pigs is a sin

Noah Smith · 2026-05-31 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the Save Our Bacon Act ("SOB Act") incorporated into the House-passed 2026 Farm Bill (now in the Senate), which would prohibit states from regulating the sale of meat based on out-of-state production methods, nullifying California's 2018 Proposition 12 and Massachusetts's 2016 equivalent. Lewis Bollard's "Save Our Pigs" Substack post explains the mechanics; Marina Bolotnikova's Vox/Future Perfect coverage documents the legislative back-channeling.

Smith writes in confessional register about the contradiction between his self-image as a decent person and the existence of 73 million pigs in US concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs), most sows confined for life in gestation or farrowing crates without room to turn around. He cites peer-reviewed work on porcine "emotional contagion" and cognition (probably smarter than dogs) to anchor the moral weight, and concedes that personal vegetarianism is both insufficient (price-effect: one person abstaining lowers price and raises others' consumption) and inadequate (analogous to refusing to eat human flesh if humans were farmed — the moral duty is to abolish the system, not opt out). The legislative argument: California's Prop 12 passed 62-38 and was upheld by the Supreme Court; polling (Harris, Data for Progress) shows ~66% of regular pork buyers find gestation crates unacceptable and majorities across demographic groups support reform. The Prop 12 implementation produced a ~20% price bump on covered products, which Smith treats as a one-time cost worth paying. He reads SOB Act proponents as motivated not just by hog-lobby capture but by an affirmative desire to entrench the right to abuse — "the cruelty is the point" — citing research on disempowered groups taking out frustrations on those with less power, and frames conservative cultural resentment as a driver. The closing move is unusual: invoking I.J. Good's "Meta-Golden Rule" (treat inferiors as you would be treated by superiors) via Vernor Vinge's 1993 singularity essay, arguing the rise of AI should prompt humans to extend moral protection downward exactly because we may soon find ourselves on the receiving end of the same logic.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

Smith does something here that is rare in his oeuvre: writes in straightforwardly moral-confessional register rather than technocratic-explanatory register. The piece is more sermon than analysis, and it works because Smith doesn't pretend otherwise — the "I have no other word for this except sin" line is doing exactly what it advertises. This is also a piece that lands without much controversy on the substance: gestation crates are documented, Prop 12 passed and was upheld, the SOB Act exists and is currently inside the Farm Bill, the polling is what he says it is. The factual scaffolding is solid.

The strongest analytical contribution is the political-economy diagnosis. Smith correctly identifies the structural problem — concentrated interest (hog producers) vs. diffuse interest (voters with a conscience) — and correctly notes the legislative tell: a measure that failed as a standalone bill is now being smuggled into omnibus legislation, which itself is evidence proponents know it can't survive direct public attention. This is the kind of process critique (the sneaky bundling) that should be salient to readers across orientation. The 20%-price-bump-is-worth-it argument is also clean: it concedes the cost honestly rather than pretending humane farming is free.

Where the piece overreaches is in the "cruelty is the point" extension. Smith offers a real but speculative motivational claim — that SOB Act proponents want to affirm the right to abuse animals, not just protect the hog lobby — and cites a workplace-aggression paper to anchor a frustrated-conservatives-displacing-onto-pigs story. This is a meaningful weakening of an otherwise tight argument. The simpler hog-producer-rent-protection explanation is sufficient to account for the legislative behavior; the affective-cruelty addition is unfalsifiable and reads as the kind of opposition-motive-impugning that Smith would (correctly) flag if a conservative did it in reverse. The Pinker-development-and-animal-cruelty link is similarly thin (correlation, not mechanism). The piece would be stronger if it stopped at "concentrated interest + omnibus-bundling tell" and left out the psychological imputation.

The closing AI/Meta-Golden-Rule move is interesting and likely will be the part of the piece that travels furthest in the discourse. Smith is essentially arguing that animal-welfare ethics now has an instrumental rationale beyond moral consistency: we should establish norms of strong-protects-weak now, while we are the strong, because superintelligent AI may soon look at our treatment of cognitively-comparable animals and apply the same logic to us. This is an argument that crosses the EA-aligned AI safety community and the animal-welfare community, both of which Smith reads. It's not airtight — there's no reason to think a superintelligence would key off human treatment of pigs rather than its own utility function — but it's a real move and one that may reframe the AI ethics → animal ethics conversation.

The other genuinely useful contribution is the framing of vegetarianism's economic limit: one person's abstention lowers price for everyone else, which raises others' consumption. This is a clean economic argument against the "be the change you wish to see" theory of personal ethics in commodity markets, and it does justify the legislative pivot. Smith is essentially making the standard public-goods argument for coercive solutions to coordination problems, applied to animal welfare. That argument is portable: it works whether or not you accept the "sin" framing.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Mario Vargas Llosa and the Possibility of a Populist Novel

Geoff Shullenberger · 2026-06-06 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The English-language publication of Mario Vargas Llosa's posthumous final novel, I Give You My Silence (trans. Adrian Nathan West, 2026); the upcoming Peruvian presidential runoff in which Keiko Fujimori (whom Vargas Llosa endorsed against the left in 2021) is a contender.

Shullenberger (Compact senior editor) treats Vargas Llosa's last novel as the author's most explicit attempt at what Marxist critic José Carlos Mariátegui called "interpretación de la realidad peruana" — a unified theory of Peruvian society — and as the formal record of his late political turn from Hayek/Thatcher "classical liberalism" to right-populism. The novel's hero, Toño Azpilcueta, a "proletarian intellectual" stuck at Lima's Universidad Nacional de San Marcos, builds his theory of Peru around the vals criollo (creole waltz) — a popular musical form Toño elevates as a vehicle of national unity, claiming demotic culture (not high art) is now where "the private history of nations" gets written. Shullenberger maps this onto Vargas Llosa's biography: cosmopolitan Spanish-noble littérateur whose 1990 presidential run was beaten by Alberto Fujimori's Thatcherite-but-corrupt-authoritarian fujimorismo — a "proto-MAGA" that combined the economic program Vargas Llosa had proposed with the populist style he had warned against. Shullenberger reads huachafería (an untranslatable Peruvian sensibility combining outlandish pretension with utter earnestness) as the cultural element Vargas Llosa once disdained but late in life partially embraced — visible in his endorsement of Keiko Fujimori in 2021. The closing question: what can the novel be in a populist age, when the form's traditional vehicles (high modernism, littérature engagée) have been overrun by the music-and-spectacle channels the populist movement actually operates in?

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

Shullenberger is one of Compact's better essayists when he stays inside the literary-criticism register, and this piece largely does. The argumentative substance is the reading of huachafería as the politically operative cultural element — bringing this Peruvian aesthetic vocabulary (florid, gesticulating, "operatic" rhetoric; "snake charmer" demagogy) into English-language post-liberal commentary, where it can do work that the standard post-liberal vocabulary (Tocqueville, Burke, MacIntyre) can't. The move is genuinely clarifying: it captures what makes populist political rhetoric register as legitimate to its audiences and unbearable to its critics — not because the critics object to the substantive program (which often overlaps with what they want) but because the style signals the wrong class register. Vargas Llosa losing to Fujimori in 1990 with the same Thatcherite program Fujimori later implemented is a real datum about how political legibility works under conditions of low elite-popular trust, and Shullenberger's connection of that to the current right-populist wave is at the strong end of Compact's analytic work.

The piece's weak spot is that it never quite tests its own thesis. Shullenberger treats Vargas Llosa's late-career endorsement of Keiko Fujimori as the author's mature reconciliation with the populist register he had earlier criticized — but the rest of the essay's argument actually demonstrates the opposite: Vargas Llosa created Toño Azpilcueta as a fictional alter ego precisely to ironize the position. Shullenberger acknowledges the ironic distance in passing ("Vargas Llosa establishes an ironic distance from Toño's woozier proclamations") but doesn't reckon with what it means that the late novel's most affirmative cultural-political claims are placed in the mouth of a tragicomic figure. The reading-through-irony move would invert Shullenberger's conclusion: rather than the late novel being commensurate with Vargas Llosa's late right-populist phase, it would read as the author's continued literary discipline of placing his political position one register removed from his own voice, even as he was operationally endorsing the political program. That's a more interesting reading than the one the essay lands on, and Shullenberger has the material for it but doesn't take it.

The essay's other latent move — never quite stated — is that Compact's editorial program is itself running the huachafería play: combining genuine post-liberal political-economic content with a register that the cosmopolitan critics it engages register as flamboyant or operatic. Read against that, the piece is partially self-referential: a Compact essay on whether the literary form can survive in a populist age, written for an audience that has chosen the populist political coalition. The closing line ("Unfortunately, the great writer can now give us only his silence") is more freighted than the essay quite says — it's also a comment on Compact's own position, which is that the cultural-political reconfiguration the magazine is documenting may have left no obvious literary form to carry it. That's a real intellectual problem the essay surfaces without quite addressing.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

You Can and Should Blame Young People When They Act Like Lazy Cheaters, Actually

Freddie deBoer · 2026-06-03 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A June 2026 New Yorker piece ("The Despair of the Professor in the Age of AI") collecting professor-laments about AI cheating, alongside a broader NYT pattern of pieces (a June 1 NYT Magazine linguist defending Gen Z "POV" usage) excusing youth behavior on structural grounds.

deBoer opens with a Gen Z linguistic tic — "POV" used to mean "look at this" rather than "from the perspective of" — and the NYT-employed-linguist's framing that grammar-traditionalists are exercising social power. He uses this as the cold-open analogy: the descriptivist-progressivist refusal to criticize youth language is the same reflex animating educators' refusal to call LLM cheating cheating. The main argument: adults who indict "the system," "the assignments," "capitalism," or "the credential mill" when students feed prompts into ChatGPT are not displaying compassion but performing youth-adjacency, "purchasing (at the students' expense) the one thing those people want more than tenure or relevance or grandchildren: the assurance that they are not old." deBoer concedes the structural critiques — incentives matter, the credential mill is rigged, eighteen-year-olds will cut corners — but argues none of that dissolves the individual student's choice, and that the student's act of lying about cheating proves the student knew it was wrong. The piece's anti-therapeutic frame: "to refuse to blame someone is to refuse to take them seriously as a moral agent"; "education is a form of coercion" and ethics, like algebra, are cultivated under constraint rather than summoned from within. He closes that the honest student who watches cheaters get the same grades while teachers exonerate them is learning the system's actual lesson: integrity is a tax only the honest pay.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

deBoer is operating in his strongest mode here — the anti-therapeutic, anti-credentialist Marxist who is also unsparing toward the cultural-left versions of structural excusing — and the piece's central move (lying-as-tell) is genuinely sharp. "Nobody lies about something they believe they were actually morally entitled to do" is the kind of one-sentence diagnostic that does real work, and it preempts the strongest steelman of the structural defense in a single beat. The piece's secondary move — connecting professorial youth-flattery to the "Cool Professor" archetype and to deeper civilizational vanity about aging — fits a project pattern deBoer has been developing for years and that the project has tracked (his prior "Is there a more pathetic figure than the Cool Professor" piece is referenced inline, and the New Yorker piece on professorial despair is the specific upstream prompt this essay responds to).

The structural problem with the piece is that its argument is both against and dependent on the structural reading. deBoer says "the system is unjust AND you, specifically, made a choice and the choice was wrong" — meaning he wants to hold the both/and posture. But the piece's rhetorical center of gravity falls hard on the individual-moral-agent side; the structural critique is conceded in single sentences and then quickly leaves the stage. A reader who thought the credential mill was the larger problem would not find much engagement with that view in this essay — the "burn down the credential mill" line is rhetorical wallpaper, not analysis. That's a defensible choice (the essay's target is the excuse-makers, not the credentialist system itself), but it does mean the piece is unbalanced in a way deBoer's better political-economy writing isn't.

The Gen Z-grammar frame at the opening also does more work than it deserves. The argument "descriptivism is meta-prescriptivism" is genuine, and the analogy to LLM-cheating exoneration is suggestive, but the link is decorative rather than load-bearing — strike the opening 700 words and the cheating argument survives unchanged, and the opening's "every descriptivism is meta-linguistically prescriptivist" is the kind of dunk that delights deBoer regulars while telegraphing to skeptics that he's grinding axes rather than analyzing. The piece would be tighter starting at "There's a particular sound a room full of educated adults makes when a young person does something wrong."

What's worth carrying into the synthesis: deBoer's diagnostic — that the refusal to judge is a status play by adults afraid of looking old, not a thoughtful position about agency — is the rare framing that names a specific psychological mechanism rather than gesturing at "the culture." It pairs cleanly with the broader project register on therapeutic culture and on what Tyler Cowen calls "complacency." The strongest version of the opposing view — that even if the individual student chose to cheat, attributing moral failure rather than structural causation reproduces the credentialist trap deBoer himself wants to burn down — gets a partial answer ("if the system is that exploitative, your obligation is to quit") but not a full one; that's the piece's residual seam.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Goodbye To All That

Harry Cheadle · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Byron Allen's mid-May 2026 majority-stake purchase of BuzzFeed and James Murdoch's late-May 2026 purchase via Lupa Systems of Vox Media's more valuable properties (including New York Magazine) — two near-simultaneous events Cheadle reads as the official end of the digital-media era that the "big three" of Vice, Vox, and BuzzFeed (peak combined valuation ~$8.4B, with Vice alone at $5.7B) defined in the 2010s.

Cheadle, a former Vice staffer with stock appreciation rights that never paid out, writes a personal-essay-shaped requiem for the 2010s digital media boom. His structural argument: the entire business model was built on traffic captured through external platforms (Google, Facebook, Instagram, YouTube), so the audience "wasn't ours in any meaningful sense." When the platforms decided to keep audiences on their own properties and capture the ad revenue themselves, the publishers were left without a business. Compounding this, the attention market that social media created turned out not to be a place for shared interests but a "vicious attention market" in which influencers, streamers, and conspiracy theorists out-competed publishers who were "trying to tell stories that were true." Cheadle catalogs the era's surviving fragments — Vice in post-bankruptcy form, Defector and Ravenous as worker-owned successors, individual journalists scattered to NYT, Atlantic, advertising, comms, or Substack — and frames the industry-wide retreat to paywalls and Discord communities as the abandonment of the original techno-optimist 2010s vision. He confesses uncertainty about what the era was even for: "We were supposed to build… what?" The closing register is genuinely elegiac rather than analytical.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The personal-memoir-as-industry-eulogy genre is well-trodden — every former BuzzFeed, Vice, and Gawker employee has written some version of this essay since 2023, and the trade-press version of "digital media is dead" has been a standing trope for at least four years. The question for the roundup is whether Cheadle's iteration adds something the genre hasn't said five times before, and the honest answer is: mostly no. The Byron Allen / James Murdoch news peg is fresh, but the diagnosis — platforms ate the audience, attention markets favored entertainers over journalists, paywalls and Substacks are the survivor strategy — is the consensus version of events circa 2024, not new analytical work.

What the piece does well is the personal-economic detail. The bit about Cheadle's Vice stock appreciation rights — "back-of-the-envelope math…something in the five figures, which to a journalist in his mid-twenties is kind of a lot" — does specific work that the abstract diagnosis doesn't. It names the wealth-promise the era made to working journalists, and the implicit silence at the end of the essay about how much money the founders and early VCs actually walked away with (Shane Smith, the Peretti brothers, etc.) is its own commentary even though Cheadle doesn't dwell on it. The Bob Odenkirk dildos anecdote, similarly, does atmospheric work that an analytic essay couldn't. As personal-essay-as-industry-record this is fine.

But on the substantive industry question — what killed digital media — Cheadle's account is missing the most important variable, which is the iOS 14 / App Tracking Transparency change in 2021 and the broader collapse of programmatic ad targeting that followed. The story Cheadle tells (platforms keeping audiences on-platform) is real but secondary; the larger story is that the entire economics of audience-as-advertising-inventory collapsed when Apple let users opt out of tracking and Google followed (partially, sloppily, but enough). A serious requiem for the era would name that. Cheadle's framing — that publishers were victims of platform predation — is partly true but lets the publishers themselves off the hook for a model that was always financially incoherent. Eight billion in combined valuation for three companies that mostly aggregated borrowed-audience clicks was always a sand-castle; the platforms didn't take what was always yours, they declined to keep subsidizing what was never yours.

There's also a missed analytical opportunity around the ideological-project framing Cheadle gestures at and then drops. He says BuzzFeed, Vox, Vice, and Upworthy "were fellow-travelers in the same ideological project," and the techno-optimist 2010s vision (more connected, more populist, more progressive, just plain nicer) is in the same passage. But he doesn't follow through on whether the political-and-cultural failure of that project — the way 2010s digital media's progressive consensus produced its own backlash, contributed to the conditions for Trumpism, and is now memory-holed by the same writers who created it — is connected to the financial failure he's mainly diagnosing. That's the more interesting essay and Cheadle doesn't write it.

The closer ("man, it was fun while it lasted") is honest in a way that flattens the analytical claim. The roundup's standing media-eulogy thread (Klippenstein on independent journalism, deBoer on professional-class media writers, Taibbi-historically on legacy outlets) covers most of this ground with more analytical edge. The Cheadle piece is publishable on memoir grounds but is the weakest of the five Persuasion picks for what the roundup needs.

Trial-source eval: This piece would not obviously survive on the regular roster — the genre is oversupplied (Klippenstein and deBoer between them cover media-collapse better and more frequently), and Cheadle's analytical signature on this piece is thin. The personal-memoir register is the value-add but the roundup is not optimized for personal memoir as such. A useful pick this week because the Byron Allen / Murdoch news peg is genuinely significant industry news, but the case for promoting Cheadle to the regular roster on the strength of this piece alone is weak. Treat as a one-off worth surfacing because the news matters, not as evidence Cheadle should be a standing source.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Why we ought to kill the healthy patient

Jeroen Bouterse · 2026-06-05 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Judith Jarvis Thomson's classic "transplant problem" thought experiment in ethics (and its standard pairing with the trolley problem); Original commentary in essay form.

Jeroen Bouterse — a Dutch historian of ideas and frequent 3QD essayist — writes a satirical first-person essay narrated from inside a fictional high-trust utopia where a randomly-striking fatal disease can only be cured by killing one healthy thirty-year-old to save five sick ones. A voluntary "5-to-1 scheme" reduces every participant's chance of dying at thirty from 20% to 4%; ethics boards routinely approve killing the healthy patient even in edge cases where consent cannot be confirmed; consequentialists then push the analogy to apply the same logic at age three; default enrollment is proposed as the next reform. The narrator presents all of this in the calm, reasonable voice of a citizen explaining to dimly-remembered ancestors how their squeamish distinctions ("killing" versus "letting die," means-versus-ends rules) were really just artifacts of their lower social trust and confused intuitions. The essay never breaks frame — its argument is the disorientation produced by watching a familiar moral atrocity get derived, step by step, from premises (veil-of-ignorance reasoning, the trolley intuition, procedural fairness, expected-utility maximization, the historical analogy to vaccination mandates) that the reader already accepts in their normal applications. The implicit target is any ethical framework that treats Thomson's original transplant case as merely a counterintuitive bullet to bite rather than a structural objection.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The essay is a single sustained piece of moral philosophy delivered in fictional voice — closer to a Stanisław Lem or Ursula Le Guin parable than to a typical 3QD link post. The genre choice is doing the argumentative work: a frontal essay rebutting consequentialist defenses of the transplant case would read as a familiar bioethics-seminar exercise, and Bouterse's frame puts the reader in the position of an inhabitant of the world the framework actually produces, which is the position the framework's defenders rarely stay in long enough to feel the discomfort of.

The argument is structurally a reductio of three separate moves that real bioethicists and effective-altruist-adjacent ethicists actually make: (a) treating the veil-of-ignorance to license collective procedures any rational agent would consent to ex ante; (b) treating the trolley intuition (most people will pull the lever to save five at the cost of one) as evidence about a deeper principle that the transplant case should also obey; (c) using the historical move from low-trust to high-trust institutions as warrant for relaxing constraints — the way modern democracies relaxed criminal procedure assumptions over time. Bouterse's essay traps all three in a setting where they conjoin to produce a state-organized organ lottery, and the trap is meant to make visible that what blocks the trolley-to-transplant generalization in ordinary moral reasoning isn't squeamishness or low social trust — it's a substantive commitment about what it means to treat a person as an end. The piece doesn't say this; it lets the reader notice that the narrator's mode of explanation makes it impossible to say.

The strongest version of the opposing view — which Bouterse anticipates but doesn't engage at length — is that the dystopia he describes is question-begging because real consequentialism would notice the second-order corrosion of trust the scheme produces and refuse to license it on those grounds. The essay's response, embedded in the world-building, is that high trust is more compatible with the scheme rather than less; in a high-trust society the verification machinery actually works, so the scheme's expected-value defense survives the second-order corrosion objection. Whether this is fair depends on whether you think public administrative trust survives the spectacle of state-organized lethal selection by random draw. The essay implicitly thinks it doesn't, and the bland competence of the narrator's voice is the indirect argument that the kind of social fabric capable of running the scheme is one in which moral perception has already been pre-anaesthetized.

The piece is also a quiet rebuke to a particular tic of online rationalist and effective-altruist writing — the move that says "if you accept premise X and you accept the trolley intuition, you must accept Y; biting the bullet is the rigorous move." Bouterse's narrator is the bullet-biter telling the story from inside the world the bullet built. It belongs in the discovery layer as a piece of original moral philosophy that the regular roster (which leans toward applied policy and political commentary) doesn't carry, and as a counterweight to the rationalist-coded utilitarian register that occasionally surfaces in Scott Alexander's posts on similar terrain.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Hard Rain

Wyatt Williams · 2026-06-01 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The July 6, 2025 attack on an Oklahoma City NWS radar tower by Anthony Tyler Mitchell, claimed by Veterans on Patrol leader Michael Lewis Arthur Meyer as religiously-motivated direct action against military "directed energy weapons"; reporting trips to Rainmaker's Pendleton, Oregon cloud-seeding operation; the July 2025 Texas Hill Country flooding (139 dead); Robert Smithson's Spiral Jetty as a structuring metaphor.

Wyatt Williams — author of Springer Mountain: Meditations on Killing and Eating and a former Weather Channel writer — opens with the security-camera footage of a bearded militiaman scaling a radar-tower fence in Oklahoma City and smashing the million-watt power supply with a hammer because his militia leader has told him the latest-generation NEXRAD radars are military directed-energy weapons being pointed at Americans. The essay then moves through three nested registers: (a) Williams's own year working at the Weather Channel, where he learned that "weather is something we feel; the numbers just tell us what it might feel like," and that the wire-service economy now ensures every storm anywhere on earth is documented and resold; (b) a reported visit to Rainmaker Technology Corporation in Pendleton, Oregon — a venture-funded cloud-seeding start-up using AI-instrumented weather balloons and silver-iodide-dispersing drones to extend drought relief and snowpack for Western water utilities; (c) the long historical arc of human weather-modification attempts — child sacrifice, rain dances, alchemical concoctions, cannon-firing — and the twentieth-century shift to actually-effective interventions starting with Vincent Schaefer's 1946 silver-iodide work at GE. Williams's structural claim is that the militia's "they're modifying the weather and that's against God" theology and the Rainmaker CEO's "we can tell the rain to fall in the places where we need it" pitch are both downstream of the same underlying transformation — the conversion of weather from a felt encounter with a force much larger than the self into a measured, modeled, and now manipulable resource. The essay closes at Smithson's Spiral Jetty on the now-receded Great Salt Lake, with the observation that "stories about doom are easy to write … the real world is much weirder than the narratives we try to fit it into," and ends literally on snow falling two days later.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The piece is a strong example of Harper's-genre long-form reported essay — the "Miscellany" slot the magazine reserves for narratives that braid first-person, reporting, and intellectual history without the obligation to land a single polemical thesis. Williams's prose discipline is the load-bearing element: the radar-tower cold open isn't presented as evidence of anything in particular (not "militia violence is rising," not "weather modification is dangerous"), but as a particular shape of human encounter with a system larger than the self that the rest of the essay is going to recur to.

The strongest analytical move is the framing of the militia conspiracy-theory and the venture-funded cloud-seeding pitch as twin expressions of the same epistemological dislocation. This is harder to do well than it sounds; the cheap version would be a "horseshoe" claim about extremes meeting, or a postmodern "both are constructions" relativism. Williams's version is more precise: both Veterans on Patrol and Rainmaker have abandoned the felt-encounter-with-the-weather phenomenology and replaced it with a model of weather as either a weaponized signal (the militia view) or an addressable resource (the Rainmaker view), and the essay's substantive argument is that this replacement is the precondition for both responses. That is a genuine intellectual claim, not just atmospheric pairing.

The piece's weakness is partially structural. Harper's Miscellany essays at this length often suffer from a slight tendency toward atmospheric drift, and the Williams piece's last third — the Spiral Jetty visit, the references to Robert Smithson and "the lines we have made" — performs the literary-essayist move of seeking metaphor over argument at exactly the point where the reader most wants the argumentative gear to engage. The Rainmaker reporting is substantive and original (it's not clear from public materials how many other journalists have been inside their Pendleton operation), and the essay could plausibly have done more with it — the venture-capital incentive structure, the water-rights political economy that makes cloud-seeding a hedge-fund-adjacent business, the federal regulatory ambiguity. Williams gestures at all of these without anchoring them. The piece is reported essay rather than investigative journalism, which is a defensible mode-choice, but the choice has costs.

The piece belongs in the discovery layer for two reasons specific to the roundup's blind spots. First, Harper's is on the publication roster but with the documented ProQuest-indexing lag (typically 1-2 months) that means current-month Harper's pieces are usually unreachable through the regular workflow; the Longreads pointer is the route by which a current-issue Harper's piece can land in a current roundup. Second, the regular roster's coverage of climate, energy, and weather is dominated by Hannah Ritchie's data-driven public-health-and-climate beat and Brian Potter's infrastructure-and-materials beat; neither covers the cultural-political dimensions of weather modification, the conspiracy-theoretic backlash, or the venture-capital cloud-seeding industry. Williams gives the project a foothold on a beat — atmospheric science as cultural object — that no roster writer carries.

Williams is a candidate worth tracking. His prior book on hunting and meat-eating (Springer Mountain) plus this Harper's appearance plus an earlier "Send in the Clouds" piece (referenced in the related-articles section) suggest a writer working consistently in the reported-essay genre on the felt-experience-of-physical-systems beat, which the regular roster lacks entirely.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Full Articles

The Platner episode and the meta-politics of Democratic renewal

Of Course This is a Coordinated Ratfucking of Graham Platner

Freddie deBoer · 2026-06-05 · Original →

… lol

As with so many of the worst things in American politics, we have the Nixon administration to thank for the term ratfucking. It entered the American political lexicon through the Nixon-era operatives who perfected its techniques: dirty tricks, sabotage, deception, and (especially) bad-faith manipulation of the pliable political newsmedia. Ratfucking isn’t just trying to hurt your political opponent’s campaign; that’s electoral politics. Ratfucking is a covert process built on plausible deniability, tactics that no establishment politician or campaign is going to publicly acknowledge as their work. Think of the planted story timed for maximum damage, the dirty trick disguised as spontaneous misfortune. The essence of ratfucking is not that the damaging material is necessarily false. It’s that the sabotage is engineered - sourced, timed, and laundered through credulous media intermediaries to produce an outcome that the saboteurs could never achieve openly. By that definition, the cascade of media revelations now engulfing Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner is as pure and plain a case of ratfucking as I can remember… and almost certainly engineered by the Democratic establishment, which cares more about control of the party than winning the Senate. (The Iron Law of Institutions wins again.)

For the record, I’m not a Maine voter and I’m not especially a Platner fan. He’s not my vision for the future of Democratic party and I haven’t donated money to him or anything. But this process has played out time and again, where the center-right faction of the Democratic party has weaponized liberal media (like, say, The New York Times, The Atlantic, and New York) to do their dirty work, and inevitably against a more populist, left-wing candidate who represents a threat to the Clinton-Obama Democratic party machine. And it sucks.

preorder All In Your Head now

So, let’s start with motive. Graham Platner is not a generic Democrat; he’s part of the ascendant insurgent wing within the party that has begun to represent a real threat to the Nancy Pelosis and Chuck Schumers who have steered the party towards neoliberalism, capitulation to the right, and pro-corporate policies since before I was born. Platner is an oysterman and Marine Corps veteran seeking to unseat five-term incumbent Susan Collins, running on a classic “throw the bums out” anti-billionaire, anti-establishment platform. Crucially, his rise was an act of defiance against the party’s Washington leadership. His platform, heavy on working-class rhetoric, pushed establishment-backed former governor Janet Mills out of the race in April, to the dismay of top Democrats in Washington who felt the popular two-term governor was a safer bet. The establishment’s preference was never a secret: Chuck Schumer abandoned any appearance of neutrality, declaring that Mills “is the best candidate to retire Susan Collins” in what Axios plainly described as a full-fledged fight between the party’s establishment and its left wing. When a faction wants a candidate gone but can’t beat him at the ballot box, the incentive to remove him by other means is obvious. That’s the soil in which ratfucking grows. (That might be a mixed metaphor idk.)

Then let’s examine the sourcing, the single most damning element. The most destructive blows against Platner didn’t come from Susan Collins or the National Republican Senatorial Committee or conservative media. Of course not! Populist left Dems are never killed by the right; they don’t need to be, given how relentlessly their own party establishment works to defeat them. And indeed the really damaging hits against Platner have come from inside his own party. The sexting story, which has detonated mere days before the primary, traces back to his campaign’s own internal files: Platner’s wife, Amy Gertner, disclosed the texts to a campaign staffer last August during an internal opposition research review, in the earliest stages of Platner’s candidacy. This should go without saying: a year-old internal disclosure does not surface on its own; an “interested party” had to have gotten involved, and it almost certainly had to be a Democratic source that had knowledge of the internal oppo research that Platner’s campaign was developing. The New York Times subsequent story about former girlfriends - of course, of course, of course it’s the New York Times, the weapon of the Democratic corporatist establishment since time immemorial - was apparently driven by “rumors” that were floating around. (I wonder who floated them!) Meanwhile, former Maine state Rep. Genevieve McDonald, who previously served as Platner’s political director, resigned from the campaign and has publicly questioned his viability, reportedly describing him as unelectable. What’s in it for her? I don’t know, let’s see where she goes in the next few years! I have a funny feeling she’s going to get a cushy landing in DC.

A campaign’s own oppo research, flagged internally, leaking to the national press thanks to vague and unattributed “rumors” at the decisive moment … these are the textbook signature of sabotage from within. It’s ratfucking, folks.

It gets stranger. One of the women central to the Times story isn’t a random Maine voter. Lyndsey Fifield, who described a roughly two-year on-again, off-again relationship with Platner, has worked for conservative groups and was part of Nikki Haley’s 2024 presidential campaign. When the case against a progressive Democrat is partly built on testimony from a Republican operative, laundered through the paper of record, the line between journalism and opposition research has effectively dissolved. And if you want to say, well, if a conservative operative is involved, isn’t it the Collins campaign behind it?, I would make two points. First, revealing the information now makes little sense when Mills is still on the ballot; if this is about winning the general instead of scuttling the primary for Platner, why do it now? I promise the Collins campaign sees sensible establishment figure Janet Mills as a stronger opponent. Second, even if this info dump was designed to help Collins, that doesn’t exonerate Schumer or the NYT in the least - I promise, both Chuck Schumer and the Dem leadership he represents and the political reporters at the Times would prefer Susan Collins get another term than have Platner secure the seat for the Democrats. Collins, who has proven to be one of the most consequential enablers of the horrors of both George W. Bush and Donald Trump, is a Very Serious Centrist of the kind that both the Democratic leadership and the NYT assume should rule the world.

And we should take care not to give short shrift to the timing, which is everything. Ratfucking lives and dies by the calendar. The pattern here isn’t the random arrival of inconvenient truths but a metronomic sequence engineered for cumulative effect - what worried Democrats themselves call a “drip, drip, drip” dynamic. (Again, quintessential ratfucking.) The inflammatory Reddit posts surfaced just a few days after Gov. Janet Mills announced she was running… precisely when the establishment most wanted to hurt Platner. The texts, known internally since 2025, broke in the final days before the June 9 primary. The oppo sat dormant for the better part of a year, then erupted at the one moment calculated to inflict maximum harm while denying voters time to absorb it. Do you really think that just… happens?

Finally, there’s a quirk of Maine primaries that makes this all particularly gross. Why release devastating material now, when Platner is the overwhelming primary favorite and cannot realistically be stopped at the ballot box? Well, one theory would have to be “because costing Platner the primary was never the actual goal.” Under Maine law, a candidate who wins the June 9 primary and subsequently withdraws by July 13 may be replaced by a nominee selected by (drumroll please) “party officials,” who would then choose a replacement. Think about it, friends! If you’re Chuck Schumer and the NYT and everyone else who will do anything to make sure that the corporatist wing of the Democratic party stays in control, what could be a better outcome than Platner winning the primary but being forced to step down during time the period when the party establishment chooses the nominee? The damaging revelations seem perfectly timed to land when Platner can no longer be defeated by Maine voters but can still be pressured into withdrawing afterward… at which point the party committee, not the electorate, gets to choose. Is that the scheme here? I don’t know, but I wouldn’t put it past anyone involved. I do know that the party sat on its own opposition research for the better part of a year, then released it in the final days before the primary, at the precise moment when Platner can no longer be stopped in the primary but can still be removed afterward. That’s all very convenient.

Bernie Sanders, who endorsed Platner early and has refused to abandon him, named the underlying logic directly: the reason the establishment wants Platner gone is not the tattoo or the texts but that the oyster farmer represents a movement within the party that’s a genuine threat to the billionaires who control our political system. Strip away the moralizing and the cynical corporate HR politics and the sequence is obvious: an inconvenient insurgent, internal material weaponized by his own side, a Republican-linked source, a calendar tuned to a removal statute…. That is ratfucking by any honest definition.

Look, Platner’s campaign confirmed he sent multiple women sexually explicit texts at the beginning of his marriage. You are free to have moral misgivings if you’d like. If you vote on those moral misgivings, rather than on the fact that Platner is the only Democrat who can defeat the 73-year-old Collins and in doing so help stop Trump’s agenda, I think that’s very childish - and if you’re one of the vast majority of centrist Dems who have excused constant accusations of sexual misconduct against Bill Clinton (which are so numerous they have their own Wikipedia page), you’re a raging hypocrite. Sure: voters deserve whatever information there is to give them before they vote, and unflattering oppo dumps before primaries are ordinary, not sinister. But a coordinated effort by all of these establishment media entities, launched with suspiciously-similar language and at this exact moment in the campaign cycle, drawing on information that could only have come from Democratic sources, against a candidate uniquely hated by both the Democratic establishment and the wealthy whose interests they represent… I would like a little transparency at least, please.

I’ve been getting the classic “That’s crazy”/“Everybody knows that” routine on this story - I’ve been told by all manner of savvy people that there’s no coordinated anti-Platner effort, but also that of course there’s a coordinated anti-Platner effort and I’m naive to believe that I could ever live in a world without ratfucking. It would be cool if people picked one or the other, if they’d settle on “that’s not happening” or “of course that’s happening lol” and not give me both at the same time. And it would particularly be cool if the establishment media stopped acting as unpaid political operatives for the center-right Democratic party establishment - but, well, I guess some things are beyond hope.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

Graham Platner Loses Washington’s Vote

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-06-01 · Original →

Gonna lose some subscribers for this one; help keep this newsletter independent by becoming a paid subscriber (seriously lol)

Maine Democrat Graham Platner, who is running against 29-year incumbent Senator Susan Collins, is involved in another “scandal” for sexting women who aren’t his wife.

Washington Monthly editor Bill Scher declared that “Mills should get back in the race,” referring to the 78-year-old governor — favored by party leaders like Chuck Schumer — who suspended her primary campaign in April after getting her clock cleaned by Platner so badly she’s probably still shitting pieces of her dentures out.

The question on Washington’s mind now is: Why can’t Maine just nominate an asexual, Harvard-educated McKinsey consultant as candidate rather than some tatted up, ex-Marine riff-raff like Platner?

The answer is simple: That’s not what Maine voters want.

People are done with the clean-cut types who’ve harbored ambitions for political office since they were on high school student council and have lived every waking moment accordingly. I call them smoothgroins: real-life barbie dolls with smooth plastic where a sexual organ should be. Think of the telegenic types like Gavin Newsom who never have a strand of hair out of place or much of anything to say (certainly not against the establishment). Think of Cory Booker, who at age 56 announced his first marriage, complete with a made-for-media rollout — clearly on the ridiculous assumption that people still give a shit about their presidential candidate having a typical family. Think of Pete Buttigieg, a politician with a public persona so clinical and detached that his own communications director, caught on camera during a raw debate prep session, complained that he was “coming across like the fucking Tin Man up there.”

Susan Collins and Janet Mills would never be embroiled in a sexting scandal (too much integrity?)

These are people who think we’re still living in the I Love Lucy world where married couples retire to separate beds so as not to offend viewers’ delicate sensibilities.

Not only are those days long gone — they never really existed. When longtime Massachusetts congressman and liberal icon Barney Frank died on May 19 at age 86, the beltway obituaries did him one last favor by sticking to his Dodd-Frank regulatory and gay-rights legacy rather than his sex scandal in which a male escort he’d hired ran a prostitution operation out of Frank’s apartment. Outlets like CNN gave him a respectful interview on the future of the Democratic Party.

Just this year, congressman Tony Gonzales of Texas resigned after pressuring a married staffer to have sex with him, leading to her suicide by immolation(!)

Some other examples off the top of my head:

  • Matt Gaetz — resigned from Congress in 2024 under a House Ethics cloud, the panel later concluding there was evidence he’d paid women for sex, including a 17-year-old
  • Eric Swalwell — the California Democrat resigned the same week as Gonzales, facing sexual-misconduct allegations from multiple women
  • John Edwards — the married former presidential contender fathered a child with a campaign videographer, then leaned on an aide to falsify a DNA test, all while his wife was dying of cancer.
  • Bill Clinton — blowjob etc.

And of course there’s personal scandals of a non-sexual nature, such as with Hegseth and Kash Patel, who are drinking themselves to (our) death.

We must not allow Platner to sully the sterling reputation of Congress

In the real world, it seems everyone and anyone can have dark present and past. Take Obama—the squeaky-cleanest of them all, the liberal patron saint of respectability and decorum. Biographer David Garrow’s Rising Star documents that before Obama ever met Michelle, he twice proposed to an earlier girlfriend Sheila Miyoshi Jager — and kept seeing her into the early ‘90s even as his relationship with Michelle deepened.

I don’t care about any of this because I never bought his or any politician’s fairy tale story to begin with. It’s patronizing that grown adults are expected to believe such things. Voters are electing a politician, not a Pope. And they know that.

Platner will survive this — not because voters are thrilled he was sexting other women, but because they already assume half of Congress is doing the same, or worse.

And it’s not as if I’m some Platner booster. He’s always struck me as an impulsive dumbass (not unlike me I suppose, except I would never run for Congress). But I'll take a dumbass over Susan Collins, a three-decade incumbent who has rubber-stamped every war she's ever met and just about every major Washington disaster since I was in elementary school.

Consider how she answered Platner's remark that she'd voted to send him to Iraq. She said “that was Platner’s decision to serve. He was not drafted.”

That's the kind of casual cruelty that falls out of these decorum-humpers the moment an election forces them to face an actual human being. And here's the thing about everyman dumbasses like Platner: they don't talk like that. That line is pure thirty-year operator — the reflex of someone who's spent a career treating war as a line item and the people fighting them as a footnote. These people hold you in contempt and are indecent straight through. They just know which side of the plate the salad fork goes on.

And let’s be real, Platner’s story here is a pretty common one. This and his other “scandals” sound a lot like other former Marines I know. So when Washington acts like it’s disqualifying, what they’re really saying is that ordinary people aren’t fit for higher office.
