Goodbye To All That

Harry Cheadle · 2026-06-05 · Original →

Party for the Creators Project, a partnership with Intel and Vice, London, 2010. (Photo by Dave M. Benett/Getty Images.)

I don’t know if anyone but me noticed, but digital media died last month.

In mid-May, a media mogul named Byron Allen bought a majority stake in BuzzFeed, which has been culturally invisible and financially struggling since shutting down its news division in 2023 and pivoting to AI content. Just weeks later, Vox Media, a collection of brands including New York Magazine, sold its more valuable properties to Lupa Systems CEO James Murdoch, the younger son of Rupert Murdoch. Companies like Vice and Vox were hailed as the future of media in the 2010s, standard-bearers of a new generation of youth-focused, internet-savvy publications that would take over from the New York Times and CNN. At the height of their valuations, around a decade ago, Vice, Vox, and BuzzFeed (the “big three” of the digital media industry) were estimated to be worth a combined $8.4 billion, with Vice accounting for $5.7 billion by itself. Supposedly smart investors were betting on these companies, and venture capital enabled them to build shiny New York headquarters with hundreds of mostly millennial staffers, including me.

Subscribe now

I joined Vice in 2010, just on the cusp of this era. I thought I was going to work at a magazine, and for a while I did—we had fashion shoots, photo spreads, fiction, a comic on the back page. For a while, we gave Bob Odenkirk his own page where he could do anything he liked; once, he wanted to do a Highlights-style “can you find the dildos in this picture?” game, which meant I had to go out and buy a half-dozen fake penises.

As Vice grew—and investors bought in—our focus shifted more toward the internet, and toward social media in particular. Weird magazine shit was out. The game was to capture attention, in the form of clicks and shares and interactions. We were ostensibly competing with BuzzFeed and Vox and other smaller sites like Upworthy, but all of these outlets were fellow-travelers in the same ideological project.

Digital media companies were all built around a single idea, which was that the internet and its attendant platforms—social media sites, Google, YouTube—had created an opportunity to build massive audiences. These companies used technical and editorial strategies that were totally foreign to their print forebears. The Huffington Post pioneered SEO practices, Gawker perfected the format of the can’t-look-away personal essay, Vice was an early adopter of online video, BuzzFeed practically invented a half-dozen viral post formats. All of this was in service of building those precious audience metrics, but the actual customers of these businesses weren’t the readers or viewers but the advertisers who coveted access to that audience; all of the major digital media companies also had teams that produced ads, including ads that looked like editorial content, aka “native advertising” or “branded content.”

There were sometimes controversies over whether some companies were inflating the size of their audiences (short answer: they were), but the underlying assumption that attracting traffic would lead to profits was rarely, if ever, questioned. None of the digital media companies used paywalls in the 2010s. Executives and investors wanted to see those traffic numbers increase, and locking out non-subscribers would mean the numbers would go down—number go up, good; number go down, bad. If these new media brands could attract huge numbers of people, particularly young people, they were bound to become financially successful at some point, right?

I had extremely self-interested reasons to want this to come to pass. I jumped aboard the Vice train early enough that I got stock appreciation rights that would have been worth something had the company been sold or gone public near the peak of its value. Sometimes I would do some back-of-the-envelope math to try to figure out how much I would make in an IPO, but the numbers were too complicated and vague—certainly something in the five figures, which to a journalist in his mid-twenties is kind of a lot.

The early 2010s were a time of techno-optimism more generally. The internet would connect us and lead to the formation of new communities. Social media could fuel and spark pro-democracy movements in autocratic countries. Citizen journalists could livestream newsworthy events in real time. The media world would have fewer gatekeepers. A connected world, it seemed to many, would be more populist, smarter, idealistic, more progressive, just plain nicer. And incidentally, we would all get rich. Sounds like a pretty good deal.

The flood of VC money that accompanied these sunny feelings was certainly real enough (a trickle went into my bank account). The jobs were real, and the equipment, and the stories the journalists produced, and the awards that some of those stories won—I remember the collective feeling of pride digital media journalists had when Official Journalism Bodies began handing out awards to online outlets. But it was all based on sand.

Digital media companies were attracting their audiences in large part through social media apps and search engines—that audience wasn’t “ours” in any meaningful sense. These platforms gradually realized they wanted to keep people on Instagram or Facebook or Google results rather than sending the traffic to publishers. The tech giants began capturing a bigger and bigger slice of online ad revenue.

At the same time, competition for eyeballs was increasing. It soon became clear to everyone that social media networks were not places people would congregate to celebrate their common interests but the most vicious attention markets ever invented. As entrepreneurial actors attempted to game these market dynamics, you saw a blossoming of influencers, streamers, and conspiracy theorists, all competing for eyeballs with some combination of sex appeal, controversy, and sensationalism. Say what you will about Vice or BuzzFeed, we generally tried to tell stories that were true. We never had a chance.

The players of the digital media age are mostly still around, at least in name. Vice was bought in bankruptcy and has resumed publishing and making films. Vox never stopped publishing and will likely continue after its sale. Even The Huffington Post, now owned by BuzzFeed, is still kicking. A group of former Gawker Media employees launched the site Defector as a kind of successor in 2020; more recently, a collection of former Eater staffers launched the new food site Ravenous. But all these outlets have retreated from the old ad-driven model, and are instead asking readers to actually pay for journalism. The dominant idea kicking around the industry is that a small but devoted following of readers who will pay you for your work is far more valuable than a much larger readership that merely clicks on your links. Everyone working in media wants to cultivate an intimate community that will follow them from one platform to another. Increasingly, the things we are creating are being put behind paywalls, in private Discord channels, on subscriber-only podcasts.

Subscribe now

A lot of the individual journalists who got their start during this era are still bouncing around as well. A few of my colleagues and competitors washed up at The New York Times or The Atlantic, old-school publications that weathered the social media age better than the upstarts that were supposed to replace them. Others pivoted toward advertising or “comms.” I remember one editor I worked with at Vice quitting to go work on a flower farm and all of us congratulating him for getting out. I stayed online: I landed a job at Eater after getting laid off from Vice, then got laid off again, then began devoting more time to my Substack (many such cases).

But our collective dream is gone. We were supposed to build… what? I’m still not sure what the end goal of the digital media era was. A more populist vision of journalism, where reporters and editors were accountable to their audiences? A shared cultural and political vocabulary that would cross borders and define a generation? A forum for frustrations with the “establishment,” whatever that might mean? Just a platform for a lot of young media talents?

Whatever we were trying to do, it failed. The money is gone. The jobs are gone. The websites we made are atrophying into junk floating around in cyberspace. I don’t know if anyone will believe me, but man, it was fun while it lasted.

Harry Cheadle is a freelance writer and editor living in Seattle.

A version of this article was originally published in Notebook Dump.

Follow Persuasion on X, Instagram, LinkedIn, and YouTube to keep up with our latest articles, podcasts, and events, as well as updates from excellent writers across our network.

And, to receive pieces like this in your inbox and support our work, subscribe below:

Why we ought to kill the healthy patient

Jeroen Bouterse · 2026-06-05 · Original →

by Jeroen Bouterse

We are fortunate to live in an equitable society, with high levels of trust and excellent administrative transparency. Many old diseases have been eradicated, and most people live healthily to old age. However, our utopia has been suffering for a long time from a peculiar and cruel illness, about which after a century of research the following is now known with moral certainty.

Everyone has a one in five chance of falling victim to this disease. No genetic or lifestyle factors predict it in any way, and it is undetectable until you are exactly thirty years of age. At that point, there are two days during which it is symptomless but detectable with advanced medical equipment, after which it is irreversibly fatal and kills rapidly. There is no cure except one: during these crucial two days, tissue from the vital organs of one healthy peer can save with certainty the lives of five diseased people. However, the required procedure inevitably kills the donor.

A few decades ago, a voluntary association was formed. If you sign up, you commit to reporting to a hospital when you turn thirty, together with thousands of peers who have turned thirty on the same day. You get tested. If you are healthy, there is now a 5% chance that you will be randomly selected as a donor, which means you will die. If you have the disease, you will be saved. For both groups, the procedure is painless. You can decide whether you want to be informed of your predicament at any point, but you cannot opt out after entering the hospital.

This ‘5-to-1’-scheme reduces every participant’s chance of dying at thirty from 20% to 4%. This being an enlightened age, by now almost everyone has realized this is the sensible thing to do. The healthy participants who die as donors are not remembered as murder victims, nor praised as heroes, but mourned as casualties of the disease itself – their deaths regarded as equally indiscriminate and senseless, but fortunately fewer in number than they were in the past.

Systems verifying that everyone has signed their papers have been exceedingly strict from the start, as have the procedures ensuring that donors are randomly selected. In several decades, no mistakes have been made. But you never know! In a perfect storm, doctors might need to act under a bizarre combination of knowns and unknowns. For instance: because of a failure of some computer systems but not others, they might be certain that they are confronted with five diseased people who need to be saved quickly, and one healthy one. At the same time, they might not have timely access to the records confirming that all patients are of sound mind and have consented to the procedure, and that this is what they came to the hospital for, rather than a sleep study.

Given the robustness of our systems, such scenarios are sometimes waved away as outlandish – especially given how few people are outside of the 5-to-1 scheme to begin with. We are reminded of cultural fears in the distant past, about the split-second decisions self-driving cars supposedly needed to make. These seemed relevant at a time when they shared the roads with humans, who were still driving and regularly crashing their vehicles into each other. Even under that curious interplay of responsible agents and erratic forces, high-stakes ethical dilemmas under precisely the right amount of knowledge and ignorance were extremely rare in practice.

However, most people understand that behind such fanciful scenarios there is a question of principle. If you know with certainty that you can save five by killing one, but you don’t know with certainty that the one person has consented or would consent to be used that way, then can you make the decision for them?

There is little controversy around the answer. All ethics boards vote in favor of killing the healthy patient if there is no further information about everybody’s preferences, provided their presence in this situation is believed to be sufficiently arbitrary. (I.e., we cannot have reason to suspect that something about them – their curiosity, their clumsiness, or their connections – made it more likely that they rather than someone else were in this position.)

This consensus does not necessarily reveal its own operative principles, because it is overdetermined: the 5-to-1 scheme clearly and uncontroversially produces the best possible outcomes; under the ‘veil of ignorance’ a rational person would always elect to be part of a 5-to-1 society; and as regarding this matter we actually live with an almost ideal veil of ignorance, we know that random thirty-year olds likely have or would have consented even if we can’t get individual confirmation. Given all this, most people believe that canceling the procedure – allowing four more deaths – would be immoral and rightly lead to outrage.

Some individual ethicists have dissented, grumbling that rather than doing critical moral thinking, the boards are choosing the route of least resistance and sanctioning whatever course of action most resembles broadly accepted practices. These dissenters warn against any relaxation of the rule that requires a signed consent form: else, they say, there is a slippery slope towards killing people who we know have not consented, or who cannot consent.

At this point, hardline consequentialists have injected a thought-experiment into the debate, to illustrate that consent is not necessary to begin with. Imagine a society just like ours, they say, plagued by the same puzzling disease as ours, equally random and merciless, with the same single remedy in which one healthy person takes the place of five diseased ones of the same age – except now the illness manifests not at thirty years old, but at three.

Even in our halfway-utilitarian society, some feel uncomfortable performing cold, rational calculations when it comes to the lives of children. However, most people say that on their behalf they would make the same choice they have in fact made for themselves in their own lives. How could they bear to live in a world where 20% of young lives were cut short rather than 4%, just because those involved could not yet understand what the correct decision was?

Historians add that even in those dark ages when people were squeamish about self-driving cars, parents had their children vaccinated despite small but nonzero risks. In fact, they were often required to do so. Apparently, even then many people realized that low odds of dying young are better than high odds of dying young. Moreover, states ruled that not only the numerically literate should benefit from those better odds.

Then as now, changing the odds from worse to better often meant re-rolling the dice, in the sense that the people who died in the new system were not a subset of the people who would have died in the prior circumstances. We understand that this does not change the moral calculus. Of course, nobody wants to be that unlucky person that dies from the re-roll; but if the outcome of the first roll is unknown, almost everybody wants to get in on it – wants to live, that is, in a society that minimizes the number of lives cut short in a fair way, and maximizes their own chances of survival.

In the past, not everyone was able to shake off confusions around probabilistic reasoning. Idealized thought-experiments did not always clear it up either, especially if they stayed in a medical orbit. Somehow, people remained biased towards the notion that in the ‘default’ case, they wouldn’t die. Feeling vigorous now, they figured they would be that single healthy person in the hospital, not one of the five hanging by a thread in the operation room.

Interestingly, they did understand the principle in a different context: that if six people were distributed unevenly over two tram tracks, you would be more likely to be in the larger than in the smaller group. Indeed, an overwhelming majority figured it would be morally sound, perhaps even obligatory, for an untrained bystander to pull a lever that killed a single person over five. No consent forms involved! No checking computer records to see that mister Other-Track had opted into this classic 5-to-1 scheme. No, just a basic moral principle: fewer premature deaths are preferable over more premature deaths.

Their unwillingness to apply that framework to our situation was due not simply to their general state of moral confusion (evident from their xenophobia, short-termism about climate and resources, lackluster response to crimes committed by their allies, and treatment of animals), but also to the less clear-cut medical situation in their time. Most of all, it flowed from the far lower levels of social trust and organization they had to operate under. Under those circumstances, it was almost impossible to kill a random person to save five in any reasonably certain and procedurally fair way. In fact, it is remarkable they even came up with the idea. Had they tried, distrust would have skyrocketed.

Understandably, it was difficult for them to disentangle the ethical core of the situation from the contingencies of their own social and political context. To them, it just looked like a terrible idea, which it was. They rationalized this intuition in terms they thought were ethical – distinctions between ‘killing’ and ‘letting die’, and supposed rules around means and their relations to ends – but which were either irrelevant or wrongheaded.

In their clear-eyed dealings with the more abstract trolley situation, however, we can see that even they would have been able to understand, with the proper context, that the tendencies in our own society are morally sound. The 5-to-1 scheme has amply proven to be extremely beneficial, and the presumption has rightly become that all else being equal, ‘the’ healthy patient – meaning a fairly chosen arbitrary person – is to be killed. That we don’t make participation obligatory is because this would make an ever-smaller minority extremely unhappy, because their non-participation only harms themselves, and because our high trust levels are partly maintained by liberal laws with a strong aversion to compulsion.

Still, a nudge in the right direction remains warranted. At least, let’s reform the 5-to-1 to include default enrollment.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response · ↩ Back to standalones table

Hard Rain

Wyatt Williams · 2026-06-01 · Original →

By Wyatt Williams | Harper's Magazine, June 2026 issue (Miscellany) Photographs by Davide Monteleone

[Miscellany] Hard Rain The battle over weather modification by Wyatt Williams , All photographs from February 2026 by Davide Monteleone for Harper’s Magazine © Davide Monteleone A radar tower stands alone in northeast Oklahoma City, surrounded by a green field. It is one hundred feet tall and topped with a smooth, white orb. Hidden inside the orb, an antenna rotates while pulsing electromagnetic waves into the distance, waiting for a faint bounce to arrive back in the dish. It is not quite an all-seeing eye. The tower can identify hail, rain drops, a cloud of cicadas, the spiraling debris in tornadic winds from more than one hundred miles away. As the sun set on July 6, 2025, though, there was nothing to detect. It was one of those summer Sunday nights in the middle of the country when you know the heat is coming, but it hasn’t arrived just yet. The moon was waxing gibbous and would be full in four days. The fireworks that had popped over the river on Independence Day were long gone. The city philharmonic had packed up its instruments after an outdoor concert titled “Red, White, & Boom!” Anyone who glanced at the news would’ve seen something about a flash flood in Texas, a riverside summer camp washed away in the night, the number of dead still unconfirmed. At 9:35 pm , a bearded man approached the radar tower and began to climb the chain-link fence around it. As he reached the top bar, the bearded man paused to rock back and forth and lean his head back, yelling into the night sky some unheard complaint. He gazed around and the lens of a security camera caught his face, retinas glowing back in the dark. On the other side of the fence, he got to work. With a hammer, he snapped open the latch guarding the radar’s million-watt power supply. There were control panels, switches, meters. He destroyed them all. Content that the tower had been disabled, he walked around the facility systematically destroying each security camera. It is unclear whether he understood that destroying cameras would not destroy the images they had captured of him. On a bicycle, he disappeared back into the night. By the next afternoon, KWTV News 9 had posted a picture on Facebook of the bearded man from their security footage, asking anyone who recognized him to report him. A call came in a day later. According to the tip, Anthony Tyler Mitchell was in the backyard of a house just a few miles from the radar station. The caller reported that he had cut the internet cable to her home. As two officers took Mitchell into custody, they recovered a pair of wire cutters and a hammer tucked into the waistband of his shorts. The following day, Michael Lewis Arthur Meyer, who is the leader of a militia named Veterans on Patrol, contacted media outlets and the Oklahoma City Police Department to take credit for the incident. He claimed that Mitchell was one of his followers, taking the direct action that Meyer had been threatening for months. The group believes that the latest generation of weather radar towers are “directed energy weapons” that the military have “pointed at the American people.” Meyer claimed that Veterans on Patrol would not stop at disabling one radar tower, that they had plans for taking down at least a dozen more. In his statements to reporters at the time, Meyer insisted that his crusade was a divine one. The weather was being modified, there were powers out there that could control it, and this was against God. “When the military plays God with the weather, they’re mocking our Heavenly Father,” he said.

F or much of the past year, I worked for the Weather Channel. I didn’t do much. It’s a big company; they need a lot of people to look at the weather and write about it. That was the job, more or less. It is easy to look at the weather. You don’t even need to go outside. When the day is still dark, at four or five in the morning, the weather will already be on the wire services. I don’t mean forecasts or temperature readings or even radar maps. In the end, those are just measurements and illustrations that approximate the thing itself. Weather is the rain before it fills the funnel of a pluviometer or the wind before it spins the cups of an anemometer. Weather is something we feel; the numbers just tell us what it might feel like. Conflating the two is an easy mistake. How else to describe three inches of rain at the Hollywood Reservoir or seventy-mile-per-hour winds at Austin-Bergstrom International Airport? Just try to articulate the strange, chaotic beauty brought on by waves of solar radiation sweeping across a continent and summoning air to rise with billions of microscopic droplets suspended within it as the undulating pressure of the atmosphere shapes that mass into a stream floating high above your head. You’ll find that is too much for anyone to think about when we could just say, “Look: a cloud. Sixty-five percent chance of rain,” and mean roughly the same thing. The weather goes on moving without anyone there to see it. Or at least it did. There isn’t much weather that we don’t see anymore. This is what I mean about the wire services. A woman in France steps outside her house to see that the brown water of the river has risen to her front steps. Her black cat sniffs at the flood’s edge. She takes out her phone and documents the scene. Within a few hours, the footage will be on Reuters, available to any news organization that wants to buy the clip and cut it down into a Reel for Instagram. Not that you necessarily need to wait. On a good day, a live feed from the Outer Banks will scan across the coast as a nor’easter blows in eight-foot swells and wet-suited surfers ride the break while you look on from your laptop. On a bad one, the same feed can show you a house built on stilts being pulled by a king tide into the ocean. The bad days are at least as beautiful as the good ones, sometimes more so. In Indonesia, a stringer walks through a village, or at least what was once a village before the mud flowed down from a forest and swept the village along with it. His hand shakes as he carries the camera, the last drops of the storm still falling. First responders are already gathering the bodies. In Alaska, a streamer sits in front of his screens, face glowing with the colors of Fortnite as his house begins to rumble. He looks around, confused first and then scared, gripping onto his desk as the pictures fall from his string-lit walls. In Florida, a meteorologist steps outside her office at dawn to look up as the thin flakes of a light snow begin to fall between the fronds of the palm trees. A drone takes flight during a test deployment in Pendleton, Oregon Low clouds drift along a mountainside in eastern Oregon A drone disperses silver iodide into a cloud A Rainmaker truck drives along a muddy access road Cameron Wessel, Rainmaker’s instrumentation specialist, prepares a weather balloon equipped with instruments that measure temperature, humidity, and wind profiles within storm systems Previous Next The shape of these episodes is always the same. A person encounters a force much larger than themselves, a force that exists on any other day but has now made itself uncommonly present, whether through strength or volume or grace. It isn’t necessary to be a religious person to recognize that this is how we have historically described encounters with God. Believers say they can see God in the golden rays of a sunrise. Skeptics note that there isn’t anything there to believe in, that the physical laws that govern the bending of light go on regardless of faith. Activists might feel the failures of an administration in the temperature. The most literal-minded among us may only claim to see “a nice day.” Our optimisms, our politics, our faiths, our failures, our visions of benevolence or cruelty: they’re all up there floating in the sky. T he first week of this year, I arrived in Pendleton, Oregon, to watch a team of ten men modify the weather. After my plane landed, I was texted a set of coordinates that led me to a dirt road deep in the high plains. At 10 pm , I parked my rental car in the dead grass of a roadside and was driven in the rest of the way. As we passed through the rolling hills, I could see their positions: three teams, each manning one drone, spread across the cattle-fenced distance. When I arrived at camp, the air was damp and near freezing, but the sky was clear and the moon full. Nothing happened quickly. We were waiting for clouds. For the nucleating agent to work correctly, conditions needed to be just right. When silver iodide meets supercooled liquid water suspended in a cloud at temperatures below negative five Celsius, it can create a chain reaction of ice-crystal formation. In essence, it makes the water floating in the atmosphere heavy enough to fall from the sky. It makes rain or snow. We sat in a red Eskimo-brand tent next to an F-150 with a generator running in the bed. There was a small space heater that barely kept the tent above freezing, two folding chairs at a folding table bearing two Starlink-enabled laptops, a gallon jug of water, three six-milligram cans of Wintergreen Zyn. Heavy-duty flight cases for batteries. A black duffel bag of silver-iodide canisters on the ground. Clouds drifted overhead. One of the men opened a hydrogen tank and filled a white balloon until it measured three feet across, attached a small radiosonde underneath, and released it into the sky. Measurements came feeding back into the laptop. Temperatures of negative five degrees Celsius could be found at five thousand feet of elevation, wind speed fifteen knots. It was a little after midnight now and time to launch the drones. The engineers tapped a few figures into their laptops, let autopilot do the rest. The quadcopters took flight in unison, their buzzsaw whirr echoing across the rolling prairie distance, their green and red lights blinking until, after a few moments, they silently disappeared into the gauzy, soft reaches of the clouds. A livestream from each beamed down into the laptops, black squares flickering with the fog of clouds. I stood outside the tent, neck craned. Up there, each drone carried fifty grams of silver iodide, puffing the white dust into clouds in a timed release that lasted for less than thirty minutes, not that I could see it. Soon, I could hear the whirring again as they landed in the field. Their lead engineer said he wasn’t happy about the flight. The clouds had been broken, more gaps between them than he would have liked. The conditions had been good but not perfect. He packed another Zyn. The night stretched on. More cloud cover swept in overhead, this time thinner and higher at twelve thousand feet. The sonde’s temperature reading said that elevation would be negative fifteen, maybe negative twenty Celsius. Silver iodide would be effective at as low as negative thirty-eight, when the supercooled liquid water would turn to ice anyway. But the team was uncertain about how the drone would fly at that temperature. Each of the quadcopters’ carbon-fiber blades had been equipped with a thin adhesive circuit that generated just enough heat to melt ice as it formed on the blade, keeping the drone aloft. The concern was that temperatures below negative twenty Celsius could delaminate the blade’s circuit, causing the whole system to fail and the drone to come crashing down. Just as they were debating this problem, another appeared: there was an error in the code. New software to operate the drones had been released to the team that morning and bugs kept coming up. They called into Rainmaker’s headquarters in El Segundo, California; an engineer FaceTimed to say he was working on it. New lines were written, systems reset. The thin clouds at twelve thousand feet passed. The quadcopters didn’t fly. It was a quiet night, just the sound of wind running through the dead grass. Two am . Two-thirty. More clouds came over. Another sonde sent down new readings: negative seven degrees Celsius, altitude of six thousand feet, wind speed fifteen knots. Conditions in the clouds were perfect. The drones went up again. Their flight path described a set of perfect lines, intentionally unlike any shape that precipitation takes in nature. Around three-thirty in the morning, I could hear it begin to happen. One drop after another, slow and then steady and then hammering down from above. It was finally raining. The field turned to mud around us. The men loaded their drones into their trucks, broke down the tent, packed up the gear. As we drove back into Pendleton, the state highways winding in the direction of the wind, everything around us was soaked. Water sprayed up from the truck tires and pooled in the ditches. The men flashed key cards at a gate and pulled into the hangar. T he earliest stories frame weather as the work of gods. When Poseidon was angry with humans, he sent storms to crush their ships and flood their cities. For Egyptians, crops would not grow without Horus and his benevolent eye shining down between the clouds. So it was with Yahweh or Indra or Tlaloc or any of the others. Whatever good or bad we saw in the sky depended on the generosity, the cruelty, the dispositions and moods of powers much larger than ourselves. We could try to pray or behave in certain ways to influence their winds, but, in the end, the weather wasn’t up to us. God had the final say. The narratives that shaped civilization agree on this part. They harmonize as a single story. Not that we haven’t tried to control it. Weather modification is among the oldest and least successful human projects. For thousands of years, we have sacrificed chil dren, sung songs and danced, brewed alchemical concoctions, chanted prayers, fired cannons, and made many other futile efforts in the attempt to somehow change the weather a little more to our liking . Stubbornly, the weather persisted, seemingly undisturbed by human action, until the twentieth century. The underside mechanisms of a drone release silver iodide into a cloud layer during a flight over Pendleton In recent years, we have become inured to a new single story about the weather: The climate is changing and this time we have done it. We continue to quibble over some details, whether the proof is in the rising tides or the wildfires or the heat index or acidifying oceans or the storms that seem to be getting stronger every year. Or else the causes and the blame, whether it was incidental that the Industrial Revolution happened to inaugurate the greatest year-on-year increase in temperature ever recorded, whether someone should have known that internal combustion would produce gases that would change the atmosphere, whether someone did in fact know that such a thing could happen but innocently ignored it— what about the swamp gases —and whether ignoring it was less a matter of innocence than ambivalence or outright selfish malevolence, a matter of preserving one’s own economic status at the expense of other people’s lives, whether the people who benefited materially from that willful ignorance should be made to pay for the harm that it has caused, will cause. You get the idea. It’s easy to get mired in the finger-pointing, to miss the most apparent fact of it. Climate change is an enormous accomplishment. From an engineering perspective, the ability to coordinate activities to modify the average temperatures of an entire planet, change the pH of deep oceans, and cause vast shifts in the distribution of arable land across continents is a realization of powers as considerable as anything humanity has achieved before. It rewrites the core story of our origins, in which we are powerless before the forces that have made us. The fact that this discovery of our powers—that we can modify the weather on a planetary scale—was more or less accidental only puts it more firmly in the canon of scientific revelations. We are also, as with our other emergent powers, quite apparently terrible at it. The innumerable crises prompted by our moment—the desertification of arable land, increasingly catastrophic storms, the deadly rise in tem peratures in previously habitable regions—are nothing if not urgent incentives to grasp hold of what we have already done. After millennia of reaching up toward the sky, the levers just out of reach, the consensus on climate change indicates that we are, in fact, already manipulating the weather every day. All of which suggests a new question: Are we ever going to get good at it? T he city of El Segundo was incorporated a little over a century ago. The name was chosen not in reference to the Spanish colonists and missions like many cities in California, but instead to a historic chapter of the fossil-fuel industry. The first successful oil refinery on the West Coast had been built in 1876. When Standard Oil (now Chevron) began construction on the state’s second oil refinery, in 1911, the area around it was named simply El Segundo. The Second. The aerospace industry followed not long after. In 1928, a small municipal airport that would eventually become Los Angeles International Airport was built nearby. A factory today known as the Boeing Satellite Development Center first broke ground there in 1946. Lockheed Martin and Northrop Grumman followed. Manufacturing and design boomed until it didn’t. As the industries of the twentieth century were incrementally offshored and in decline, El Segundo found a second beginning. The first office of SpaceX, founded in 2002, was in one of the city’s long blocks of low, industrial buildings. The startups that have since populated the area have made a point of distinguishing themselves from the tech companies operating in Silicon Valley to the north. Instead of focusing on software and apps, the founders of El Segundo are, almost as a rule, focused on “hard tech”: machines, circuits, rockets, engines, technology that is designed to physically do something in the world. They say they are building a new economy of industry, rather than an attention economy. The distinction is not without some condescension. On the afternoon I first arrived at the headquarters of Rainmaker, just a few blocks down the road from SpaceX’s first address, I saw something that resembled a cross between a high-tech manufacturing facility, a research lab, and an elaborate tree-house tech club. Behind a roll-up garage door, there were a half dozen stations covered with a sprawl of circuits and wires, soldering guns and Allen wrenches, carbon-fiber prop blades and stainless-steel hardware, where engineers were carefully assembling quadcopters. A squad of 3D printers chugged along in the corner. Whiteboards tracked each step in the assembly process. In the stairwell, five swords—one Japanese, one Jordanian, and so on—hung on the wall. To the right, a lab was enclosed behind glass for testing an innovation that I was immediately told I couldn’t write about. A refrigerator was stocked with Celsius, two hundred milligrams of caffeine per serving. Cans of Zyn were littered across almost every desk. A massive American flag draped from one wall. Upstairs, a couple dozen desks were occupied by a team who scarcely glanced away from their screens. I sat down and waited for Augustus Doricko. Long before I met him last fall, Doricko had positioned himself as an unofficial mayor of the Gundo, as the locals call it. With a long blond mullet and an aluminum cross worn atop a black sweater, he has thrust himself in front of countless cameras and claimed, among other things, that “Gundo is for hard tech what Florence was for art during the Renaissance.” In the account that Doricko gave me, the pandemic changed his life. He had been a student at UC Berkeley at the time, living what sounds like a fairly typical undergraduate existence. He studied physics, read Richard Dawkins, went to parties, did drugs. He describes that period of his life as cynical and unfulfilled. (“Camus and Sartre couldn’t give me any reason not to live that way.”) But when the pandemic hit and classes went remote, he retreated to his family’s home in Texas. He liked lifting weights, so he picked up a job as a personal trainer. Among his clients was a man who drilled water wells for a living. In between sets, he educated Doricko about the economy of water for farmers. Across the West, a pattern of desertification has set in as populations have risen. Water rights, always gravely important in the West, have only grown more precious as the commodity is exchanged in a complex web of agencies and mar kets. In a drought year, a farmer in Cali fornia’s Central Valley might pay $2,000 for an acre-foot of water on the open market. It’s worth noting that water is the most abundant substance on the surface of the earth. The problem isn’t scarcity; it’s location. As many places have too much as have too little. The weather provides an opportunity for redistribution: as water rises in warm air, it’s carried by wind around the world, falling as rain when the conditions are right. The prayer for it to fall here rather than there is one that farmers know all too well. A chapel near Rainmaker’s testing grounds in Pendleton A General Electric diagram from 1948 illustrating cloud microphysics and precipitation formation A radar dish that collects atmospheric data at Rainmaker’s headquarters in El Segundo, California Previous Next The solutions beyond prayer are much more expensive. A transcontinental pipeline to bring water from an area of abundance to an area of undersupply could cost billions and take decades, even if the contractor is able to navigate the virtually impossible legal tangle of building it. Dams have proven themselves effective at collecting water, but they, too, can cost billions and cause as many legal and environmental problems as they solve. Doricko directed his attention to another question: Could we produce water that wasn’t already there? He looked at atmospheric-water generators, a technology that condenses available humidity from the surrounding air and collects it in tanks. He wasn’t confident it would help. Eventually, he started reading about cloud seeding. “My first reaction was, like, giddy,” he said. It sounded to him like technology pulled from a science-fiction novel. “And my second reaction was desperate depression.” Cloud seeding had been around for eighty years but had never become ubiquitous. What was wrong with it? Why hadn’t it caught on? He started talking to experts in the field, attended the Weather Modification Association Conference, and came to the conclusion that the issue wasn’t if it could work, but a matter of quantifying exactly how well it did. At the same time, Doricko was on a spiritual journey. The relative hollowness of his life in Berkeley had sent him seeking—a rabbi, an imam, et cetera—and finally he chose to be baptized. Doricko possessed the zeal of the convert: he thrived within the framework of faith. He read, among other things, Earthkeeping: Christian Stewardship of Natural Resources, a book edited by Loren Wilkinson, a professor at Regent College, a small theological school in British Columbia. The book lays out a framework for Christian environmentalism that derives from humanity’s designation by God as stewards of the earth. “Let them have dominion over the fish of the sea, and over the fowl of the air, and over the cattle, and over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth,” the passage from Genesis goes. God adds, “Replenish the earth , and su bdue it.” In this mandate, Doricko saw simple guidelines: If humanity has technology capable of benefiting the earth, are we not morally obligated to use it? If cloud seeding could end a drought or make water more abundant for a farmer or help prevent wildfires, isn’t that exactly what God would want? The following events happened with such speed as to seem almost in evitable: Doricko dropped out of Berkeley, became a Thiel Fellow, founded Rainmaker, raised $31 million, and started seeding clouds. Rainmaker is structured around two business opportunities that Doricko identified in the cloud-seeding market. The first has to do with cost. The most high-profile cloud-seeding operations have been enormously expensive, in part due to the planes and the pilots hired to fly them. The second concerns validation. Even though cloud seeding has been proven, studied, and practiced for decades, the chaotic nature of weather introduces doubt: Isn’t it always possible that it might have rained anyway? The industry had lagged, Doricko thought, because it hadn’t addressed these issues. Rainmaker’s solution came in the form of two pieces of hardware. The first was an autopiloted drone capable of flying in extremely adverse high winds and icing conditions. At scale, the drones could operate at a fraction of the cost of planes. The second was a mobile sensor unit designed to, among other things, capture the granular detail of precipitation patterns. He named the sensor units Eden and the drones Elijah. As we spoke, Rainmaker was embarking, alongside the state of Utah, on its highest-profile project yet: cloud seeding in the Bear River Basin, north of Salt Lake City, where drought and water demand have been rapidly shrinking the shoreline of the Great Salt Lake. As the lake bed dries up, dust storms laden with toxic heavy metals have become more common. Adding more water to the supply could be one of cloud seeding’s great accomplishments. When I asked Doricko if cloud seeding could refill the Great Salt Lake, he offered an uncharacteristically measured answer that amounted to: We have to wait and see. Overpromising, he told me, was one of the mistakes that cloud seeders had made before. He is twenty-six-years-old. As he looked at me across the table, squinting against the southern California sun, I could see only a hint that the pressure was beginning to wear on him. In the ninth century bc , Elijah prophesied that God would punish idolatrous worshippers of Baal with years of drought. The brooks dried up. Crops died. Bread became scarce. For a faithful few, there was but one barrel of flour and one jug of olive oil that would not be emptied. In the third year, Elijah reported to King Ahab that a hard rain was coming, though none could be seen on the horizon. From the peak of Mount Carmel, Elijah prayed with his head between his knees. Six times a servant went to look for the rain and six times returned to report clear skies to the praying Elijah. The seventh time that Elijah sent him away, the servant reported, “Behold, there ariseth a little cloud out of the sea, like a man’s hand.” The cloud grew. The rain came. The drought ended. Roughly three thousand years later, in 1946, two General Electric researchers in New York used dry ice to make rain fall from an artificial cloud in their lab without asking God about it first. That same month, a third GE researcher, Bernard Vonnegut, improved their procedure by introducing silver iodide. The innovation depends on a fundamental aspect of clouds: within many, there is already liquid water that, because of its purity, is below freezing but hasn’t yet crystallized into ice. Ice crystals need a nucleus, something to orient the water and form around. Bernard chose silver iodide because its crystal structure resembles that of ice. Augustus Doricko in El Segundo It would have been around this time that Bernard helped his younger brother, Kurt, get a job in GE’s PR department. Fifteen years later, Kurt published the novel Cat’s Cradle, which is more or less about how environmental engineering would ruin the world. The idea that our own technology may be the thing that destroys us is, of course, nothing new. In 1955, the mathematician John von Neumann, then a lead adviser for the U.S. Department of Defense, published an essay titled “Can We Survive Technology?” Von Neumann had played a critical role in the Manhattan Project, solving a number of the complex mathematical problems that rendered the atomic weapon usable. He was well aware of the weapon’s apocalyptic potential. In the essay, he situates this newfound nuclear power in a natural context. “Nature has, of course, been operating nuclear processes all along, well and massively, but her ‘natural’ sites for this industry are entire stars,” he writes. The challenge for nuclear scientists, in his opinion, would be to control that power within a space much, much smaller than a star. He was beginning to shift his focus to the frontier of weather. The second half of his essay is devoted to the question of a “Controlled Climate.” Von Neumann had already done the math on climate change. Our ability to alter the climate wholesale was not lost on him. Von Neumann saw the burgeoning field of cloud seeding as just one example of what he thought would eventually become humanity’s total control of the weather. To change the weather, though, one first needed to know what it would do. Weather systems are driven by physical interactions that can be calculated. With enough data points, equations can, for instance, determine the direction of a storm. During the Second World War, von Neumann had already seen the unprecedented calcula tions that could be accomplished by early computers. Beginning in 1950, he assembled a team in Princeton, New Jersey, to attempt to predict the weather over North America for a twenty-four-hour period using a computer the size of a small library. This first attempt was not particularly successful. (The computer needed more than twenty-four hours to complete the equations.) But it laid the groundwork for the weather models that meteorologists use today. Above all, von Neumann was compelled by the significance of weather-modeling systems for the military. Much like the atomic race of that time, weather modification, he believed, would have grave global consequences depending on which nation first developed such powers: Present awful possibilities of nuclear warfare may give way to others even more awful. After global climate control becomes possible, perhaps all our present involvements will seem simple. We should not deceive ourselves: once such possibilities become actual, they will be exploited. In the years following von Neumann’s death in 1957, much of what he foresaw began to come true. By 1968, the Department of the Interior was annually investing some $5 million in cloud-seeding projects across the country. The Department of Defense tried repeatedly to use silver iodide to divert hurricanes. At the same time, the Air Force was seeding the skies over Vietnam to create rains to flood the supply lines of the Ho Chi Minh Trail. The operation was kept secret until it was eventually confirmed by the leaked Pentagon Papers. Mathematical models of weather prediction, driven by exponential gains in computing power, became the standard tool for meteorologists around the world. Another of von Neumann’s last revelations, that of an impending technological singularity involving a machine that teaches itself, appears to be inching closer to reality as artificial intelligence is built out by an expanding network of data centers. Among the many problems posed by the growth of artificial intelligence is an irony that von Neumann would, perhaps, have enjoyed: the data centers need more water. “For progress,” he wrote, “there is no cure.” I n the months leading up to the attack on the radar tower in Oklahoma, Michael Lewis Arthur Meyer had been telling anyone who would listen that the U.S. military was using a network of radar towers as weapons. In May, CNN reported that the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration had been warned that Meyer’s militia was conducting “penetration drills” to prepare for an attack on the towers. At the time, Meyer was living in a tent encampment in Oklahoma City, posting messages several times per day on Telegram and livestreaming long monologues about weather modification on YouTube. “If you’re just now tuning in, this is Lewis Arthur, inde pendent investigative journalist with VOP News. You can see the white vehicle to the right of that death tower. That tower is a NEXRAD”—Next Generation Weather Radar—“tower, a directed-energy weapon that helps the military manipulate weather systems. . . . We want the weather command center to be shut down. We want every NEXRAD tower across the United States of America to be powered down. We want HAARP”—the High-frequency Active Auroral Research Program—“to be powered down. And we want the United States military, the Air Force, to have authorization to utilize their new F-45s to take out any jet in the sky that is dispersing chemical and biological agents over the American people.” Delusions need more than mere imagination to be sustained. Meyer’s claims are, of course, untrue. But they are spun from an intricate cloth, threads of truth woven in with invention and paranoia. The secretive way in which the military deployed weather modification in Vietnam, the catastrophizing claims advanced by von Neumann and other luminaries in the field, and the facts of climate change, which create heightened and dramatic weather outliers on a near-daily basis, have a way of meshing with the fantasy that our government is much more capable of weather control than it is. Given all this, it is not surprising that someone might construct a scenario in which the disasters we see are not merely chaotic but the work of a malevolent group. On the weekend of July 4, Meyer’s posts to Telegram took on a new urgency. Beginning late on the night of July 3 and into the early hours of July 4, a torrential thunderstorm dumped more than ten inches of rain on Kerr County, Texas. As runoff funneled into the Guadalupe River, water levels rose thirty feet in less than an hour. “MILITARY STRIKES TEXAS!!” wrote Meyer. “Weather Weapons will eventually come to your community.” The flood swept through homes and pastures. Bridges washed away. A whole neighborhood underwater. “More Americans will continue to be poisoned and murdered by the U.S. Military due to their cowardice and ignorance,” he wrote. “These weapons will not prosper against those who know Jesus while you still have air in your lungs.” More than a hundred and sixty people were rescued from the water by helicopters. Boats were sent out to look for people clinging to roofs and trees. At a summer camp for girls, twenty-seven campers were killed. Underneath a picture of a radar tower that he posted online, Meyer wrote: “The children just murdered in Texas may cause unintended consequences for these particular weapons. God is good.” In fact, it was radar towers that were responsible for trying to prevent the disaster. For days prior to the storm, the National Weather Service had been warning of severe flash flooding and notifying the Texas Division of Emergency Management. The warnings escalated on July 3, and the National Weather Service eventually issued twenty-two alerts. Nevertheless, Meyer continued with his messaging: “MILITARY HAS INNOCENT BLOOD ON THEIR HANDS!” “Children are killed! Hundreds are missing! Thousands are displaced!” “Guadalupe River flooding was caused by weather modification weapons!” In gathering ostensible proof for his claims, Meyer posted a picture from a NASA satellite showing a “red sprite,” a rare electrical discharge that appears sometimes in the atmosphere, and claimed it was proof of “directed-energy weapons.” He reposted news reports showing that Rainmaker had been conducting cloud-seeding operations in another part of Texas, evidence that weather modification had been happening in the state. He was not alone. On July 5, Marjorie Taylor Greene, then a representative for Georgia, announced that she was introducing legislation with Tim Burchett, of Tennessee, to ban “the dangerous and deadly practice” of weather modification. For years, she’d been pro moting a rhetoric similar to Meyer’s. Last year, she wrote on X: First, they said we were crazy for saying they are controlling the weather and spraying chemicals in our skies. Now, they are admitting that they are controlling the weather and spraying chemicals in our skies, BUT that it’s not causing any harm. Call me crazy, I don’t care. but I’ll go ahead and say it. Weather modification and geoengineering is deadly and dangerous. Meyer dubbed his plan to take down Doppler radars “Operation Leaning Tower” and called for volunteers: “We can take out 11 to 15 of these directed-energy weapons if there are 10 to 14 men willing to step up and defend our children from this treasonous and murderous Military.” He posted a link to a YouTube video of a man explaining the steps necessary to bring down a 5G cell tower; he demonstrated which box to break open to disable its control panel and shared advice on how to avoid electrocuting yourself. On July 6, Anthony Tyler Mitchell jumped the chain-link fence in Okla homa City. After the attack was made public, Meyer posted: “LONE WOLF STRIKES NEXRAD IN OKC WHILE VOP RUNS DIVER SION OPERATIONS!!” “Can you hear us now, President Trump? STOP MURDERING OUR CHILDREN WITH YOUR MILITARY!” After Mitchell was arrested, Meyer sent out a message to his supporters saying, “Stay the course . . . no matter what. . . . Should Anthony notify the DHS Fusion Center thugs that he saw our pleas for action and he acted, Anthony has not committed a crime and they will release him barring any deceitful ‘plea’ tactic.” (Meyer believes that no one can be charged for destroying a radar tower because “eliminating these weather weapons” is an attempt to save lives and protect people. Mitchell, he felt, should be able to plead self-defense.) I called Meyer one afternoon last December to hear his side of the story. I listened to him for a little over an hour, occasionally getting in a question or two when he would let me. The story he has crafted takes the well-known facts of government-sponsored weather modification and blends them with fantastical ideas—such as that all meteorologists have signed gag orders preventing them from speaking the truth about radar towers—that have no basis in reality. As we spoke, I had little interest in attempting to convince him otherwise, though I did tell him I’d seen no proof of the things he was claiming. I was more interested in trying to understand why he had chosen this fantasy in particular. That week, as we spoke, an atmospheric river had hit Washington State. The flooding had been deadly, but not quite as bad as in Texas. Meyer told me that he had plans to go to Washington and speak with the victims. He said he’d done this before in North Carolina, after Hurricane Helene. Amid these disasters, Meyer told me, he simply walks up to people who have lost everything, their homes, their families, who find themselves standing around outside wondering what is next. He says to them, “God didn’t do this, the military did.” If they listen, he keeps talking. Parker Beard in Pendleton A tripod-mounted antenna that enables Rainmaker engineers to control drones Previous Next It is not so hard to understand, I think, why someone would want to believe what he tells them. The weather is irrational. Looking into the void of floodwater, who wouldn’t want some kind of story to explain it? We are too familiar with the beauty—the hue of golden-red in a morning fog, the droplets that feed our crops and fill our ponds, the breeze that, just for a moment, suspends a hawk motionless in flight as she looks down across a prairie’s grass—to grasp how violence and indiscriminate cruelty could flow from the same source. How could it sweep away a whole family in the night? How could it leave the bloated bodies of children face down in the mud of a riverbank? If you believe in an all-knowing and all-loving God, isn’t it much harder to accept that He might have been responsible for this? Wouldn’t it be easier if there were someone else to blame? Pendleton, where I watched Rainmaker conduct its drone research, is a town on the Umatilla River, founded in 1868 and perhaps best known for the Pendleton Woolen Mills, which has its origins in making Native American trade b lankets, or the Round-Up, a century-old rodeo that attracts fifty thousand cowboys and spectators every year. The faux saloons and aging storefronts can give the impression of a place fading like the old pictures of outlaws on its walls. The state prison accounts for a not insignificant portion of the town’s population. Yet, over the past decade, Pendleton has become a new frontier. In 2014, Pendleton was authorized by the Federal Aviation Administration to create a test range for un manned aerial systems. The range now includes fourteen thousand square miles of airspace; those who operate there are allowed to steer beyond the visual line of sight that drone pilots are otherwise required to adhere to. In other words, Pendleton pilots can fly into the clouds. Dozens of companies have flocked to the area, including defense and surveillance con tractors like Boeing and Cubic Corporation, as well as Amazon, which has tested its home-delivery drones in Pendleton. Rainmaker’s hangar, which is just yards away from the Oregon National Guard’s Chinook heliport, could easily pass for that of a defense contractor. An American flag nearly the entire width of the space hangs on the back wall. Heavy-duty flight cases are stacked and marked. One, I noticed, was set aside for a demo for visitors from Saudi Arabia. The men who work here keep odd hours, crashing on air mattresses after operations that sometimes last well beyond the first light of dawn. Between flights, the same crew repairs and mo difies the drones. When the right weather forecast comes in, they load out for deployment with admirable efficiency. They’re led by a twenty-three-year-old engineer named Parker Beard who, until about a year ago, was a philosophy major at Pep perdine University. Like Doricko, Beard wears his hair in a long mullet. He is whippet-thin, with the build of a mountaineer (he soloed Mount Whitney in California), and goes through about a can of Zyn a day. He describes his experience of college as reading literature during the day and coding at night. He spent two years writing a bot that traded volatile crypto tokens, which was successful for a time until it wiped out Beard’s entire investment portfolio in a matter of hours. He told me that it was a formative experience for him—that in losing what little money he had, he was able to confront the fundamentally extractive nature of trading. Beard has attended church and identified as a Christian his entire life. But when he was eleven or twelve, he began having trouble seeing what was so impressive about God. Certain things that were supposed to define God—that he was omnipresent, that he knew all—didn’t seem that unusual anymore. Wasn’t that just the internet? It was everywhere and it knew everything. How was God any different? Beard told me this anecdote while we were discussing the ways in which AI would be used in Rainmaker’s work: building weather models to better predict the right conditions for clouds, flying the drones to seed them. It was easy to see this stuff as godlike, just like the internet when he was a kid. Beard said the thing that AI could never do is love, to be all-loving as God is, in part because love requires sacrifice. AI could never give up his only son. Maybe, he believed, we were the missing part there. Maybe we could use the technology to be loving, use it to benefit the world, rather than simply extract things from it. Sacrifice, of course, isn’t easy. Beard told me that it had been an extremely hard fall, one of the driest seasons in the West on record. Without the right amount of moisture in the atmosphere, cloud seeding is of little use. His crew had spent too many cold days standing out in the field, toes going numb, waiting for clouds that never came. He’d taken to reading The Campaigns of Napo leon by David G. Chandler and, in the long hours of waiting, recounting the stories of persistence and triumph to the team. He wasn’t sure if it was working. Much of Rainmaker’s core technology is still a work in progress. Elijah, the company’s proprietary drone, has gone through dozens of modifications as they build and break and rebuild the machine to fly in the harsh, cold winds required to seed effectively. And the radar and satellite data to validate the precipitation that cloud se eding can offer have not yet crystallized. Even the operation on the night that I arrived, when it seemed plainly obvious to me that the downpour I observed in the area had been seeded by their drones, had produced only inconclusive results. Of that operation, Kaitlyn Suski, Rainmaker’s head of research, wrote to me, While we did observe a feature on radar we believe was caused by our seeding mission, it’s not possible with our current technology to fully distinguish that feature from the naturally occurring precipitation. In the future we aim to expand our validation techniques to do just that. A few weeks after my trip, Doricko emailed me data from Rainmaker’s operations in Utah’s Bear River Basin. It had been a historically bad year for the Great Salt Lake. Reports were circulating about a snow drought unlike anything the West had seen in decades. But in the region where Rainmaker was operating, the map showed something dramatically different. “This winter, the Bear River Basin has outperformed other Utah ba sins in snowpack accumulation,” Doricko wrote. “The Bear River Basin reached 75% of median snowpack on February 1, compared to a statewide average of 56%. The Bear River Basin also received 110% of median water-year-to-date precipitation compared to 96% statewide.” He wasn’t comfortable taking full credit, he said, but the numbers were promising. “We are perform ing radar and satellite analysis to determine the exact volume of water generated by our cloud seeding operations in the Bear River Basin and look forward to sharing our findings with the public at the end of the season.” Snow falls during a drone deployment in Pendleton N ot long after I went to Pendleton, I drove to the northeast corner of the Great Salt Lake, to the remote edge where Robert Smithson built the Spiral Jetty in 1970. That week, I’d been working remotely from Utah, clocking in at 3 am to help cover a winter storm that had stretched across the country from Texas to Maine. Footage was coming in from Mississippi, where branches of winter trees and power lines were collapsing under thick casings of ice. The National Weather Service was reporting twenty-seven inches of snow in Kittery, Maine, and twenty-three inches at Boston Logan International Airport. Twenty-six states saw more than six inches of snow that week, but almost all of them were in the eastern half of the country, far from the snow drought plaguing the West. The water simply wouldn’t fall where it was needed. When I clocked out eight hours later, I could look out the window of my condo and see the slopes of Deer Valley packed with skiers, white snow spotted with the fluorescent colors of Gore-Tex jackets. There wouldn’t even be enough snow to ski on were it not for the fact that ski resorts have built their own weather-modification systems fueled by the region’s dwindling water supply: snow machines puff out clouds of powder onto the slopes at night. Directly next to the winding slopes, the ground was bare and brown. Driving north along the interstate, the density of suburban homes slowly dissolved into grazing land, herds of black Angus dotted in the dry grass, hayfields with bales stacked as high as barns. I passed a solar farm, panels facing the noonday sun, and when the pavement ended I switched the Jeep into four-wheel drive. I’d read essays by writers making their pilgrimage down this road to see Smithson’s masterpiece, and they warned of thick mud, ice, snow that could cripple a smaller vehicle. The road was bone-dry, dusty. Prior to making the Spiral Jetty, Smithson had seen aerial photographs of earthworks in Peru: long, artful striations across the mountains, unmistakably unnatural, that long outlived the people and societies who built them. Were they meant to be seen by God? Smithson hired trucks to carry loads of basalt rocks to the edge of the lake, where they were dumped one at a time until they completed a path five hundred yards long. He died in a plane crash only three years later, looking down at the earth to scout out a location for another work that could be seen from above. Smithson anticipated that the lake would modify his creation. Wearing down the basalt. Weathering his shape into the earth. In the Eighties, an unprecedented period of precipitation for the region raised the level of the lake, submerg ing the Jetty and hiding it for years. Eventually that trend reversed and the Jetty re emerged; it had changed somewhat differently than Smithson had imagined. As the current drought took hold, the shore of the lake has receded, abandoning the Jetty entirely. On the afternoon I arrived, the edge of the Salt Lake was a white line somewhere off in the distance, almost a mirage. It appeared to belong to a des ert. A few steer browsed a high ridge, looking for grass among the brittle sagebrush. Two jet fighters roared across the sky to Hill Air Force Base. I walked slowly along the curve of the basalt until I reached the spiral’s center. Smithson’s work is quite obviously about time. The long drive out. The slow process of balancing as you step from one rock to the next. It elongates the moment, leaving you with plenty of space to consider the things we are do ing to the earth. Of all of the lines we have made, I don’t think Smithson’s will be of much note. With the spiral divorced from the water that once made it grand and colorful, the Spiral Jetty now looks insignificant, as if a single turn of the wind might cover it with sand. There were thin clouds in the blue sky that day. Somewhere off in the distance, there were drones in a warehouse waiting for the right conditions. Somewhere up in the atmosphere, water was floating along. We might think of cloud seeding as a kind of accelerator: were we to ever master it, it would hasten the changes of weather that we wait or pray for. Droughts would still come, but we could end them sooner. We could tell the rain to fall in the places where we need it. When the land around you is dry, it’s easy to believe it will go on that way forever. Stories about doom are easy to write. They seem inevitable. That’s a problem of storytelling, though, not of the world. The real world is much weirder than the narratives we try to fit it into. The endings and beginnings we shape are only for ourselves; they shave off the strange chaos to make it a little more understandable, to give it a little meaning. We should know by now that the conclusions we are headed toward haven’t been written yet, that they require a new kind of imagination. They ask us to see something in the sky we don’t yet know how to describe. Two days later, it began to snow.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response · ↩ Back to standalones table

Index by Author

Author Title Publication Summary
Aurelien Nothing In Common... Aurelien Aurelien argues forty years of neoliberalism have stripped Western societies of the shared inheritances that let Britain endure 1939-45, leaving governments unable to summon collective sacrifice for the crises ahead.
Frannie Block As Ezra Jin's Case Gains Attention, His Family Worries China's Retaliating Free Press Block reports that three weeks after Trump asked Xi to free imprisoned Chinese pastor Ezra Jin, Jin's lawyer was denied access at the detention center, raising family fears that Beijing is retaliating against the international attention.
Jeroen Bouterse Why we ought to kill the healthy patient discovery Bouterse stages a near-future utopia whose consequentialist logic and "veil of ignorance" reasoning produce, with majoritarian consent, exactly the violation Judith Jarvis Thomson's transplant case was designed to make unthinkable — to show how procedural fairness can launder a moral atrocity.
Matt Bruenig For the Middle Class, US Labor Taxes Are Similar to Nordic Labor Taxes People's Policy Project (Bruenig) Bruenig shows that once you add employer-sponsored-insurance premiums to the US tax wedge, average-wage American workers pay as much or more in effective labor taxes than Nordic workers.
Ines Burrell Russia’s Oil Is Getting Hammered Persuasion Burrell argues Ukraine's spring 2026 strikes are not opportunistic damage to Russian oil revenues but a deliberate three-pronged campaign — exports, budget revenue, and future production — designed to permanently shrink Russian oil capacity in a way Western sanctions never could.
Oren Cass Tariffs on Forced Labor Are Free-Market Commonplace Cass argues the USTR's June 2 Section 301 forced-labor tariffs on 60 countries are not pretextual but the recovery of an 1890-1930 American principle that capital should compete on worker productivity, not exploitability.
CC Translations: On the Coal Mine Explosion in Liushenyu, Shanxi, That Killed 82 Miners and Injured 128 China Digital Times China Digital Times curates Chinese-press and WeChat investigations of the May 22 Shanxi mine disaster (82 dead, 128 injured) that show the death toll jumped from 8 to 82 within hours of a central Beijing directive demanding "timely and accurate" disclosure, and that 123 of the 247 underground workers had been unregistered to skirt safety regulations.
Harry Cheadle Goodbye To All That Persuasion Cheadle (former Vice staffer) declares the digital-media era over with Byron Allen's BuzzFeed acquisition and James Murdoch's purchase of Vox's prime brands, and reads the whole 2010s VC-financed traffic-chasing experiment as a 15-year sand-castle built on social-platform audiences the publishers never owned.
American Compass Reclaiming American Citizenship Commonplace American Compass argues the country's malaise is the loss of "thick" reciprocal citizenship — replaced by consumer-subject identity — and proposes policy across education, immigration, industrial, housing, and tech-regulation domains organized around restoring citizenship as the load-bearing concept.
Gregory Conti What Pope Leo Should Have Said About AI Compact Conti argues that Pope Leo XIV's encyclical Magnifica Humanitas on AI fails by accepting the inevitabilist frame — treating generative AI as a normal technology requiring regulation rather than a categorical break (humans lose their monopoly on language) that warrants prohibition.
Tyler Cowen Law professors prefer AI over peer answers Marginal Revolution Cowen flags a blinded-evaluation study showing law professors prefer LLM answers to fellow law professors' answers 75% of the time, and bluntly tells academics their human capital has been devalued — being prolific is no longer a comparative advantage.
Tyler Cowen Consent-based laws and aggregate fertility Marginal Revolution Cowen surfaces a Journal of Health Economics paper finding European tacit-consent sexual-assault laws reduced fertility by 4%, driven by risk-averse withdrawal from dating markets — with the implicit gesture (single "tekl" reaction) that the finding is significant and underweighted in policy discourse.
Tyler Cowen Should you move to Argentina? (from my email) Marginal Revolution Cowen-curated guest letter from an Argentine-American in Buenos Aires argues Peter Thiel is right to be in Argentina but wrong about why — the libertarian-revolution narrative is exaggerated, and the country's real asset is demographics, raw materials, and geographic isolation, not Milei's policy program.
Tyler Cowen Should we recriminalize marijuana? Marginal Revolution Cowen argues against marijuana recriminalization not because the harms of legalization are illusory but because paternalist enforcement requires state capacity, information control, and credibility the modern US government no longer has.
Freddie deBoer Of Course This is a Coordinated Ratfucking of Graham Platner deBoer deBoer argues the cascade of NYT/Atlantic/NY Mag revelations about Maine Senate insurgent Graham Platner is engineered Democratic-establishment sabotage timed for a post-primary replacement maneuver, not organic journalism.
Freddie deBoer LLMs Were Mostly (But Not Entirely) Useless at Extra-Textual Tasks Involved in the Composition of My Next Novel deBoer deBoer reports LLMs were mostly useless for meta-textual novel-composition tasks (chapter summaries, word counts, character-presence balancing) but identifies "social deference" — model accepting user errors — as a more consequential failure than hallucination.
Freddie deBoer You Can and Should Blame Young People When They Act Like Lazy Cheaters, Actually deBoer deBoer argues educator refusal to blame students for LLM cheating is a status performance — adults purchasing youth-adjacency at student expense — and that blame is itself a form of moral respect.
Ross Douthat Graham Platner and the Amoral Center New York Times Douthat argues that in a society fragmented into incompatible moral paradigms (evangelical, tech-right, MAHA on the right; Black church, EA, Piker-socialist on the left), the upright moralist becomes more threatening than the sleaze — because sincerity in someone else's moral framework imposes a code alien to yours — which explains both Trump's appeal to non-evangelical voters and the swing-voter case for Platner over the religious-progressive Talarico.
Ross Douthat When Is It Wrong to Use A.I.? New York Times Douthat argues that A.I. critics — including Pope Leo XIV in his recent encyclical — should stop issuing generalized laments and instead name specific uses (student cheating, ghostwriting by chatbot, AI-girlfriend chatbots) as discrete sins, on the grounds that disciplined moral condemnation now lays the institutional foundation for post-disruption regulation later.
Ross Douthat Anna Paulina Luna Wants Everything Disclosed: interesting times New York Times Douthat interviews Rep. Anna Paulina Luna (R-FL) as a representative figure of disclosure-populist conservatism — convinced the federal government is hiding important things (Epstein files, JFK records, UAPs) — and uses the interview to argue that institutional suspicion has become the unifying populist energy crossing left and right.
Euro|topics (curated debate) UN Security Council: Germany left empty-handed Euro|topics Euro|topics aggregates European-press readings of Germany's failed UN Security Council bid as a Global-South verdict on Berlin's restrained criticism of Israel's Gaza conduct, with German papers split between contrition and dismissal of the UN itself.
Euro|topics (curated debate) Zelensky proposes direct peace talks to Putin Euro|topics Euro|topics aggregates Ukrainian and Russian-exile commentators reading Zelensky's open-letter peace-talks proposal as a deliberate rhetorical trap addressed to Western and Russian elites — not to Putin — paired with a drone strike on St. Petersburg as the operative "cognitive attack."
Audrey Fahlberg EXCLUSIVE: Justice Department Closes In on Illegal Alien for Smuggling Unaccompanied Minors Free Press Fahlberg reports DOJ is preparing charges against a Guatemalan national who allegedly filed at least eight fraudulent sponsorship applications to take custody of unaccompanied migrant children — six successful — using the relaxed Biden-era ORR vetting regime.
Thomas Fazi Hungary's New Power Elite Compact Fazi argues that Peter Magyar's new Hungarian government, far from a liberal-democratic restoration, is a coordinated capture of the state by US LNG interests, Atlanticist foreign-policy networks, and Western banking capital — visible in the careers of three of his ministers.
Francis Fukuyama Why We Need Judicial Independence Persuasion Fukuyama argues that judicial independence — traced from the Investiture Controversy through the Glorious Revolution — is the institutional precondition for Western economic dominance, and Trump's rejection of it puts him in the company of Caesaropapist autocrats.
