Suzy Hansen Made in the USA New York Review of Books (discovery via Longreads) Hansen reads Pete Hegseth not as a Trump-era anomaly but as the load-bearing expression of a continuous American imperial culture that the Democratic establishment refused to renounce after the Iraq War and has therefore left intact for the current Iran war.
Josh Kaplan How Antisemitism United America's Political Extremes Free Press Kaplan argues right-wing and left-wing antisemitism have converged into a single conspiratorial frame about "Zionist" control, exemplified this week by Megyn Kelly endorsing a "pedophiles supporting Israeli lobby" dog whistle.
Scott Barry Kaufman Anti-Woke, or Just Wounded? A Typology of Two Types of Anti-Woke Intellectuals Skeptic (discovery via 3 Quarks Daily) Kaufman argues anti-woke intellectuals split into two psychologically distinct types — idea-critics versus wounded crusaders — and that grandiose/vulnerable narcissism is what reliably converts the latter into a permanent grievance machine.
Kara Kennedy Two Drinks with . . . the Mom Who Prodded Trump About Childcare Free Press Saujani argues universal childcare is the rare bipartisan-popular policy both parties keep failing on — Republicans because of culture-war framing, Democrats because they avoid the language of "mother" — and the Mamdani-Hochul NYC pilot shows the political dividend of just running on it.
Ezra Klein There's Something Else We Should Be Worrying About New York Times Klein argues the A.I. policy conversation has been all defense — preventing harms — and proposes a positive public-goods agenda: a public-option frontier model, compute access for universities, Warp Speed-style outcome guarantees, and government data sets that could let A.I. attack rare diseases, materials science, and citizen-facing bureaucracy.
Ezra Klein Ian Bremmer on the Risks America Poses to the World: The Ezra Klein Show New York Times Klein and Bremmer argue the United States — not China — is the principal source of geopolitical uncertainty, and that Trumpism is best understood not via a Thucydides trap but a "Gracchi trap": an internal grievance-driven political revolution that weakens institutional norms and makes American allies see the U.S. as undependable.
Ezra Klein The New Right's Very Old Vision of Men: The Ezra Klein Show New York Times Klein and Helen Lewis (Atlantic) argue New Right "masculinism" — Bronze Age Pervert pagan-vitalist + Christian Reconstructionist branches, Yenor's "family wage" project, Helen Andrews's "demographic feminization" thesis — has become the unifying ideology of a MAGA coalition otherwise splintering over Iran/protectionism, and that its central rhetorical move (women's empathy is destroying institutions) has no internal stopping mechanism, which is why the Nick Fuentes "women in gulags" comment exposed the no-line problem.
Ken Klippenstein Graham Platner Loses Washington’s Vote Klippenstein Klippenstein argues Washington's outrage at Maine Senate candidate Graham Platner's sexting scandal reflects a class-coded preference for sterile "smoothgroin" politicians over actual humans — and that voters already assume half of Congress is worse.
Ken Klippenstein Let Them Drive Less Klippenstein Klippenstein reports oil-and-gas executives are building Marie-Antoinette-style corporate militias in anticipation of public backlash to Iran-war gas-price gouging — a sector-specific extension of the post-UnitedHealthcare-CEO executive-protection boom.
Damon Linker Welcome to the Postmodern Presidency Persuasion Linker argues that Trump's second-term governance — incoherent rationales, war as media spectacle, truth as a tool for manipulation — vindicates Allan Bloom's warning that the "Nietzscheanization of the left" by postmodernism would end with raw political will dominating everything.
Andrew C. McCarthy How the Trump DOJ Extracted a Guilty Plea from John Bolton discovery McCarthy concedes the Bolton prosecution is part of Trump's lawfare campaign against political enemies AND that Bolton's classified-document conduct was genuine misconduct — and argues both can be true at once.
Jude Blanchette and Richard McGregor After the Invasion: China Considers the Problem of Ruling Taiwan discovery-wotr Blanchette and McGregor argue, drawing on a leaked 2024 Xiamen think-tank paper and a decade of Chinese-language scholarship, that Beijing's strategic shift from "peaceful unification" to "complete national unity" has surfaced an unresolved problem its own analysts now openly worry about — how to govern 23 million people in a high-income liberal democracy after a hypothetical takeover — and that this should refocus US/allied Taiwan policy toward reinforcing the institutional features that make occupation costly, not only deterring the invasion itself.
Eli McKown-Dawson Why can’t California count? Nate Silver Silver Bulletin shows California's slow vote-counting isn't a function of size or mail-voting (Florida counts faster with mail; UK and India count faster at scale) — it's an administration choice that ranks the state 41st on the MIT Elections Performance Index and feeds voter-fraud conspiracy theories.
Robinson Meyer Exclusive: Americans Now Overwhelmingly Oppose New Data Centers Near Them discovery-heatmap A new Heatmap/Embold poll finds 71% of Americans now oppose a data center near their home — a 49-point swing in nine months — with rural and young voters most hostile and 20+ projects already canceled in Q1 over $41B in planned investment.
Peter Mitchell Restoring Honor Culture in the U.S. Military discovery Mitchell uses Alasdair MacIntyre's After Virtue to argue that US service-academy honor codes have been hollowed out by due-process legalism and DEI-era enforcement constraints, and that restoration requires reviving the public, shame-based "drumming-out" rituals that VMI and West Point abandoned.
Noahpinion How much more software do we really need? Noahpinion Smith argues coding-agent "tokenmaxxing" isn't producing shipped products because consumer software is a mature industry; AI's real value will come via robotics and AI-first business models, not Better-Facebook.
Mike Pence Mike Pence: Conservatives Have Forgotten What They Believe Free Press Pence argues Trump's second term has abandoned Goldwater-Reagan conservatism (tariffs as the largest peacetime tax hike, isolationism on Ukraine, marginalizing pro-life cause), and conservatives need a new manifesto to recover the principles.
Brian Potter How Long Does It Take to Plan a Bridge? Construction Physics (Potter) Potter compiles planning and construction timelines for 67 US bridges and finds modern planning times (~10 yrs) aren't unusually long historically, but construction times more than doubled from the 1940s-50s to the 1980s-90s.
Juan David Rojas My Time Inside the Immigration Industrial Complex Compact Rojas, drawing on his two years as a Miami-Dade case manager for Unaccompanied Alien Children, argues that the Biden-era asylum surge was largely driven by economic migration and employer demand, that progressives have refused to reckon with this, and that the Trump backlash — though cruel and counterproductive — fills a vacuum Democrats left open.
Matthew Schmitz Heteropessimist Horror Compact Schmitz argues that a new wave of Gen Z horror films and TV (Obsession, Euphoria S3, The Testaments, Something Very Bad Is Going to Happen) constitutes a "heteropessimist horror" genre whose despair about heterosexual relationships maps a deregulated dating market in which old courtship forms have collapsed and no replacement has emerged.
Adam Serwer The Supreme Court Has Invented a Right to Discriminate discovery Serwer argues the Roberts Court's Alabama ruling — paired with Louisiana v. Callais — has constructively rewritten the Civil War amendments to permit intentional racial discrimination in voting as long as a partisan pretext is offered.
Geoff Shullenberger Mario Vargas Llosa and the Possibility of a Populist Novel Compact Substack Shullenberger reads Vargas Llosa's final novel as a (partial) populist novel that asks whether the literary form can survive in an age where popular-music kitsch ("huachafería") rather than high modernism articulates the political nation.
Nate Silver Should Democrats panic about Platner? Nate Silver Silver argues that despite Platner's polling resilience to Maine scandals (Nazi-tattoo, sexts, Reddit posts), Maine polls have systematically overrated Democrats by ~4 points since 2014 and Collins has a documented history of beating her polls — so Mills would still be the lower-variance bet in a blue year.
Bright Simons The death of KOKO Networks: a post-mortem of a biofuel startup and the rise of carbon economies in Africa discovery-african-args The collapse of KOKO Networks — a Kenyan clean-cookstove/bioethanol-ATM startup denied a carbon-credit letter of authorization after allegedly inflating its non-renewable-biomass baseline from 38% to 98% — anchors a long essay arguing that African carbon markets reproduce the colonial-era "redemptive commerce" template, with Kenya's host communities receiving roughly 1% of credit revenue while developers harvest the rest via Mauritius treaty havens.
Gail Slater The Feds Don't Have an Antitrust Monopoly Commonplace Slater argues that when federal antitrust enforcement retreats (as on LiveNation and NexStar), state AGs become not a fallback but the system's primary enforcers, recovering antitrust's Granger-era state-based origins.
Noah Smith I'm kind of over the whole "Anti-monopoly" movement Noahpinion Smith renounces the anti-monopoly movement — not the underlying concern about corporate power — as a monomaniacal, anti-empirical, faction-building intellectual pseudo-cult that picks low-margin targets and anathematizes critics.
Noah Smith Why Europe should put up trade barriers against Chinese goods Noahpinion Smith argues Europe should erect tariffs and non-tariff barriers on Chinese high-tech goods because dependence on Chinese drone-component supply chains would be militarily catastrophic if Russia invades.
Noah Smith The way we treat pigs is a sin Noahpinion Smith condemns the Save Our Bacon Act — buried inside the 2026 Farm Bill — as moral degeneration that would nullify California's Prop 12 and entrench gestation-crate torture nationwide.
Joseph C. Sternberg China Gets Anxious Enough to Choose Worker Freedom WSJ Sternberg argues Beijing's loosening of the hukou residence-registration system is best read not as the consumption-unlocking reform Western economists hope for, but as a defensive political move by an authoritarian regime worried about a slow-growth economy producing too many disappointed urban migrants and unemployed young people.
Matt Stoller A Billionaire Explains Why American Business Now Feels like the Mafia BIG Stoller reads John Malone's 2024 autobiography as the rosetta stone for how a single generation of cable-and-media barons translated Chicago School antitrust into mob-style corporate coercion, paving the way for today's tech oligarchs.
Matt Stoller Monopoly Round-Up: After SpaceX Goes Public, Does the Stock Market Finally Fall? BIG Stoller argues the SpaceX IPO is engineered market-rigging (NASDAQ rule changes, retail-investor dumping) and that the AI-revenue boom (Anthropic to $45B run-rate) reflects market-power lock-in via token billing, not genuine end-user value.
SW Sensitive Words: Search Censorship, Tiananmen, “8 Squared,” and “64 + Division” China Digital Times China Digital Times catalogs the Tiananmen-related search terms blocked across Chinese platforms for the 37th anniversary, frames this year's escalation (cemetery ban for the Tiananmen Mothers, new term "64+division" as flagged) against the recent leak of General Xu Qinxian's 1990 secret trial, and uses the censorship list itself as evidence that even arithmetic ("8 squared," "535+64") is now a censored evasion class.
Alex Tabarrok CA Logic Marginal Revolution Tabarrok deploys the California-17-year-old-can-drive-but-not-ride-Uber inconsistency as a one-line indictment of regulatory paternalism that protects the wrong thing.
Wessie du Toit Inside Britain’s broken war machine discovery Du Toit argues, on reporting from inside British defense startups and the MoD, that the UK's procurement system — top-heavy "prime" contractors plus a risk-zero, scrupulously-fair tender process — has become structurally incapable of fielding modern military capability and is hemorrhaging promising startups to the US.
Adam Tooze Chartbook 451 What times are these? Crying fire in a Berlin lecture theatre. Chartbook (Tooze) Tooze argues that EU-progressive policy-restoration framings (municipal socialism, price stabilization, financial regulation) are anachronistic in the face of AI-driven US capital accumulation that may be ending the economy as a labor-mediated social form within a decade.
Mitra Vand The Islamic Republic Is More Dug in Than Ever Persuasion Vand argues the joint US-Israeli strikes did not weaken the Islamic Republic but consolidated it — empowering the IRGC, drawing erstwhile dissidents into patriotic defense, and giving the regime the information vacuum it needed to narrate the war on its own terms.
Fabio Vighi The Coming Age of Digital Warfare Compact Vighi argues that programmable digital dollars — stablecoins tied to US Treasuries and freezable by OFAC — mark money's terminal abstraction from labor into a system of pure administrative control, with the Iran sanctions as the first live-fire demonstration.
Wyatt Williams Hard Rain Harper's Magazine (discovery via Longreads) Williams uses a militia attack on a National Weather Service radar tower as the entry point for a reported essay on the Rainmaker cloud-seeding industry, arguing that the conspiracy-theoretic backlash against weather modification is downstream of a longer civilizational confusion between weather-as-felt and weather-as-measured that the seeding industry is now intensifying.
Matthew Yglesias Seventeen thoughts on Graham Platner Slow Boring Yglesias argues Platner's personal-scandal vulnerabilities (sexting, tattoo) are survivable but his bigger problem is running a factional campaign in a state he only needs to unite partisan Democrats to win.
Matthew Yglesias James Talarico has an obligation to win Slow Boring Yglesias warns James Talarico must actually win Texas — not just over-perform Beto/Allred-style — by breaking taboos to peel off Cornyn voters, against an opponent (Paxton) who's a weak candidate but genuinely impressive at climbing while criming.
Matthew Yglesias The triumph of capital Slow Boring Yglesias argues asset-price returns have wildly outpaced wages since 2012, meaning the best route to affluence is inheriting capital — even though new fortunes (Musk, Bezos, Zuckerberg) are mostly self-made.
Matthew Yglesias Can AI Cure Cancer? Slow Boring Piper and Demsas debate whether AI will cure cancer — Piper argues LLMs are bad at unverifiable tasks like cancer biology so progress will stay incremental; Demsas expects step-change from AlphaFold-style tools and AI-augmented human researchers.
Matthew Yglesias Elizabeth Warren’s “new“ child care push doesn’t make sense Slow Boring Yglesias argues Warren's Child Care for Every Community Act is a poorly-designed, interest-group-driven program for a niche electoral concern, and Democrats should pursue an expanded CTC, regulatory fixes, and au-pair-friendly immigration instead.
Matthew Yglesias The origins of horseshoe politics Slow Boring Yglesias argues horseshoe politics is mostly a media phenomenon (temperamental + negative-polarization + zero-sum-ideology convergences) and that political capital is a finite budget, not a flywheel.
Matthew Yglesias Learning from Poland’s economic success story Slow Boring Yglesias argues Poland's FDI-driven catch-up growth shows that "European" features like a high minimum wage and universal health care don't kill prosperity — but Europe's labor-market rigidity does kill tech start-ups.

↩ Back to TOC
